Method: Propose CausalTimePrior to generate synthetic TSCMs with paired data; features include configurable causal graphs, nonlinear autoregressive dynamics, regime-switching, and multiple intervention types (hard, soft, time-varying). Use these to generate datasets for PFN pretraining and evaluation of causal effect estimation in-context.

Result: PFNs trained on data from CausalTimePrior demonstrate capability to perform in-context causal effect estimation on held-out TSCMs, showing promise for foundation models in time-series causal inference.

Conclusion: The framework enables principled generation of synthetic interventional time-series data, addressing lack of interventional benchmarks and enabling development of time-series causal foundation models.

Abstract: Prior-data fitted networks (PFNs) have emerged as powerful foundation models for tabular causal inference, yet their extension to time series remains limited by the absence of synthetic data generators that provide interventional targets. Existing time series benchmarks generate observational data with ground-truth causal graphs but lack the interventional data required for training causal foundation models. To address this, we propose \textbf{CausalTimePrior}, a principled framework for generating synthetic temporal structural causal models (TSCMs) with paired observational and interventional time series. Our prior supports configurable causal graph structures, nonlinear autoregressive mechanisms, regime-switching dynamics, and multiple intervention types (hard, soft, time-varying). We demonstrate that PFNs trained on CausalTimePrior can perform in-context causal effect estimation on held-out TSCMs, establishing a pathway toward foundation models for time series causal inference.

</details>


### [130] [Fingerprinting Concepts in Data Streams with Supervised and Unsupervised Meta-Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11094)
*Ben Halstead,Yun Sing Koh,Patricia Riddle,Mykola Pechenizkiy,Albert Bifet,Russel Pears*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FiCSUM provides a general fingerprint-based framework for representing concept behavior in data streams via a large set of meta-information features with dynamic weighting, improving drift detection accuracy and drift modeling across 11 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Concept drift in data streams undermines learning performance; existing representations use few meta-information features and may fail to distinguish concepts, especially with recurring drifts.

Method: FiCSUM constructs a fingerprint (vector) of many distinct meta-information features to represent a concept's supervised and unsupervised behaviors, with a dynamic weighting strategy that adapts which features describe drift for a given dataset.

Result: FiCSUM outperforms state-of-the-art methods on 11 real-world and synthetic datasets in both classification/accuracy and in modeling underlying concept drift.

Conclusion: A versatile fingerprint-based approach with adaptive feature weighting that enhances concept drift detection and modeling across diverse datasets, enabling more robust adaptation to evolving data streams.

Abstract: Streaming sources of data are becoming more common as the ability to collect data in real-time grows. A major concern in dealing with data streams is concept drift, a change in the distribution of data over time, for example, due to changes in environmental conditions. Representing concepts (stationary periods featuring similar behaviour) is a key idea in adapting to concept drift. By testing the similarity of a concept representation to a window of observations, we can detect concept drift to a new or previously seen recurring concept. Concept representations are constructed using meta-information features, values describing aspects of concept behaviour. We find that previously proposed concept representations rely on small numbers of meta-information features. These representations often cannot distinguish concepts, leaving systems vulnerable to concept drift. We propose FiCSUM, a general framework to represent both supervised and unsupervised behaviours of a concept in a fingerprint, a vector of many distinct meta-information features able to uniquely identify more concepts. Our dynamic weighting strategy learns which meta-information features describe concept drift in a given dataset, allowing a diverse set of meta-information features to be used at once. FiCSUM outperforms state-of-the-art methods over a range of 11 real world and synthetic datasets in both accuracy and modeling underlying concept drift.

</details>


### [131] [Graph Tokenization for Bridging Graphs and Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11099)
*Zeyuan Guo,Enmao Diao,Cheng Yang,Chuan Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A graph tokenization framework combines reversible graph serialization with BPE to produce graph-adjacent sequences, enabling Transformers to operate on graphs with minimal architectural changes; achieves SOTA on 14 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large pretrained Transformers rely on tokenization; extending to graphs is hard; need a tokenizer that preserves graph structure and leverages LLM ecosystems.

Method: Graphs are serialized reversibly; global statistics of substructures guide serialization so frequent substructures appear often, enabling BPE to merge into meaningful tokens; sequences are then fed to Transformers (e.g., BERT) without changing architecture.

Result: State-of-the-art on 14 graph benchmarks; often outperforms GNNs and graph transformers; demonstrates bridging graphs and sequence models; code released.

Conclusion: Tokenization bridging approach enables direct application of sequence models to graph data and expands the ecosystem of tools for graphs.

Abstract: The success of large pretrained Transformers is closely tied to tokenizers, which convert raw input into discrete symbols. Extending these models to graph-structured data remains a significant challenge. In this work, we introduce a graph tokenization framework that generates sequential representations of graphs by combining reversible graph serialization, which preserves graph information, with Byte Pair Encoding (BPE), a widely adopted tokenizer in large language models (LLMs). To better capture structural information, the graph serialization process is guided by global statistics of graph substructures, ensuring that frequently occurring substructures appear more often in the sequence and can be merged by BPE into meaningful tokens. Empirical results demonstrate that the proposed tokenizer enables Transformers such as BERT to be directly applied to graph benchmarks without architectural modifications. The proposed approach achieves state-of-the-art results on 14 benchmark datasets and frequently outperforms both graph neural networks and specialized graph transformers. This work bridges the gap between graph-structured data and the ecosystem of sequence models. Our code is available at \href{https://github.com/BUPT-GAMMA/Graph-Tokenization-for-Bridging-Graphs-and-Transformers}{\color{blue}here}.

</details>


### [132] [Task-Conditioned Routing Signatures in Sparse Mixture-of-Experts Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11114)
*Mynampati Sri Ranganadha Avinash*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Routing signatures reveal task-conditioned structure in sparse MoE routing; deep layers amplify task signals; high within-task similarity; strong task-classification from routing alone; MOE-XRAY toolkit released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand whether MoE routing does more than mere capacity balancing and whether routing patterns encode task information that can be used for interpretability and debugging.

Method: Define per-prompt routing signatures as layer-wise, per-expert activation vectors; compute similarity within vs across task categories; quantify with mean similarities and Cohen's d; train logistic regression on routing signatures for four-way task classification with cross-validation; introduce permutation and load-balancing baselines; analyze layer-wise signal strength and low-dimensional projections; test on OLMoE-1B-7B-0125-Instruct; release MOE-XRAY.

Result: Within-category routing similarity: 0.8435 ± 0.0879; across-category: 0.6225 ± 0.1687; Cohen's d = 1.44; classifier accuracy: 92.5% ± 6.1% on four-way task classification; baselines show results are not due to sparsity or balancing; deeper layers exhibit stronger task structure; MOE-XRAY toolkit released.

Conclusion: Routing in sparse MoE transformers is a task-sensitive component of conditional computation, not merely a balancing mechanism.

Abstract: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures enable efficient scaling of large language models through conditional computation, yet the routing mechanisms responsible for expert selection remain poorly understood. In this work, we introduce routing signatures, a vector representation summarizing expert activation patterns across layers for a given prompt, and use them to study whether MoE routing exhibits task-conditioned structure. Using OLMoE-1B-7B-0125-Instruct as an empirical testbed, we show that prompts from the same task category induce highly similar routing signatures, while prompts from different categories exhibit substantially lower similarity. Within-category routing similarity (0.8435 +/- 0.0879) significantly exceeds across-category similarity (0.6225 +/- 0.1687), corresponding to Cohen's d = 1.44. A logistic regression classifier trained solely on routing signatures achieves 92.5% +/- 6.1% cross-validated accuracy on four-way task classification. To ensure statistical validity, we introduce permutation and load-balancing baselines and show that the observed separation is not explained by sparsity or balancing constraints alone. We further analyze layer-wise signal strength and low-dimensional projections of routing signatures, finding that task structure becomes increasingly apparent in deeper layers. These results suggest that routing in sparse transformers is not merely a balancing mechanism, but a measurable task-sensitive component of conditional computation. We release MOE-XRAY, a lightweight toolkit for routing telemetry and analysis.

</details>


### [133] [Learning Tree-Based Models with Gradient Descent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11117)
*Sascha Marton*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gradient-based learning of axis-aligned, hard decision trees using straight-through backpropagation on a dense tree representation, enabling joint optimization of all parameters and seamless integration with gradient-based ML methods, achieving state-of-the-art results across tabular, multimodal, and RL domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the non-differentiable, combinatorial nature and locally greedy optimization of traditional decision tree methods (e.g., CART), enabling end-to-end gradient-based training and better integration with modern ML pipelines.

Method: Backpropagation with a straight-through estimator applied to a dense, axis-aligned decision tree representation to enable joint optimization of all tree parameters and produce hard, interpretable trees.

Result: Reported state-of-the-art performance across multiple domains, including interpretable small-tabular settings, complex tabular data, multimodal learning, and interpretable reinforcement learning, without information loss.

Conclusion: Bridging decision trees with gradient-based optimization enhances performance and applicability, allowing seamless integration into ML workflows and overcoming limitations of greedy, non-differentiable tree construction.

Abstract: Tree-based models are widely recognized for their interpretability and have proven effective in various application domains, particularly in high-stakes domains. However, learning decision trees (DTs) poses a significant challenge due to their combinatorial complexity and discrete, non-differentiable nature. As a result, traditional methods such as CART, which rely on greedy search procedures, remain the most widely used approaches. These methods make locally optimal decisions at each node, constraining the search space and often leading to suboptimal tree structures. Additionally, their demand for custom training methods precludes a seamless integration into modern machine learning (ML) approaches.
  In this thesis, we propose a novel method for learning hard, axis-aligned DTs through gradient descent. Our approach utilizes backpropagation with a straight-through operator on a dense DT representation, enabling the joint optimization of all tree parameters, thereby addressing the two primary limitations of traditional DT algorithms. First, gradient-based training is not constrained by the sequential selection of locally optimal splits but, instead, jointly optimizes all tree parameters. Second, by leveraging gradient descent for optimization, our approach seamlessly integrates into existing ML approaches e.g., for multimodal and reinforcement learning tasks, which inherently rely on gradient descent.
  These advancements allow us to achieve state-of-the-art results across multiple domains, including interpretable DTs rees for small tabular datasets, advanced models for complex tabular data, multimodal learning, and interpretable reinforcement learning without information loss. By bridging the gap between DTs and gradient-based optimization, our method significantly enhances the performance and applicability of tree-based models across various ML domains.

</details>


### [134] [A Learning-Based Superposition Operator for Non-Renewal Arrival Processes in Queueing Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11118)
*Eliran Sherzer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Data-driven superposition operator for merging arrival processes; uses a DL model trained on MAPs to map input moments and autocorrelations to aggregate moments (up to five) and short-range dependence; enables scalable, decomposition-based queueing analysis and outperforms renewal-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Analytical intractability of superposition for general non-renewal streams; need to preserve higher-order variability and dependence; existing renewal surrogates or Markovian representations are limited.

Method: Train a deep learning model on synthetically generated MAPs where exact superposition is known; input: low-order moments and autocorrelation descriptors of multiple streams; output: moments and dependence of merged process; learns compact representation; evaluated on diverse regimes.

Result: Uniformly low prediction errors across variability and correlation regimes; substantial improvement over renewal-based approximations; integrates with modules for departures and steady-state to enable decomposition-based evaluation.

Conclusion: Provides a scalable alternative to traditional analytical approaches, preserving higher-order variability and dependence for distributional performance analysis in feed-forward networks with merging flows.

Abstract: The superposition of arrival processes is a fundamental yet analytically intractable operation in queueing networks when inputs are general non-renewal streams. Classical methods either reduce merged flows to renewal surrogates, rely on computationally prohibitive Markovian representations, or focus solely on mean-value performance measures.
  We propose a scalable data-driven superposition operator that maps low-order moments and autocorrelation descriptors of multiple arrival streams to those of their merged process. The operator is a deep learning model trained on synthetically generated Markovian Arrival Processes (MAPs), for which exact superposition is available, and learns a compact representation that accurately reconstructs the first five moments and short-range dependence structure of the aggregate stream. Extensive computational experiments demonstrate uniformly low prediction errors across heterogeneous variability and correlation regimes, substantially outperforming classical renewal-based approximations.
  When integrated with learning-based modules for departure-process and steady-state analysis, the proposed operator enables decomposition-based evaluation of feed-forward queueing networks with merging flows. The framework provides a scalable alternative to traditional analytical approaches while preserving higher-order variability and dependence information required for accurate distributional performance analysis.

</details>


### [135] [Group Resonance Network: Learnable Prototypes and Multi-Subject Resonance for EEG Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11119)
*Renwei Meng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A method called Group Resonance Network (GRN) integrates individual EEG dynamics with group resonance modeling to improve cross-subject EEG-based emotion recognition, achieving state-of-the-art results on SEED and DEAP with ablations validating the contributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-subject variability in EEG-based emotion recognition limits generalization; existing methods emphasize subject-invariant features but miss stimulus-locked shared group dynamics that could improve performance.

Method: GRN comprises: (1) an individual encoder for band-wise EEG features, (2) a set of learnable group prototypes for prototype-induced resonance, (3) a multi-subject resonance branch encoding PLV/coherence-based synchrony with a small reference set, and (4) a resonance-aware fusion module that combines individual and group-level representations for final classification.

Result: On SEED and DEAP under subject-dependent and leave-one-subject-out protocols, GRN consistently outperforms competitive baselines. Ablation studies show prototype learning and multi-subject resonance modeling provide complementary benefits.

Conclusion: GRN effectively blends individual EEG dynamics with group-level resonance to enhance cross-subject emotion recognition; prototype learning and multi-subject resonance modeling are key contributing components.

Abstract: Electroencephalography(EEG)-basedemotionrecognitionre- mains challenging in cross-subject settings due to severe inter-subject variability. Existing methods mainly learn subject-invariant features, but often under-exploit stimulus-locked group regularities shared across sub- jects. To address this issue, we propose the Group Resonance Network (GRN), which integrates individual EEG dynamics with offline group resonance modeling. GRN contains three components: an individual en- coder for band-wise EEG features, a set of learnable group prototypes for prototype-induced resonance, and a multi-subject resonance branch that encodes PLV/coherence-based synchrony with a small reference set. A resonance-aware fusion module combines individual and group-level rep- resentations for final classification. Experiments on SEED and DEAP under both subject-dependent and leave-one-subject-out protocols show that GRN consistently outperforms competitive baselines, while abla- tion studies confirm the complementary benefits of prototype learning and multi-subject resonance modeling.

</details>


### [136] [High-resolution weather-guided surrogate modeling for data-efficient cross-location building energy prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11121)
*Piragash Manmatharasan,Girma Bitsuamlak,Katarina Grolinger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A weekly, weather-informed surrogate model generalizes across locations by leveraging shared short-term weather-energy patterns, reducing the need for multi-site simulations while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physics-based building energy simulations (e.g., EnergyPlus) are accurate but computationally expensive. Many surrogate models are location-specific and fail to generalize across sites or climates. Exploiting cross-location, short-term patterns can enable scalable, reusable surrogates.

Method: Introduce a high-resolution (weekly) weather-informed surrogate modeling approach that captures recurring short-term weather-energy demand patterns shared across regions, enabling training from a single location and generalization to other sites without extensive multi-site simulations.

Result: When trained on a single location, the model maintains high predictive accuracy for other sites within the same climate zone with no noticeable performance loss, and shows only minimal degradation when applied across different climate zones.

Conclusion: Climate-informed generalization enables scalable, reusable surrogate models for building energy design, reducing the need for extensive multi-site simulations and supporting sustainable design practices.

Abstract: Building design optimization often depends on physics-based simulation tools such as EnergyPlus, which, although accurate, are computationally expensive and slow. Surrogate models provide a faster alternative, yet most are location-specific, and even weather-informed variants require simulations from many sites to generalize to unseen locations. This limitation arises because existing methods do not fully exploit the short-term weather-driven energy patterns shared across regions, restricting their scalability and reusability. This study introduces a high-resolution (weekly) weather-informed surrogate modeling approach that enhances model reusability across locations. By capturing recurring short-term weather-energy demand patterns common to multiple regions, the proposed method produces a generalized surrogate that performs well beyond the training location. Unlike previous weather-informed approaches, it does not require extensive simulations from multiple sites to achieve strong generalization. Experimental results show that when trained on a single location, the model maintains high predictive accuracy for other sites within the same climate zone, with no noticeable performance loss, and exhibits only minimal degradation when applied across different climate zones. These findings demonstrate the potential of climate-informed generalization for developing scalable and reusable surrogate models, supporting more sustainable and optimized building design practices.

</details>


### [137] [Beyond Barren Plateaus: A Scalable Quantum Convolutional Architecture for High-Fidelity Image Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11131)
*Radhakrishnan Delhibabu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable quantum convolutional neural network (QCNN) framework using localized cost functions and tensor-network initialization to mitigate barren plateaus, achieving 98.7% MNIST accuracy and log(N) parameter efficiency, bridging theory and practice in quantum computer vision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Barren plateaus and poor empirical performance hinder QCNNs; there is a need for hardware-efficient, scalable QCNN architectures to make quantum machine learning viable for vision tasks.

Method: Introduce localized cost functions and a hardware-efficient tensor-network initialization strategy within a QCNN architecture; scale evaluation on MNIST; compare against baseline QCNN; analyze gradient behavior and parameter efficiency.

Result: Achieves 98.7% MNIST accuracy (vs 52.32% baseline); demonstrates O(log N) fewer trainable parameters to reach >95% convergence; provides evidence that gradient vanishing is mitigated and QCNNs can outperform classical baselines in this setting.

Conclusion: Presents a scalable, practical QCNN framework for computer vision tasks that overcomes barren plateaus, suggesting QCNNs can be competitive with classical models on vision benchmarks when equipped with localized costs and tensor-network-structured initialization.

Abstract: While Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNNs) offer a theoretical paradigm for quantum machine learning, their practical implementation is severely bottlenecked by barren plateaus -- the exponential vanishing of gradients -- and poor empirical accuracy compared to classical counterparts. In this work, we propose a novel QCNN architecture utilizing localized cost functions and a hardware-efficient tensor-network initialization strategy to provably mitigate barren plateaus. We evaluate our scalable QCNN on the MNIST dataset, demonstrating a significant performance leap. By resolving the gradient vanishing issue, our optimized QCNN achieves a classification accuracy of 98.7\%, a substantial improvement over the baseline QCNN accuracy of 52.32\% found in unmitigated models. Furthermore, we provide empirical evidence of a parameter-efficiency advantage, requiring $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ fewer trainable parameters than equivalent classical CNNs to achieve $>95\%$ convergence. This work bridges the gap between theoretical quantum utility and practical application, providing a scalable framework for quantum computer vision tasks without succumbing to loss landscape concentration.

</details>


### [138] [Higher-Order Modular Attention: Fusing Pairwise and Triadic Interactions for Protein Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11133)
*Shirin Amiraslani,Xin Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Higher-Order Modular Attention (HOMA) that combines pairwise attention with explicit triadic interactions, using block-structured windowed triadic attention for scalability; shows consistent gains on three protein-related benchmarks over standard self-attention and efficient baselines; suggests triadic terms enhance expressiveness at a controllable cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protein sequence-to-phenotype mapping often involves cooperative dependencies among three or more residues that standard pairwise attention may miss; capturing higher-order interactions with scalable attention is needed for accurate predictions.

Method: Proposes HOMA, a unified attention operator that fuses pairwise attention with a triadic interaction pathway. To scale to long sequences, it employs block-structured, windowed triadic attention. The method is evaluated on three TAPE benchmarks (Secondary Structure, Fluorescence, Stability).

Result: HOMA yields consistent improvements across all three tasks compared with standard self-attention and efficient variants (block-wise attention, Linformer). The gains indicate that explicit triadic terms provide complementary representation for protein sequence prediction with controllable additional computation.

Conclusion: Explicit incorporation of triadic interactions via HOMA augments the representational capacity for protein sequence modeling without prohibitive cost, making higher-order dependencies practically exploitable in sequence-to-phenotype prediction.

Abstract: Transformer self-attention computes pairwise token interactions, yet protein sequence to phenotype relationships often involve cooperative dependencies among three or more residues that dot product attention does not capture explicitly. We introduce Higher-Order Modular Attention, HOMA, a unified attention operator that fuses pairwise attention with an explicit triadic interaction pathway. To make triadic attention practical on long sequences, HOMA employs block-structured, windowed triadic attention. We evaluate on three TAPE benchmarks for Secondary Structure, Fluorescence, and Stability. Our attention mechanism yields consistent improvements across all tasks compared with standard self-attention and efficient variants including block-wise attention and Linformer. These results suggest that explicit triadic terms provide complementary representational capacity for protein sequence prediction at controllable additional computational cost.

</details>


### [139] [Scaling Reasoning Efficiently via Relaxed On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11137)
*Jongwoo Ko,Sara Abdali,Young Jin Kim,Tianyi Chen,Pashmina Cameron*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: REOPOLD Relaxed On-Policy Distillation stabilizes on-policy distillation by treating teacher-student log-likelihood ratio as token rewards; introduces mixture-based reward clipping, entropy-based token-level dynamic sampling, and an exploration-to-refinement training strategy; achieving 6.7–12x sample efficiency and inference speedups, with a 7B student matching a 32B teacher in visual reasoning (~3.32x speedup).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-policy distillation aims to transfer reasoning to capacity-constrained models but suffers from instability and negative transfer when imposing strict imitation constraints; robust, reward-guided relaxation is needed to enable efficient, scalable transfer.

Method: Interpret on-policy distillation as policy optimization; REOPOLD relaxes imitation constraints and leverages rewards from the teacher through mixture-based reward clipping, entropy-based token-level dynamic sampling, and a unified exploration-to-refinement training strategy.

Result: Empirically outperforms baselines on math, visual, and agentic tool-use tasks; achieves 6.7–12x greater sample efficiency and enables a 7B student to match a 32B teacher in visual reasoning with ~3.32x inference speedup.

Conclusion: Relaxed, reward-guided on-policy distillation improves stability, sample efficiency, and scalability, enabling smaller models to approach larger teachers’ capabilities with faster inference.

Abstract: On-policy distillation is pivotal for transferring reasoning capabilities to capacity-constrained models, yet remains prone to instability and negative transfer. We show that on-policy distillation can be interpreted, both theoretically and empirically, as a form of policy optimization, where the teacher-student log-likelihood ratio acts as a token reward. From this insight, we introduce REOPOLD (Relaxed On-Policy Distillation) a framework that stabilizes optimization by relaxing the strict imitation constraints of standard on-policy distillation. Specifically, REOPOLD temperately and selectively leverages rewards from the teacher through mixture-based reward clipping, entropy-based token-level dynamic sampling, and a unified exploration-to-refinement training strategy. Empirically, REOPOLD surpasses its baselines with superior sample efficiency during training and enhanced test-time scaling at inference, across mathematical, visual, and agentic tool-use reasoning tasks. Specifically, REOPOLD outperforms recent RL approaches achieving 6.7~12x greater sample efficiency and enables a 7B student to match a 32B teacher in visual reasoning with a ~3.32x inference speedup.

</details>


### [140] [H2LooP Spark Preview: Continual Pretraining of Large Language Models for Low-Level Embedded Systems Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11139)
*Amit Singh,Vedant Nipane,Pulkit Agrawal,Jatin Kishnani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Continual pretraining of an open 7B LLM (OLMo-3-7B-a) for embedded systems using BF16 LoRA on 8 H100 GPUs, leveraging SpecMap-derived data to achieve large perplexity reductions and competitive code-generation accuracy across 13 embedded domains, outperforming some frontier models on select tasks; model checkpoint released open-source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Specialized embedded domains (hardware registers, vendor SDKs, RTOS, HALs) are underrepresented in standard pretraining data, limiting LLM performance in embedded code generation. A domain-focused continual pretraining pipeline aims to adapt an open-weight model to this niche efficiently.

Method: Continual pretraining with high-rank LoRA (rank 512) on OLMo-3-7B-a using BF16 LoRA on 8 NVIDIA H100 GPUs. Training corpus comprises 23.5B tokens across 13 embedded domains, derived from a 100B-token repository-datasheet corpus (117 manufacturers) via a hierarchical datasheet-to-code mapping (SpecMap).

Result: In-domain perplexity reduced by 70.4%; held-out repository perplexity reduced by 66.1%. On 13-domain generative code-completion benchmarks, the 7B model surpasses Claude Opus 4.6 and Qwen3-Coder-30B in token accuracy on 8 categories. The approach demonstrates that targeted continual pretraining enables smaller open-weight models to rival frontier systems in specialized tasks.

Conclusion: High-rank, continual pretraining with domain-aligned data and LoRA adapters enables effective adaptation of open-weight models to embedded systems programming; results support broader use for targeted domain specialization, with the training checkpoint released as open-source on Huggingface.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong code generation abilities in general-purpose programming languages but remain limited in specialized domains such as low-level embedded systems programming. This domain involves hardware register manipulation, vendor-specific SDKs, real-time operating system APIs, and hardware abstraction layers that are underrepresented in standard pretraining corpora. We introduce H2LooP Spark Preview, a continual pretraining (CPT) pipeline that adapts the OLMo-3-7B-a fully open language model to the embedded systems domain using BF16 LoRA with rank-stabilized scaling on 8 NVIDIA H100 GPUs. Our training corpus is constructed from repository-datasheet pairs covering 100B tokens of raw embedded systems data across 117 manufacturers, processed using the hierarchical datasheet-to-code mapping approach proposed in SpecMap (Nipane et al., 2026). The resulting curated dataset split contains 23.5B tokens across 13 embedded domains. Continual pretraining with high-rank LoRA (r=512) yields substantial gains, reducing in-domain perplexity by 70.4% and held-out repository perplexity by 66.1%. On generative code completion benchmarks spanning 13 embedded domains, our 7B model outperforms Claude Opus 4.6 and Qwen3-Coder-30B on 8 categories in token accuracy, showing that targeted continual pretraining enables smaller open-weight models to rival frontier systems on specialized technical tasks. We release the production training checkpoint on Huggingface as an open-source artifact.

</details>


### [141] [Procedural Fairness via Group Counterfactual Explanation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11140)
*Gideon Popoola,John Sheppard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GCIG introduces Group Counterfactual Integrated Gradients, an in-processing regularization that enforces explanation invariance across protected groups conditioned on the true label, aiming to improve procedural fairness without sacrificing predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Procedural fairness (stability of model explanations across protected groups) is underexplored compared to outcome-based fairness; lack of explanation consistency can erode trust and transparency.

Method: GCIG computes explanations with respect to multiple Group Conditional baselines for each input and penalizes cross-group variation in attributions during training, yielding an in-processing regularization that enforces explanation invariance conditioned on the true label.

Result: GCIG substantially reduces cross-group explanation disparity while maintaining competitive predictive performance and favorable accuracy–fairness trade-offs compared to six state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Aligning model reasoning across groups via stable group-conditioned explanations is a principled and practical avenue to advance fairness beyond solely constraining predictions.

Abstract: Fairness in machine learning research has largely focused on outcome-oriented fairness criteria such as Equalized Odds, while comparatively less attention has been given to procedural-oriented fairness, which addresses how a model arrives at its predictions. Neglecting procedural fairness means it is possible for a model to generate different explanations for different protected groups, thereby eroding trust. In this work, we introduce Group Counterfactual Integrated Gradients (GCIG), an in-processing regularization framework that enforces explanation invariance across groups, conditioned on the true label. For each input, GCIG computes explanations relative to multiple Group Conditional baselines and penalizes cross-group variation in these attributions during training. GCIG formalizes procedural fairness as Group Counterfactual explanation stability and complements existing fairness objectives that constrain predictions alone. We compared GCIG empirically against six state-of-the-art methods, and the results show that GCIG substantially reduces cross-group explanation disparity while maintaining competitive predictive performance and accuracy-fairness trade-offs. Our results also show that aligning model reasoning across groups offers a principled and practical avenue for advancing fairness beyond outcome parity.

</details>


### [142] [Attention Gathers, MLPs Compose: A Causal Analysis of an Action-Outcome Circuit in VideoViT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11142)
*Sai V R Chereddy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Video ViT classifiers encode hidden 'success vs failure' semantics via a cascade in internal circuitry; attention heads gather evidence while MLP blocks compose concepts, yielding a robust, distributed representation of outcomes; indicates hidden knowledge beyond explicit task and motivates mechanistic oversight for trustworthy AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how video models trained for simple classification develop latent, semantically meaningful representations of outcomes, enabling better Trustworthy AI through mechanistic interpretability.

Method: Mechanistic interpretability approach using activation patching and ablation analysis on a pre-trained video Vision Transformer; layer-wise circuit decoding from early layers (0) to deeper layers (11); causal analysis to map roles of Attention Heads and MLP Blocks in the 'success vs failure' signal.

Result: Reveals an amplification cascade: outcome-relevant information is progressively amplified from layers 5 through 11; causal analysis shows a division of labor with Attention Heads as evidence gatherers and MLP Blocks as robust concept composers driving the success signal; circuit is distributed and redundant, explaining resilience to ablations; suggests existence of hidden knowledge beyond the explicit classification task.

Conclusion: Mechanistic oversight is necessary to build genuinely Explainable and Trustworthy AI; models can develop sophisticated internal representations for complex outcomes even when trained on simple tasks, underscoring the importance of auditing internal circuits.

Abstract: The paper explores how video models trained for classification tasks represent nuanced, hidden semantic information that may not affect the final outcome, a key challenge for Trustworthy AI models. Through Explainable and Interpretable AI methods, specifically mechanistic interpretability techniques, the internal circuit responsible for representing the action's outcome is reverse-engineered in a pre-trained video vision transformer, revealing that the "Success vs Failure" signal is computed through a distinct amplification cascade. While there are low-level differences observed from layer 0, the abstract and semantic representation of the outcome is progressively amplified from layers 5 through 11. Causal analysis, primarily using activation patching supported by ablation results, reveals a clear division of labor: Attention Heads act as "evidence gatherers", providing necessary low-level information for partial signal recovery, while MLP Blocks function as robust "concept composers", each of which is the primary driver to generate the "success" signal. This distributed and redundant circuit in the model's internals explains its resilience to simple ablations, demonstrating a core computational pattern for processing human-action outcomes. Crucially, the existence of this sophisticated circuit for representing complex outcomes, even within a model trained only for simple classification, highlights the potential for models to develop forms of 'hidden knowledge' beyond their explicit task, underscoring the need for mechanistic oversight for building genuinely Explainable and Trustworthy AI systems intended for deployment.

</details>


### [143] [Systematic Scaling Analysis of Jailbreak Attacks in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11149)
*Xiangwen Wang,Ananth Balashankar,Varun Chandrasekaran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A compute-based scaling-law framework for LLM jailbreaks, comparing four attack paradigms across model families; prompting-based methods are more compute-efficient; vulnerability varies by harm type, with misinformation easier to elicit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of systematic understanding of how jailbreak success scales with attacker effort across methods, model families, and harm types; establish a scaling-law framework to quantify attacker progress.

Method: Treat each attack as a compute-bounded optimization evaluated on a shared FLOPs axis; evaluate four paradigms (optimization-based, self-refinement prompting, sampling-based selection, genetic optimization) across multiple model families and scales; fit a saturating exponential to FLOPs–success curves to derive efficiency summaries; perform same-state comparisons to interpret prompt-space optimization.

Result: Prompting-based approaches tend to be more compute-efficient than optimization-based methods; prompt-based updates can be cast into an optimization view and outperform in prompt space; attacks occupy distinct regions of success vs. stealthiness; vulnerability is strongly goal-dependent, with misinformation harms typically easier to elicit.

Conclusion: The scaling behavior of jailbreaks is contingent on attacker goals, with implications for defense: defenses should account for different attack budgets and the ease of prompt-space optimization, especially for misinformation-related harms; a unified framework facilitates cross-method comparisons across model families and scales.

Abstract: Large language models remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks, yet we still lack a systematic understanding of how jailbreak success scales with attacker effort across methods, model families, and harm types. We initiate a scaling-law framework for jailbreaks by treating each attack as a compute-bounded optimization procedure and measuring progress on a shared FLOPs axis. Our systematic evaluation spans four representative jailbreak paradigms, covering optimization-based attacks, self-refinement prompting, sampling-based selection, and genetic optimization, across multiple model families and scales on a diverse set of harmful goals. We investigate scaling laws that relate attacker budget to attack success score by fitting a simple saturating exponential function to FLOPs--success trajectories, and we derive comparable efficiency summaries from the fitted curves. Empirically, prompting-based paradigms tend to be the most compute-efficient compared to optimization-based methods. To explain this gap, we cast prompt-based updates into an optimization view and show via a same-state comparison that prompt-based attacks more effectively optimize in prompt space. We also show that attacks occupy distinct success--stealthiness operating points with prompting-based methods occupying the high-success, high-stealth region. Finally, we find that vulnerability is strongly goal-dependent: harms involving misinformation are typically easier to elicit than other non-misinformation harms.

</details>


### [144] [Algorithmic Capture, Computational Complexity, and Inductive Bias of Infinite Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11161)
*Orit Davidovich,Zohar Ringel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Infinite-width transformers exhibit a bias toward low-complexity algorithms within the Efficient Polynomial Time Heuristic Scheme (EPTHS); they can generalize to arbitrary problem sizes for simple tasks but fail to capture higher-complexity algorithms, despite universal expressivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formalize Algorithmic Capture (grokking) and delineate true algorithmic learning from statistical interpolation; analyze the inductive biases and computational limits of transformers in algorithmic tasks.

Method: Theoretical analysis of infinite-width transformers in both the lazy (NTK-like) and rich regimes; derive upper bounds on inference-time computational complexity for learnable functions; categorize functions within the EPTHS class and assess the bias toward low-complexity algorithms.

Result: Infinite-width transformers possess universal expressivity but an inductive bias that favors low-complexity algorithms within EPTHS; this bias prevents learning higher-complexity algorithms while enabling success on simpler tasks such as search, copy, and sort.

Conclusion: Transformers are predisposed toward simpler algorithmic tasks due to inductive bias, limiting their ability to capture higher-complexity algorithms; improving performance on complex algorithms likely requires alternative inductive biases, architectures, or training dynamics.

Abstract: We formally define Algorithmic Capture (i.e., ``grokking'' an algorithm) as the ability of a neural network to generalize to arbitrary problem sizes ($T$) with controllable error and minimal sample adaptation, distinguishing true algorithmic learning from statistical interpolation. By analyzing infinite-width transformers in both the lazy and rich regimes, we derive upper bounds on the inference-time computational complexity of the functions these networks can learn. We show that despite their universal expressivity, transformers possess an inductive bias towards low-complexity algorithms within the Efficient Polynomial Time Heuristic Scheme (EPTHS) class. This bias effectively prevents them from capturing higher-complexity algorithms, while allowing success on simpler tasks like search, copy, and sort.

</details>


### [145] [Huntington Disease Automatic Speech Recognition with Biomarker Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11168)
*Charles L. Wang,Cady Chen,Ziwei Gong,Julia Hirschberg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HD-focused ASR comparison reveals architecture-dependent errors; Parakeet-TDT outperforms encoder-decoder and CTC; HD-specific adaptation reduces WER from 6.99% to 4.95%; biomarker-based auxiliary supervision proposed; error patterns shift with severity; code/models open-sourced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the underexplored area of Huntington's disease pathology in ASR by evaluating cross-family ASR architectures on a high-fidelity clinical corpus and characterizing error regimes to guide model design and adaptation.

Method: Systematic cross-family evaluation of ASR (encoder-decoder, CTC, and Parakeet-TDT) on a high-fidelity HD speech corpus not previously used for end-to-end training; analyze WER and substitution/deletion/insertion types; apply HD-specific adaptation; propose biomarker-based auxiliary supervision; report open-source code and models.

Result: Parakeet-TDT achieves the best performance among studied architectures; HD-specific adaptation reduces WER from 6.99% to 4.95%; error regimes are architecture-dependent rather than uniformly improved; severity-dependent reshaping of error behavior induced by HD; proposed auxiliary supervision method; code and models released.

Conclusion: HD pathology interacts with ASR architecture in non-uniform ways; targeted adaptation and auxiliary supervision can yield meaningful gains, and shared datasets enable systematic cross-family comparisons and reproducibility.

Abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) for pathological speech remains underexplored, especially for Huntington's disease (HD), where irregular timing, unstable phonation, and articulatory distortion challenge current models. We present a systematic HD-ASR study using a high-fidelity clinical speech corpus not previously used for end-to-end ASR training. We compare multiple ASR families under a unified evaluation, analyzing WER as well as substitution, deletion, and insertion patterns. HD speech induces architecture-specific error regimes, with Parakeet-TDT outperforming encoder-decoder and CTC baselines. HD-specific adaptation reduces WER from 6.99% to 4.95% and we also propose a method for using biomarker-based auxiliary supervision and analyze how error behavior is reshaped in severity-dependent ways rather than uniformly improving WER. We open-source all code and models.

</details>


### [146] [Bayesian Optimization of Partially Known Systems using Hybrid Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11199)
*Eike Cramer,Luis Kutschat,Oliver Stollenwerk,Joel A. Paulson,Alexander Mitsos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid model-based Bayesian optimization integrating Gaussian-process constraints from partially known physics to accelerate optimization of expensive black-box systems; demonstrates rapid convergence and superior designs in an in-silico distillation problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Bayesian optimization can be data-inefficient for high-dimensional nonlinear systems; leveraging partial physics can constrain and guide learning, improving sample efficiency.

Method: Formulate a hybrid model by writing known physics equations and treating unknowns with a Gaussian process; treat GP as a constraint within the nonlinear stochastic program; solve via sample-average approximation; compare to standard BO on in-silico single-stage distillation optimization.

Result: Hybrid BO converges in as few as one iteration (depending on initial samples) and yields significantly better designs than standard BO, which does not converge within 25 seeds; demonstrates the benefit of incorporating mechanistic constraints.

Conclusion: Partial-physics-informed model-based BO is a promising approach for efficiently optimizing partially known systems, combining mechanistic insight with data-driven learning to improve convergence and design quality.

Abstract: Bayesian optimization (BO) has gained attention as an efficient algorithm for black-box optimization of expensive-to-evaluate systems, where the BO algorithm iteratively queries the system and suggests new trials based on a probabilistic model fitted to previous samples. Still, the standard BO loop may require a prohibitively large number of experiments to converge to the optimum, especially for high-dimensional and nonlinear systems. We present a hybrid model-based BO formulation that combines the iterative Bayesian learning of BO with partially known mechanistic physical models. Instead of learning a direct mapping from inputs to the objective, we write all known equations for a physics-based model and infer expressions for variables missing equations using a probabilistic model, in our case, a Gaussian process (GP). The final formulation then includes the GP as a constraint in the hybrid model, thereby allowing other physics-based nonlinear and implicit model constraints. This hybrid model formulation yields a constrained, nonlinear stochastic program, which we discretize using the sample-average approximation. In an in-silico optimization of a single-stage distillation, the hybrid BO model based on mass conservation laws yields significantly better designs than a standard BO loop. Furthermore, the hybrid model converges in as few as one iteration, depending on the initial samples, whereas, the standard BO does not converge within 25 for any of the seeds. Overall, the proposed hybrid BO scheme presents a promising optimization method for partially known systems, leveraging the strengths of both mechanistic modeling and data-driven optimization.

</details>


### [147] [Representation Finetuning for Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11201)
*Haihua Luo,Xuming Ran,Tommi Kärkkäinen,Huiyan Xue,Zhonghua Chen,Qi Xu,Fengyu Cong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoRe introduces Continual Representation Learning by finetuning in a low-rank representation subspace, enabling task-specific interventions with explicit objectives, achieving parameter efficiency and superior performance over existing continual learning methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods operate in weight space via black-box optimization and offer no explicit control over representation drift, making them sensitive to domain shifts and catastrophic forgetting in continual learning.

Method: CoRe shifts finetuning from weight space to representation space by performing task-specific interventions within a low-rank linear subspace of hidden representations, guided by explicit objectives to ensure stability for past tasks while maintaining plasticity for new tasks; updates are constrained to the low-rank subspace.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple continual learning benchmarks show that CoRe preserves parameter efficiency and significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Representation finetuning constitutes a new, more effective and interpretable paradigm for continual learning.

Abstract: The world is inherently dynamic, and continual learning aims to enable models to adapt to ever-evolving data streams. While pre-trained models have shown powerful performance in continual learning, they still require finetuning to adapt effectively to downstream tasks. However, prevailing Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods operate through empirical, black-box optimization at the weight level. These approaches lack explicit control over representation drift, leading to sensitivity to domain shifts and catastrophic forgetting in continual learning scenarios. In this work, we introduce Continual Representation Learning (CoRe), a novel framework that for the first time shifts the finetuning paradigm from weight space to representation space. Unlike conventional methods, CoRe performs task-specific interventions within a low-rank linear subspace of hidden representations, adopting a learning process with explicit objectives, which ensures stability for past tasks while maintaining plasticity for new ones. By constraining updates to a low-rank subspace, CoRe achieves exceptional parameter efficiency. Extensive experiments across multiple continual learning benchmarks demonstrate that CoRe not only preserves parameter efficiency but also significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods. Our work introduces representation finetuning as a new, more effective and interpretable paradigm for continual learning.

</details>


### [148] [Reference-Guided Machine Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11210)
*Jonas Mirlach,Sonia Laguna,Julia E. Vogt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Reference-Guided Unlearning (ReGUn) that uses a disjoint held-out dataset as a class-conditioned reference to distill memory removal, achieving better forgetting-utility trade-offs than baselines by prioritizing distributional indistinguishability over performance-degradation heuristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unlearning methods often rely on unstable performance heuristics (loss maximization, random labeling) that can harm generalization. A principled objective—distributional indistinguishability between forget data and unseen data—promises stable, robust unlearning.

Method: Introduce ReGUn, a framework that leverages a disjoint held-out dataset as a reference for class-conditioned distillation. It guides the model to forget targeted data while aligning its behavior with truly unseen data, using distillation-based objectives.

Result: Empirical evaluation across multiple architectures, natural image datasets, and varying forget fractions shows ReGUn consistently yields better forgetting-utility trade-offs than standard approximate baselines.

Conclusion: ReGUn provides a principled, reference-guided approach to unlearning focused on distributional indistinguishability, with demonstrated improvements over baseline methods. Future work could explore sensitivity to reference dataset choice and scalability.

Abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove the influence of specific data from trained models while preserving general utility. Existing approximate unlearning methods often rely on performance-degradation heuristics, such as loss maximization or random labeling. However, these signals can be poorly conditioned, leading to unstable optimization and harming the model's generalization. We argue that unlearning should instead prioritize distributional indistinguishability, aligning the model's behavior on forget data with its behavior on truly unseen data. Motivated by this, we propose Reference-Guided Unlearning (ReGUn), a framework that leverages a disjoint held-out dataset to provide a principled, class-conditioned reference for distillation. We demonstrate across various model architectures, natural image datasets, and varying forget fractions that ReGUn consistently outperforms standard approximate baselines, achieving a superior forgetting-utility trade-off.

</details>


### [149] [Monitoring and Prediction of Mood in Elderly People during Daily Life Activities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11230)
*Daniel Bautista-Salinas,Joaquín Roca González,Inmaculada Méndez,Oscar Martinez Mozos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Wearable-based mood monitoring for elderly using wristband signals and EMA; ML classifier predicts mood states; shows promising accuracy, competitive with state-of-the-art for happiness/activeness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable real-world mood monitoring and well-being support in the elderly with noninvasive, continuous sensing.

Method: Collect physiological signals via wristband during daily activities; deploy mobile EMA app for self-reported mood; train a classifier using wristband features to predict mood states; evaluate against existing methods for happiness and activeness.

Result: Mood-state classification achieves promising accuracy; performance comparable to SOTA for happiness and activeness.

Conclusion: This approach demonstrates feasibility of real-time mood prediction from wearable data in real-life settings, with potential to enhance elderly care, though details on dataset and generalization are needed.

Abstract: We present an intelligent wearable system to monitor and predict mood states of elderly people during their daily life activities. Our system is composed of a wristband to record different physiological activities together with a mobile app for ecological momentary assessment (EMA). Machine learning is used to train a classifier to automatically predict different mood states based on the smart band only. Our approach shows promising results on mood accuracy and provides results comparable with the state of the art in the specific detection of happiness and activeness.

</details>


### [150] [Differentiable Thermodynamic Phase-Equilibria for Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11249)
*Karim K. Ben Hicham,Moreno Ascani,Jan G. Rittig,Alexander Mitsos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DISCOMAX: a differentiable, thermodynamically consistent phase-equilibrium predictor using discrete state enumeration with masked softmax and straight-through gradients; achieves superior performance on binary liquid-liquid equilibria and generalizes to other equilibrium data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Thermodynamics-aware ML for phase equilibria; existing physics-informed approaches struggle with extremum-principle data (e.g., LLE) and ensuring consistency during training and inference.

Method: Discrete enumeration of feasible states; masked softmax to aggregate; straight-through gradient estimator; end-to-end learning of neural g^E models with thermodynamic structure.

Result: Outperforms surrogate-based methods on binary LLE data; demonstrates thermodynamic consistency at training and inference; general framework for learning from various equilibrium data.

Conclusion: DISCOMAX provides a general, physics-consistent differentiable framework for equilibrium modeling that can be extended to other equilibrium datasets beyond LLE.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of phase equilibria remains a central challenge in chemical engineering. Physics-consistent machine learning methods that incorporate thermodynamic structure into neural networks have recently shown strong performance for activity-coefficient modeling. However, extending such approaches to equilibrium data arising from an extremum principle, such as liquid-liquid equilibria, remains difficult. Here we present DISCOMAX, a differentiable algorithm for phase-equilibrium calculation that guarantees thermodynamic consistency at both training and inference, only subject to a user-specified discretization. The method is rooted in statistical thermodynamics, and works via a discrete enumeration with subsequent masked softmax aggregation of feasible states, and together with a straight-through gradient estimator to enable physics-consistent end-to-end learning of neural $g^{E}$-models. We evaluate the approach on binary liquid-liquid equilibrium data and demonstrate that it outperforms existing surrogate-based methods, while offering a general framework for learning from different kinds of equilibrium data.

</details>


### [151] [Beyond the Class Subspace: Teacher-Guided Training for Reliable Out-of-Distribution Detection in Single-Domain Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11269)
*Hong Yang,Devroop Kar,Qi Yu,Travis Desell,Alex Ororbia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Single-domain training induces Domain-Sensitivity Collapse (DSC), reducing sensitivity to domain shifts and harming OOD detection; a Teacher-Guided Training (TGT) framework uses a frozen multi-domain teacher (DINOv2) to distill residual structure into the student, with no inference cost, yielding strong far-OOD improvements across eight single-domain benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In practical applications, models are often trained on a single domain. This leads to DSC, where supervised learning compresses features into a low-rank class subspace and suppresses directions carrying domain-shift information. Consequently, distance- and logit-based OOD scores lose sensitivity to domain shift. A remedy is needed to preserve or restore domain sensitivity without adding inference complexity.

Method: Introduce Teacher-Guided Training (TGT). During training, distill class-suppressed residual structure from a frozen multi-domain teacher (DINOv2) into the student. After training, discard the teacher and auxiliary head, incurring no extra inference overhead.

Result: Across eight single-domain benchmarks, TGT yields large far-OOD FPR@95 reductions for distance-based scorers: MDS improves by 11.61 percentage points, ViM by 10.78 pp, and kNN by 12.87 pp (ResNet-50 average). In-domain OOD and classification accuracy are maintained or slightly improved.

Conclusion: TGT mitigates Domain-Sensitivity Collapse by transferring residual multi-domain information into single-domain trained models, restoring sensitivity to domain shift and substantially improving far-OOD detection without any inference-time cost.

Abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection methods perform well on multi-domain benchmarks, yet many practical systems are trained on single-domain data. We show that this regime induces a geometric failure mode, Domain-Sensitivity Collapse (DSC): supervised training compresses features into a low-rank class subspace and suppresses directions that carry domain-shift signal. We provide theory showing that, under DSC, distance- and logit-based OOD scores lose sensitivity to domain shift. We then introduce Teacher-Guided Training (TGT), which distills class-suppressed residual structure from a frozen multi-domain teacher (DINOv2) into the student during training. The teacher and auxiliary head are discarded after training, adding no inference overhead. Across eight single-domain benchmarks, TGT yields large far-OOD FPR@95 reductions for distance-based scorers: MDS improves by 11.61 pp, ViM by 10.78 pp, and kNN by 12.87 pp (ResNet-50 average), while maintaining or slightly improving in-domain OOD and classification accuracy.

</details>


### [152] [Duration Aware Scheduling for ASR Serving Under Workload Drift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11273)
*Darshan Makwana,Yash Jogi,Harsh Kotta,Aayush Kubba*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Duration-aware scheduling for ASR serving using audio length as a proxy for processing time; integrating SJF and HRRN into vLLM improves E2E latency, with SJF significantly reducing median latency but risking starvation; HRRN offers a balanced trade-off, reducing median latency and bounding tail-latency growth, maintaining throughput and low scheduling overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FCFS scheduling in large-scale ASR serving causes head-of-line blocking under workload drift. Audio duration correlates with processing time for ASR models (e.g., Whisper), enabling duration-aware policies to reduce end-to-end latency without sacrificing throughput.

Method: Incorporate two classical scheduling algorithms, Shortest Job First (SJF) and Highest Response Ratio Next (HRRN), into the vLLM serving framework. Evaluate on LibriSpeech test-clean under realistic and drifted workloads, comparing against a FCFS baseline.

Result: SJF reduces median E2E latency by up to 73% at high load but increases 90th percentile tail latency by up to 97% due to starvation of long requests. HRRN reduces median E2E latency by up to 28% and bounds tail-latency degradation to at most 24%. Gains persist under workload drift with no throughput penalty and scheduling overhead <0.1 ms per request.

Conclusion: Duration-aware scheduling with SJF and HRRN can substantially improve E2E latency in ASR serving. SJF alone risks starvation; HRRN mitigates this while maintaining most latency gains, providing a practical trade-off for drifted workloads and low scheduling overhead.

Abstract: Scheduling policies in large-scale Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) serving pipelines play a key role in determining end-to-end (E2E) latency. Yet, widely used serving engines rely on first-come-first-served (FCFS) scheduling, which ignores variability in request duration and leads to head-of-line blocking under workload drift. We show that audio duration is an accurate proxy for job processing time in ASR models such as Whisper, and use this insight to enable duration-aware scheduling. We integrate two classical algorithms, Shortest Job First (SJF) and Highest Response Ratio Next (HRRN), into vLLM and evaluate them under realistic and drifted workloads. On LibriSpeech test-clean, compared to baseline, SJF reduces median E2E latency by up to $73\%$ at high load, but increases $90$th-percentile tail latency by up to $97\%$ due to starvation of long requests. HRRN addresses this trade-off: it reduces median E2E latency by up to $28\%$ while bounding tail-latency degradation to at most $24\%$. These gains persist under workload drift, with no throughput penalty and $<0.1$\,ms scheduling overhead per request.

</details>


### [153] [Client-Conditional Federated Learning via Local Training Data Statistics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11307)
*Rickard Brännvall*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A no-communication FL method that conditions a single global model on each client's PCA statistics, achieving oracle-level performance across diverse heterogeneity types and datasets, and showing sparsity robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data heterogeneity in federated learning without cluster discovery or per-client models; improve performance under sparse or multi-dimensional heterogeneity.

Method: Use locally computed PCA statistics from each client to condition the global model; no additional communication required; integrate statistics via conditioning inputs or adapters to the global model.

Result: Evaluated across 97 configurations spanning four heterogeneity types (label shift, covariate shift, concept shift, combined heterogeneity), four datasets (MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100), and seven baselines; matches Oracle (which knows true cluster assignments) across all settings; surpasses Oracle by 1–6% on combined heterogeneity where continuous statistics offer richer information than discrete clusters; uniquely sparsity-robust among tested methods.

Conclusion: The proposed PCA-statistics conditioning approach provides a robust, communication-efficient solution to FL heterogeneity, achieving Oracle-level performance and improving in settings with richer continuous statistics, while maintaining sparsity robustness.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) under data heterogeneity remains challenging: existing methods either ignore client differences (FedAvg), require costly cluster discovery (IFCA), or maintain per-client models (Ditto). All degrade when data is sparse or heterogeneity is multi-dimensional. We propose conditioning a single global model on locally-computed PCA statistics of each client's training data, requiring zero additional communication. Evaluating across 97~configurations spanning four heterogeneity types (label shift, covariate shift, concept shift, and combined heterogeneity), four datasets (MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100), and seven FL baseline methods, we find that our method matches the Oracle baseline -- which knows true cluster assignments -- across all settings, surpasses it by 1--6% on combined heterogeneity where continuous statistics are richer than discrete cluster identifiers, and is uniquely sparsity-robust among all tested methods.

</details>


### [154] [Heavy-Tailed Principle Component Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11308)
*Mario Sayde,Christopher Khater,Jihad Fahs,Ibrahim Abou-Faycal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PCA can be robustly applied to heavy-tailed data under a superstatistical model using a log-loss formulation; the principal components match those from the Gaussian generator, enabling robust covariance estimation and improved performance over classical PCA in impulsive/noisy settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical PCA relies on second-order moments and is fragile with heavy-tailed or impulsive data; a unified treatment for infinite-variance models is needed.

Method: Assume X = A^{1/2}G with A>0 random and G Gaussian. Use a logarithmic loss; show PCA directions coincide with PCA of the Gaussian generator's covariance. Propose robust covariance estimators from heavy-tailed data, compared to empirical covariance and Tyler's scatter estimator.

Result: Under the log loss, principal components align with those from the Gaussian generator; robust estimators recover principal directions reliably and outperform classical PCA under heavy-tailed/impulsive noise, while remaining competitive under Gaussian noise.

Conclusion: A logarithmic loss yields a well-defined PCA under infinite variance; robust covariance estimation enables reliable PCA for heavy-tailed data, with broad applicability and strong empirical performance.

Abstract: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a cornerstone of dimensionality reduction, yet its classical formulation relies critically on second-order moments and is therefore fragile in the presence of heavy-tailed data and impulsive noise. While numerous robust PCA variants have been proposed, most either assume finite variance, rely on sparsity-driven decompositions, or address robustness through surrogate loss functions without a unified treatment of infinite-variance models. In this paper, we study PCA for high-dimensional data generated according to a superstatistical dependent model of the form $\mathbf{X} = A^{1/2}\mathbf{G}$, where $A$ is a positive random scalar and $\mathbf{G}$ is a Gaussian vector. This framework captures a wide class of heavy-tailed distributions, including multivariate $t$ and sub-Gaussian $α$-stable laws. We formulate PCA under a logarithmic loss, which remains well defined even when moments do not exist. Our main theoretical result shows that, under this loss, the principal components of the heavy-tailed observations coincide with those obtained by applying standard PCA to the covariance matrix of the underlying Gaussian generator. Building on this insight, we propose robust estimators for this covariance matrix directly from heavy-tailed data and compare them with the empirical covariance and Tyler's scatter estimator. Extensive experiments, including background denoising tasks, demonstrate that the proposed approach reliably recovers principal directions and significantly outperforms classical PCA in the presence of heavy-tailed and impulsive noise, while remaining competitive under Gaussian noise.

</details>


### [155] [On the Robustness of Langevin Dynamics to Score Function Error](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11319)
*Daniel Yiming Cao,August Y. Chen,Karthik Sridharan,Yuchen Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Langevin dynamics is fragile to even vanishingly small L^p errors in the score estimate, failing to approximate the target distribution within TV distance over any polynomial time, whereas diffusion models remain faithful under similar conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess the robustness of score-based generative modeling to errors in the score function estimate and compare two popular approaches: Langevin dynamics vs diffusion models.

Method: Theoretical analysis showing that L^2 (and general L^p) errors in the score estimate lead to TV-distance divergence from the target distribution for Langevin dynamics, regardless of polynomial time horizon, even for simple high-dimensional distributions; contrasts with existing results that diffusion models can sample faithfully under small score-error assumptions.

Result: For any polynomial time horizon, Langevin dynamics with an estimated score suffers meaningful deviation from the target distribution in TV distance, despite arbitrarily small L^2/L^p score estimation error; diffusion models avoid this degradation.

Conclusion: The findings justify preferring diffusion models over Langevin dynamics when the score function must be learned from data and caution against using Langevin dynamics with estimated scores.

Abstract: We consider the robustness of score-based generative modeling to errors in the estimate of the score function. In particular, we show that Langevin dynamics is not robust to the L^2 errors (more generally L^p errors) in the estimate of the score function. It is well-established that with small L^2 errors in the estimate of the score function, diffusion models can sample faithfully from the target distribution under fairly mild regularity assumptions in a polynomial time horizon. In contrast, our work shows that even for simple distributions in high dimensions, Langevin dynamics run for any polynomial time horizon will produce a distribution far from the target distribution in Total Variation (TV) distance, even when the L^2 error (more generally L^p) of the estimate of the score function is arbitrarily small. Considering such an error in the estimate of the score function is unavoidable in practice when learning the score function from data, our results provide further justification for diffusion models over Langevin dynamics and serve to caution against the use of Langevin dynamics with estimated scores.

</details>


### [156] [Hindsight-Anchored Policy Optimization: Turning Failure into Feedback in Sparse Reward Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11321)
*Yuning Wu,Ke Wang,Devin Chen,Kai Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HAPO introduces a hindsight-anchored policy optimization with a SSI operator and Thompson-inspired gating to stabilize learning in sparse rewards, achieving asymptotic consistency by annealing teacher signals into an on-policy gradient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the instability and bias of group-based methods (e.g., GRPO) in sparse-reward RL, where pure RL suffers from high variance and mixed-policy approaches incur distributional bias; seek a principled, self-paced mechanism to leverage demonstrations without becoming a ceiling.

Method: HAPO uses the Synthetic Success Injection (SSI) operator to anchor optimization to teacher demonstrations during failure, controlled by a Thompson-sampling–inspired gating mechanism that creates a self-paced curriculum; the framework animates between off-policy guidance and on-policy updates.

Result: Theoretically proven asymptotic consistency: as policy improves, the reliance on teacher signals naturally decays, ensuring the on-policy gradient remains unbiased in the limit; off-policy guidance acts as a temporary scaffold rather than a persistent constraint.

Conclusion: HAPO provides a principled approach to combining demonstrations with on-policy learning in sparse rewards, mitigating biases from mixed-policy methods and enabling performance beyond static teacher forcing by progressively reducing dependency on demonstrations as the agent learns.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a promising paradigm for post-training reasoning models. However, group-based methods such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) face a critical dilemma in sparse-reward settings: pure Reinforcement Learning (RL) suffers from advantage collapse and high-variance gradient estimation, while mixed-policy optimization introduces persistent distributional bias. To resolve this dilemma, we introduce Hindsight-Anchored Policy Optimization (HAPO). HAPO employs the Synthetic Success Injection (SSI) operator, a hindsight mechanism that selectively anchors optimization to teacher demonstrations during failure. This injection is governed by a Thompson sampling-inspired gating mechanism, creating an autonomous, self-paced curriculum. Theoretically, we demonstrate that HAPO achieves \textit{asymptotic consistency}: by naturally annealing the teacher signal as the policy improves, HAPO recovers the unbiased on-policy gradient. This ensures off-policy guidance acts as a temporary scaffold rather than a persistent ceiling, enabling the model to surpass the limitations of static teacher forcing.

</details>


### [157] [Meta-Reinforcement Learning with Self-Reflection for Agentic Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11327)
*Teng Xiao,Yige Yuan,Hamish Ivison,Huaisheng Zhu,Faeze Brahman,Nathan Lambert,Pradeep Dasigi,Noah A. Smith,Hannaneh Hajishirzi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MR-Search proposes an in-context meta-reinforcement learning framework for agentic search with self-reflection, training policies across episodes that adapt via explicit self-reflections to improve cross-episode exploration. It introduces a multi-turn RL algorithm with turn-level credit assignment and reports substantial gains (9.2%–19.3%) across eight benchmarks, with code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome sparse rewards and non-stationary exploration in single-episode RL by learning a policy that conditions on and updates from past episodes through explicit self-reflection, enabling better during-test exploration.

Method: Frame a meta-RL formulation (MR-Search) where a policy conditions on past episodes and leverages explicit self-reflection after each episode as contextual input.
