Traditional models for predicting the risk of chronic diseases predominantly focus on either survival analysis or classification independently. In this paper, we show survival analysis methods can be re-engineered to enable them to do classification efficiently and effectively, thereby making them a comprehensive tool for developing disease risk surveillance models. The results of our experiments on real-world big EMR data show that the performance of survival models in terms of accuracy, F1 score, and AUROC is comparable to or better than that of prior state-of-the-art models like LightGBM and XGBoost. Lastly, the proposed survival models use a novel methodology to generate explanations, which have been clinically validated by a panel of three expert physicians.

</details>


### [183] [Hybrid Energy-Aware Reward Shaping: A Unified Lightweight Physics-Guided Methodology for Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11600)
*Qijun Liao,Jue Yang,Yiting Kang,Xinxin Zhao,Yong Zhang,Mingan Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid Energy-Aware Reward Shaping (H-EARS) combines potential-based reward shaping with energy-aware action regularization to deliver linear-time complexity and improved RL performance with partial physics priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data inefficiency and safety concerns in model-free RL for continuous control, where full physics models are costly or unavailable.

Method: Unify potential-based reward shaping with energy-aware action regularization; use functional decomposition to balance task and energy potentials; constrain action magnitude; achieve O(n) complexity by focusing on dominant energy components; provide theoretical foundations: functional independence, energy-based acceleration, convergence with function approximation, approximate potential error bounds; analyze Lyapunov stability heuristics.

Result: Empirical results show improved convergence, stability, and energy efficiency across baselines; vehicle simulations under extreme conditions validate safety-critical applicability.

Conclusion: Lightweight physics priors can enhance model-free RL without full system models and enable transfer from lab to industry.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning excels in continuous control but often requires extensive exploration, while physics-based models demand complete equations and suffer cubic complexity. This study proposes Hybrid Energy-Aware Reward Shaping (H-EARS), unifying potential-based reward shaping with energy-aware action regularization. H-EARS constrains action magnitude while balancing task-specific and energy-based potentials via functional decomposition, achieving linear complexity O(n) by capturing dominant energy components without full dynamics. We establish a theoretical foundation including: (1) functional independence for separate task/energy optimization; (2) energy-based convergence acceleration; (3) convergence guarantees under function approximation; and (4) approximate potential error bounds. Lyapunov stability connections are analyzed as heuristic guides. Experiments across baselines show improved convergence, stability, and energy efficiency. Vehicle simulations validate applicability in safety-critical domains under extreme conditions. Results confirm that integrating lightweight physics priors enhances model-free RL without complete system models, enabling transfer from lab research to industrial applications.

</details>


### [184] [AutoScout: Structured Optimization for Automating ML System Configuration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11603)
*Jimmy Shong,Yuhan Ding,Yihan Jiang,Liheng Jing,Haonan Chen,Gaokai Zhang,Aditya Akella,Fan Lai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AutoScout is a general-purpose ML systems configurator that optimizes mixed discrete/continuous configuration with hierarchical dependencies, achieving 2.7–3.0× training speedup over expert-tuned settings by adaptive feature prioritization and multi-fidelity simulators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ML training/inference systems expose a rapidly expanding configuration space spanning model-parallelism strategies, communication optimizations, and low-level runtime parameters. End-to-end efficiency is highly sensitive to these choices, yet identifying high-performance configurations is challenging due to heterogeneous feature types, conditional dependencies, and high profiling costs. Existing approaches optimize narrow subsets or rely on heuristic methods that do not generalize as spaces grow.

Method: Formulates the system configuration as a mixed-discrete/continuous optimization problem with hierarchical dependencies and introduces a hybrid optimization framework that jointly refines sparse structural decisions and dense execution parameters. To reduce profiling cost, AutoScout adaptively prioritizes high-impact configuration features and ensembles simulators with varying fidelity.

Result: Across diverse models, hardware platforms, and deployment objectives, AutoScout consistently identifies high-performance configurations, achieving 2.7-3.0× training speedup over expert-tuned settings.

Conclusion: AutoScout provides a general-purpose configurator for ML training, fine-tuning, and inference that scales with expanding configuration spaces, reduces profiling cost via adaptive feature prioritization and multi-fidelity simulation, and delivers consistently high-performance configurations.

Abstract: Machine learning (ML) systems expose a rapidly expanding configuration space spanning model-parallelism strategies, communication optimizations, and low-level runtime parameters. End-to-end system efficiency is highly sensitive to these choices, yet identifying high-performance configurations is challenging due to heterogeneous feature types (e.g., sparse and dense parameters), conditional dependencies (e.g., valid execution parameters only under specific upstream decisions), and the high search (profiling) cost. Existing approaches either optimize a narrow subset of configuration dimensions or rely on ad-hoc heuristics that fail to generalize as configuration spaces continue to grow. We present AutoScout, a general-purpose systems configurator for ML training, fine-tuning, and inference. It formulates the system configuration as a mixed-discrete/continuous optimization problem with hierarchical dependencies and introduces a hybrid optimization framework that jointly refines sparse structural decisions and dense execution parameters. To reduce profiling cost, AutoScout adaptively prioritizes high-impact configuration features and ensembles simulators with varying fidelity. Across diverse models, hardware platforms, and deployment objectives, AutoScout consistently identifies high-performance configurations, achieving 2.7-3.0$\times$ training speedup over expert-tuned settings.

</details>


### [185] [Fractional Rotation, Full Potential? Investigating Performance and Convergence of Partial RoPE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11611)
*Mohammad Aflah Khan,Krishna P. Gummadi,Manish Gupta,Abhilasha Ravichander*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Partial RoPE (rotary positional embedding) can drastically reduce memory usage (~10x) while preserving convergence and final performance; applying RoPE to about 10% of hidden dimensions suffices across models and data qualities; NoPE can cause instability but can be mitigated by minimal RoPE or QK-Norm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate how varying the fraction of hidden dimensions receiving rotary transformations impacts training dynamics, convergence, and efficiency, given memory constraints with long context lengths.

Method: Systematic experiments across architectures, model sizes, sequence lengths, and datasets, comparing full RoPE, partial RoPE (~10%), and NoPE; evaluate convergence, final loss, stability, and benchmark performance; assess the effect of QK-Norm.

Result: Memory savings up to 10x compared to standard RoPE cache with comparable final loss; convergence achieved when RoPE is applied to ~10% of dimensions; results consistent across model sizes, sequence lengths, and data qualities; NoPE may cause unstable learning trajectories, mitigated by minimal RoPE or QK-Norm, which tends to converge to higher loss.

Conclusion: Partial RoPE offers a practical efficiency-stability trade-off, enabling significant memory reductions without sacrificing performance; designers should consider applying RoPE to a small fraction of dimensions and use stabilization techniques (e.g., minimal RoPE or QK-Norm) when NoPE is used.

Abstract: Rotary Positional Embedding (RoPE) is a common choice in transformer architectures for encoding relative positional information. Although earlier work has examined omitting RoPE in specific layers, the effect of varying the fraction of hidden dimensions that receive rotary transformations remains largely unexplored. This design choice can yield substantial memory savings, which becomes especially significant at long context lengths. We find up to 10x memory savings over the standard RoPE cache, while achieving comparable final loss. In this work, we present a systematic study examining the impact of partial RoPE on training dynamics and convergence across architectures and datasets. Our findings uncover several notable patterns: (1) applying RoPE to only a small fraction of dimensions (around 10%) achieves convergence comparable to using full RoPE; (2) these trends hold consistently across model size, sequence lengths and datasets of varying quality and architectures, with higher-quality data resulting in lower overall loss and similar benchmark performance; and (3) some models trained with NoPE (No Positional Encoding) showcase unstable learning trajectories, which can be alleviated through minimal RoPE application or QK-Norm which converges to a higher loss. Together, these results offer practical guidance for model designers aiming to balance efficiency and training stability, while emphasizing the previously overlooked importance of partial RoPE.

</details>


### [186] [Personalized Federated Learning via Gaussian Generative Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11620)
*Peng Hu,Jianwei Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces pFedGM, a Gaussian-generative, personalized federated learning method that models client heterogeneity in representations and uses a Kalman-gain-inspired dual-scale fusion to produce personalized classifier heads, achieving competitive results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address both data heterogeneity and heterogeneous representation in federated learning, extending personalization beyond classifier-head tuning to representation distribution modeling.

Method: Train a Gaussian generator with weighted re-sampling to capture client heterogeneity; employ a dual objective: a global objective maximizing inter-class distances across clients and a local objective minimizing intra-class distances within clients; decouple the Gaussian classifier into a navigator (global optimization) and a statistic extractor (distribution statistics); use a Kalman-gain-inspired dual-scale fusion framework at global and local levels to yield personalized classifier heads; model the global representation distribution as a prior and client-specific data as the likelihood, enabling Bayesian inference for class probabilities.

Result: Empirically, pFedGM achieves superior or competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods across varied heterogeneity (class-count differences), environmental corruption, and multiple datasets/configurations.

Conclusion: pFedGM provides a principled mechanism to balance global collaboration and local personalization by modeling representation distributions and applying Bayesian inference, demonstrating robust performance in non-i.i.d. federated settings.

Abstract: Federated learning has emerged as a paradigm to train models collaboratively on inherently distributed client data while safeguarding privacy. In this context, personalized federated learning tackles the challenge of data heterogeneity by equipping each client with a dedicated model. A prevalent strategy decouples the model into a shared feature extractor and a personalized classifier head, where the latter actively guides the representation learning. However, previous works have focused on classifier head-guided personalization, neglecting the potential personalized characteristics in the representation distribution. Building on this insight, we propose pFedGM, a method based on Gaussian generative modeling. The approach begins by training a Gaussian generator that models client heterogeneity via weighted re-sampling. A balance between global collaboration and personalization is then struck by employing a dual objective: a shared objective that maximizes inter-class distance across clients, and a local objective that minimizes intra-class distance within them. To achieve this, we decouple the conventional Gaussian classifier into a navigator for global optimization, and a statistic extractor for capturing distributional statistics. Inspired by the Kalman gain, the algorithm then employs a dual-scale fusion framework at global and local levels to equip each client with a personalized classifier head. In this framework, we model the global representation distribution as a prior and the client-specific data as the likelihood, enabling Bayesian inference for class probability estimation. The evaluation covers a comprehensive range of scenarios: heterogeneity in class counts, environmental corruption, and multiple benchmark datasets and configurations. pFedGM achieves superior or competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [187] [Context-dependent manifold learning: A neuromodulated constrained autoencoder approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11673)
*Jérôme Adriaens,Guillaume Drion,Pierre Sacré*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NcAE extends constrained autoencoders with a neuromodulatory mechanism that tunes geometric constraints (via gain and bias) based on static context, enabling context-dependent manifold learning while preserving projection guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard cAEs tie latent geometry to inputs and struggle with varying physical parameters or environments, leading to entangled contextual shifts with the data representation.

Method: Introduce a Neuromodulated Constrained Autoencoder (NcAE) that conditions gain and bias parameters of the geometric constraints on static contextual information, enabling adaptive, context-aware manifold learning while maintaining projection properties.

Result: Evaluations on dynamical systems show NcAE captures regime-dependent changes in manifold geometry and maintains rigorous projection behavior, demonstrating successful decoupling of global context from local representations.

Conclusion: Neuromodulation provides a flexible mechanism to build physics-informed, context-aware representations in systems with non-stationary environmental constraints, laying a foundation for broader adaptable latent geometries.

Abstract: Constrained autoencoders (cAE) provide a successful path towards interpretable dimensionality reduction by enforcing geometric structure on latent spaces. However, standard cAEs cannot adapt to varying physical parameters or environmental conditions without conflating these contextual shifts with the primary input. To address this, we integrated a neuromodulatory mechanism into the cAE framework to allow for context-dependent manifold learning. This paper introduces the Neuromodulated Constrained Autoencoder (NcAE), which adaptively parameterizes geometric constraints via gain and bias tuning conditioned on static contextual information. Experimental results on dynamical systems show that the NcAE accurately captures how manifold geometry varies across different regimes while maintaining rigorous projection properties. These results demonstrate that neuromodulation effectively decouples global contextual parameters from local manifold representations. This architecture provides a foundation for developing more flexible, physics-informed representations in systems subject to (non-stationary) environmental constraints.

</details>


### [188] [Entropy-Preserving Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11682)
*Aleksei Petrenko,Ben Lipkin,Kevin Chen,Erik Wijmans,Marco Cusumano-Towner,Raja Giryes,Philipp Krähenbühl*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Policy gradient-based LM reasoning often loses exploration due to entropy collapse. The paper analyzes entropy dynamics, identifies factors affecting it, and introduces entropy-preserving methods (REPO and ADAPO) to maintain diversity and improve final performance and trainability in sequential environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Entropy reduction is a common side effect of many policy gradient objectives, harming exploration and diversity. Actively monitoring and controlling entropy can sustain diverse trajectories and improve learning stability and generalization.

Method: Formal analysis of entropy dynamics under leading policy gradient objectives; empirical investigation of factors (e.g., numerical precision) affecting entropy; development of REPO (advantage-modified to regulate entropy) and ADAPO (adaptive asymmetric clipping) as entropy-control mechanisms.

Result: Entropy-preserving training maintains diversity throughout optimization, leading to final policies that are more performant and retain trainability for sequential learning in new environments.

Conclusion: Entropy control is beneficial for policy gradient LM reasoning. REPO and ADAPO provide practical mechanisms to regulate exploration, improving performance and adaptability across sequential tasks.

Abstract: Policy gradient algorithms have driven many recent advancements in language model reasoning. An appealing property is their ability to learn from exploration on their own trajectories, a process crucial for fostering diverse and creative solutions. As we show in this paper, many policy gradient algorithms naturally reduce the entropy -- and thus the diversity of explored trajectories -- as part of training, yielding a policy increasingly limited in its ability to explore. In this paper, we argue that entropy should be actively monitored and controlled throughout training. We formally analyze the contributions of leading policy gradient objectives on entropy dynamics, identify empirical factors (such as numerical precision) that significantly impact entropy behavior, and propose explicit mechanisms for entropy control. These include REPO, a family of algorithms that modify the advantage function to regulate entropy, and ADAPO, an adaptive asymmetric clipping approach. Models trained with our entropy-preserving methods maintain diversity throughout training, yielding final policies that are more performant and retain their trainability for sequential learning in new environments.

</details>


### [189] [Implicit Statistical Inference in Transformers: Approximating Likelihood-Ratio Tests In-Context](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10573)
*Faris Chaudhry,Siddhant Gadkari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ICL in Transformers is analyzed under a Bayesian decision-theoretic view: Transformers implement Bayes-optimal sufficient statistics via task-adaptive estimators rather than fixed kernel smoothing, with evidence from linear vs nonlinear tasks and mechanistic interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a rigorous, ground-truth understanding of in-context learning by framing it as simple binary hypothesis testing and showing how models approximate Bayes-optimal statistics.

Method: Train Transformers on tasks with distinct geometries (linear shifted means vs nonlinear variance estimation), assess whether the models recover Bayes-optimal sufficient statistics up to monotone transformations, and use mechanistic probes (logit lens, circuit alignment) to infer decision boundaries and computation patterns.

Result: The models approximate Bayes-optimal sufficient statistics up to monotone transformations, matching an ideal oracle estimator in nonlinear regimes; mechanistic analysis suggests adaptive decision boundaries, resembling a voting ensemble for linear tasks and deeper sequential computation for nonlinear tasks, not a fixed kernel smoothing heuristic.

Conclusion: ICL arises from constructing task-adaptive statistical estimators rather than simple similarity matching.

Abstract: In-context learning (ICL) allows Transformers to adapt to novel tasks without weight updates, yet the underlying algorithms remain poorly understood. We adopt a statistical decision-theoretic perspective by investigating simple binary hypothesis testing, where the optimal policy is determined by the likelihood-ratio test. Notably, this setup provides a mathematically rigorous setting for mechanistic interpretability where the target algorithmic ground truth is known. By training Transformers on tasks requiring distinct geometries (linear shifted means vs. nonlinear variance estimation), we demonstrate that the models approximate the Bayes-optimal sufficient statistics from context up to some monotonic transformation, matching the performance of an ideal oracle estimator in nonlinear regimes. Leveraging this analytical ground truth, mechanistic analysis via logit lens and circuit alignment suggests that the model does not rely on a fixed kernel smoothing heuristic. Instead, it appears to adapt the point at which decisions become linearly decodable: exhibiting patterns consistent with a voting-style ensemble for linear tasks while utilizing a deeper sequential computation for nonlinear tasks. These findings suggest that ICL emerges from the construction of task-adaptive statistical estimators rather than simple similarity matching.

</details>


### [190] [EvoFlows: Evolutionary Edit-Based Flow-Matching for Protein Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11703)
*Nicolas Deutschmann,Constance Ferragu,Jonathan D. Ziegler,Shayan Aziznejad,Eli Bixby*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Presents EvoFlows, a variable-length Seq2Seq model for protein design that applies controlled edits (insertions, deletions, substitutions) to a template sequence, learning mutational trajectories via edit flows, achieving comparable distribution modeling to masked LMs and superior generation of natural-like mutants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of autoregressive and masked language models in protein engineering; enable explicit control over where and what mutations occur and capture mutational paths and family-wide sequence distributions.

Method: Introduces edit flows enabling limited edits; learns mutational trajectories between evolutionarily related sequences; jointly models natural protein distributions and mutational paths; evaluated in silico on UNIREF and OAS protein communities.

Result: Shows EvoFlows achieve distribution modeling quality comparable to leading masked LMs and improved ability to generate non-trivial, natural-like mutants from a template.

Conclusion: EvoFlows offers a controllable, efficient alternative to LMs for protein engineering, enabling targeted sequence edits and realistic mutational trajectories; promising for design workflows; further experimental validation recommended.

Abstract: We introduce EvoFlows, a variable-length sequence-to-sequence protein modeling approach uniquely suited to protein engineering. Unlike autoregressive and masked language models, EvoFlows perform a limited, controllable number of insertions, deletions, and substitutions on a template protein sequence. In other words, EvoFlows predict not only _which_ mutation to perform, but also _where_ it should occur. Our approach leverages edit flows to learn mutational trajectories between evolutionarily-related protein sequences, simultaneously modeling distributions of related natural proteins and the mutational paths connecting them. Through extensive _in silico_ evaluation on diverse protein communities from UNIREF and OAS, we demonstrate that EvoFlows capture protein sequence distributions with a quality comparable to leading masked language models commonly used in protein engineering, while showing improved ability to generate non-trivial yet natural-like mutants from a given template protein.

</details>


### [191] [Mitigating the Multiplicity Burden: The Role of Calibration in Reducing Predictive Multiplicity of Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11750)
*Mustafa Cavus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Calibration reduces predictive multiplicity in Rashomon set for credit risk; minority class bears higher multiplicity; post-hoc calibration (Platt, Isotonic, Temperature) lowers obscurity; Platt/Isotonic most robust.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensure probabilistic reliability and stability in high-stakes ML; study the relationship between calibration and predictive multiplicity to reduce algorithmic arbitrariness and improve fairness.

Method: Empirical study on nine credit risk benchmark datasets; analyze where multiplicity concentrates; apply post-hoc calibration methods to the predictions; measure obscurity and multiplicity across Rashomon set; compare calibration methods.

Result: Predictive multiplicity concentrates in low confidence regions; minority class observations have higher multiplicity; post-hoc calibration reduces multiplicity/obscurity; Platt Scaling and Isotonic Regression most effective.

Conclusion: Calibration serves as a consensus layer to mitigate predictive multiplicity and support procedural fairness in credit scoring.

Abstract: As machine learning models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes environments, ensuring both probabilistic reliability and prediction stability has become critical. This paper examines the interplay between classification calibration and predictive multiplicity - the phenomenon in which multiple near-optimal models within the Rashomon set yield conflicting credit outcomes for the same applicant. Using nine diverse credit risk benchmark datasets, we investigate whether predictive multiplicity concentrates in regions of low predictive confidence and how post-hoc calibration can mitigate algorithmic arbitrariness. Our empirical analysis reveals that minority class observations bear a disproportionate multiplicity burden, as confirmed by significant disparities in predictive multiplicity and prediction confidence. Furthermore, our empirical comparisons indicate that applying post-hoc calibration methods - specifically Platt Scaling, Isotonic Regression, and Temperature Scaling - is associated with lower obscurity across the Rashomon set. Among the tested techniques, Platt Scaling and Isotonic Regression provide the most robust reduction in predictive multiplicity. These findings suggest that calibration can function as a consensus-enforcing layer and may support procedural fairness by mitigating predictive multiplicity.

</details>


### [192] [Exploiting Expertise of Non-Expert and Diverse Agents in Social Bandit Learning: A Free Energy Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11757)
*Erfan Mirzaei,Seyed Pooya Shariatpanahi,Alireza Tavakoli,Reshad Hosseini,Majid Nili Ahmadabadi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A free-energy-based social bandit algorithm that merges an agent's own experience with observations of others' policies to learn the optimal policy, achieving logarithmic regret and robustness to suboptimal agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To incorporate social learning into reinforcement learning by leveraging observations of other agents' actions without direct access to their rewards, thereby improving individual learning outcomes in multi-agent settings.

Method: Proposes a free-energy-based algorithm over the policy space for social bandits. The agent estimates others' expertise without any oracle or social norms, integrating direct environment experiences with the observed policies of others. The approach provides theoretical convergence guarantees to the optimal policy and identifies relevant agents.

Result: Theoretical proof of convergence to the optimal policy. Empirical evaluations show superior performance of the social learning method compared to alternatives across varied scenarios, robustness to random or suboptimal agents, and the ability to identify and exploit informative agents. Maintains logarithmic regret.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that free-energy social learning can effectively leverage social information in bandit settings, achieving strong performance and robustness in mixed-agent populations while preserving favorable regret properties.

Abstract: Personalized AI-based services involve a population of individual reinforcement learning agents. However, most reinforcement learning algorithms focus on harnessing individual learning and fail to leverage the social learning capabilities commonly exhibited by humans and animals. Social learning integrates individual experience with observing others' behavior, presenting opportunities for improved learning outcomes. In this study, we focus on a social bandit learning scenario where a social agent observes other agents' actions without knowledge of their rewards. The agents independently pursue their own policy without explicit motivation to teach each other. We propose a free energy-based social bandit learning algorithm over the policy space, where the social agent evaluates others' expertise levels without resorting to any oracle or social norms. Accordingly, the social agent integrates its direct experiences in the environment and others' estimated policies. The theoretical convergence of our algorithm to the optimal policy is proven. Empirical evaluations validate the superiority of our social learning method over alternative approaches in various scenarios. Our algorithm strategically identifies the relevant agents, even in the presence of random or suboptimal agents, and skillfully exploits their behavioral information. In addition to societies including expert agents, in the presence of relevant but non-expert agents, our algorithm significantly enhances individual learning performance, where most related methods fail. Importantly, it also maintains logarithmic regret.

</details>


### [193] [A Further Efficient Algorithm with Best-of-Both-Worlds Guarantees for $m$-Set Semi-Bandit Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11764)
*Botao Chen,Jongyeong Lee,Chansoo Kim,Junya Honda*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FTPL with geometric resampling (GR) is analyzed for m-set semi-bandits (a special case of combinatorial semi-bandits). The paper proves optimal regret bounds for adversarial (O(√(m d T))) and stochastic (log) settings using Fréchet and Pareto perturbations, and improves computational efficiency via conditional GR to O(m d (log(d/m)+1)).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FTPL's optimality in m-set semi-bandits was open, unlike FTRL which has established optimality. The work seeks to establish regret-optimality and computational efficiency for FTPL in this setting.

Method: Extend FTPL with geometric resampling (GR) to m-set semi-bandits. Analyze FTPL with Fréchet and Pareto perturbations to achieve optimal regret bounds in adversarial and stochastic regimes. Develop conditional geometric resampling for efficient loss estimation, reducing complexity from O(d^2) to O(md(log(d/m)+1)).

Result: Adversarial: FTPL with Fréchet/Pareto perturbations achieves the best possible regret O(√(m d T)). Stochastic: FTPL with these perturbations achieves logarithmic regret, indicating Best-of-Both-Worlds optimality for m-set semi-bandits. Computational: Conditional GR yields loss estimation with O(md(log(d/m)+1)) complexity, improving over O(d^2) without sacrificing regret performance.

Conclusion: FTPL with GR attains optimal regret in adversarial and stochastic M-set semi-bandit settings and becomes computationally efficient via conditional GR, establishing Best-of-Both-Worlds effectiveness for FTPL in this domain.

Abstract: This paper studies the optimality and complexity of Follow-the-Perturbed-Leader (FTPL) policy in $m$-set semi-bandit problems. FTPL has been studied extensively as a promising candidate of an efficient algorithm with favorable regret for adversarial combinatorial semi-bandits. Nevertheless, the optimality of FTPL has still been unknown unlike Follow-the-Regularized-Leader (FTRL) whose optimality has been proved for various tasks of online learning. In this paper, we extend the analysis of FTPL with geometric resampling (GR) to $m$-set semi-bandits, which is a special case of combinatorial semi-bandits, showing that FTPL with Fréchet and Pareto distributions with certain parameters achieves the best possible regret of $O(\sqrt{mdT})$ in adversarial setting. We also show that FTPL with Fréchet and Pareto distributions with a certain parameter achieves a logarithmic regret for stochastic setting, meaning the Best-of-Both-Worlds optimality of FTPL for $m$-set semi-bandit problems. Furthermore, we extend the conditional geometric resampling to $m$-set semi-bandits for efficient loss estimation in FTPL, reducing the computational complexity from $O(d^2)$ of the original geometric resampling to $O(md(\log(d/m)+1))$ without sacrificing the regret performance.

</details>


### [194] [Language Generation with Replay: A Learning-Theoretic View of Model Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11784)
*Giorgio Racca,Michal Valko,Amartya Sanyal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Replay adversary reveals when replay of a model's own outputs into training data can cause model collapse; benign for uniform generation but causes separations for weaker notions (non-uniform and generation in the limit); aligns with practical mitigations yet also shows failure modes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs scale, the web contains increasing machine-generated content that may re-enter training data. A learning-theoretic perspective on model collapse (beyond heuristics) is needed to understand when training contamination harms generation.

Method: Theoretical analysis in the language generation in the limit framework. Introduces a replay adversary that augments the training stream with the generator's past outputs and analyzes its impact under different generation notions.

Result: Replay is benign for uniform generation but provably creates separations for non-uniform generation and generation in the limit. The results align with common mitigations (data cleaning, watermarking, output filtering) and identify regimes where these ideas can fail.

Conclusion: Provides a fine-grained characterization of when replay constrains generation, offering theoretical justification for practical practices and highlighting their limitations under certain generation notions; informs design of data-contamination defenses and future theoretical work.

Abstract: As scaling laws push the training of frontier large language models (LLMs) toward ever-growing data requirements, training pipelines are approaching a regime where much of the publicly available online text may be consumed. At the same time, widespread LLM usage increases the volume of machine-generated content on the web; together, these trends raise the likelihood of generated text re-entering future training corpora, increasing the associated risk of performance degradation often called model collapse. In practice, model developers address this concern through data cleaning, watermarking, synthetic-data policies, or, in some cases, blissful ignorance. However, the problem of model collapse in generative models has not been examined from a learning-theoretic perspective: we study it through the theoretical lens of the language generation in the limit framework, introducing a replay adversary that augments the example stream with the generator's own past outputs. Our main contribution is a fine-grained learning-theoretic characterization of when replay fundamentally limits generation: while replay is benign for the strongest notion of uniform generation, it provably creates separations for the weaker notions of non-uniform generation and generation in the limit. Interestingly, our positive results mirror heuristics widely used in practice, such as data cleaning, watermarking, and output filtering, while our separations show when these ideas can fail.

</details>


### [195] [Disentangled Representation Learning through Unsupervised Symmetry Group Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11790)
*Dang-Nhu Barthélémy,Annabi Louis,Argentieri Sylvain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unsupervised discovery of symmetry group structure and LSBD representations with identifiability guarantees; two algorithms; validated on three environments; outperforms LSBD baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome reliance on known symmetry groups or subgroup properties in symmetry-based disentanglement; enable agents to autonomously uncover factor structure from interaction.

Method: Prove identifiability under minimal assumptions; derive two algorithms: (1) discovery of group decomposition from interaction data; (2) learning Linear Symmetry-Based Disentangled representations without assuming specific subgroup properties.

Result: Empirical validation on three environments with varying group decompositions; superior performance to existing LSBD approaches.

Conclusion: Introduces a robust framework for autonomous symmetry discovery and LSBD learning with theoretical identifiability and practical gains across diverse environments.

Abstract: Symmetry-based disentangled representation learning leverages the group structure of environment transformations to uncover the latent factors of variation. Prior approaches to symmetry-based disentanglement have required strong prior knowledge of the symmetry group's structure, or restrictive assumptions about the subgroup properties. In this work, we remove these constraints by proposing a method whereby an embodied agent autonomously discovers the group structure of its action space through unsupervised interaction with the environment. We prove the identifiability of the true symmetry group decomposition under minimal assumptions, and derive two algorithms: one for discovering the group decomposition from interaction data, and another for learning Linear Symmetry-Based Disentangled (LSBD) representations without assuming specific subgroup properties. Our method is validated on three environments exhibiting different group decompositions, where it outperforms existing LSBD approaches.

</details>


### [196] [Exponential-Family Membership Inference: From LiRA and RMIA to BaVarIA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11799)
*Rickard Brännvall*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unifies LiRA, RMIA, and BASE as instances of an exponential-family log-likelihood ratio framework; introduces BaVarIA, a Bayesian variance inference attack, with variants BaVarIA-t and BaVarIA-n, achieving robust performance across low-budget/shadow-model settings; establishes a BASE1-4 hierarchy and demonstrates competitive gains across 12 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy auditing of machine learning models via membership inference attacks (MIAs) has matured with LiRA, RMIA, and BASE, but they rely on different scoring assumptions and are not clearly comparable. A unifying, variance-robust framework is needed to clarify relationships and improve performance in practical, low-budget regimes.

Method: Frame MIAs as an exponential-family log-likelihood ratio (LR) test, with data-point level parameters. Show that LiRA, RMIA, and BASE are special cases under different distributional assumptions and parameter-counts, forming a BASE1-4 hierarchy. Identify variance estimation as the main bottleneck at small shadow-model budgets. Propose BaVarIA, a Bayesian variance inference attack using conjugate normal-inverse-gamma priors for the per-point variance, yielding BaVarIA-t (Student-t predictive) or BaVarIA-n (Gaussian with stabilized variance) without extra hyperparameter tuning.

Result: Across 12 datasets and 7 shadow-model budgets, BaVarIA matches or improves LiRA and RMIA, with the largest gains in low-shadow-model and offline regimes.

Conclusion: A unified exponential-family LR framework clarifies the relationships among leading MIAs and centers variance estimation as a bottleneck. BaVarIA provides stable, competitive performance with principled Bayesian variance inference, reducing the need for manual thresholding and hyperparameter tuning.

Abstract: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) are becoming standard tools for auditing the privacy of machine learning models. The leading attacks -- LiRA (Carlini et al., 2022) and RMIA (Zarifzadeh et al., 2024) -- appear to use distinct scoring strategies, while the recently proposed BASE (Lassila et al., 2025) was shown to be equivalent to RMIA, making it difficult for practitioners to choose among them. We show that all three are instances of a single exponential-family log-likelihood ratio framework, differing only in their distributional assumptions and the number of parameters estimated per data point. This unification reveals a hierarchy (BASE1-4) that connects RMIA and LiRA as endpoints of a spectrum of increasing model complexity. Within this framework, we identify variance estimation as the key bottleneck at small shadow-model budgets and propose BaVarIA, a Bayesian variance inference attack that replaces threshold-based parameter switching with conjugate normal-inverse-gamma priors. BaVarIA yields a Student-t predictive (BaVarIA-t) or a Gaussian with stabilized variance (BaVarIA-n), providing stable performance without additional hyperparameter tuning. Across 12 datasets and 7 shadow-model budgets, BaVarIA matches or improves upon LiRA and RMIA, with the largest gains in the practically important low-shadow-model and offline regimes.

</details>


### [197] [Inverse Neural Operator for ODE Parameter Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11854)
*Zhi-Song Liu,Wenqing Peng,Helmi Toropainen,Ammar Kheder,Andreas Rupp,Holger Froning,Xiaojie Lin,Michael Boy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: INO is a two-stage neural inversion framework that reconstructs ODE parameters from sparse observations via a differentiable surrogate (Stage 1) and an amortized drift in parameter space (Stage 2), delivering fast, accurate parameter recovery with large speedups over gradient-based inversion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recover hidden ODE parameters from sparse/partial observations for stiff dynamic systems while avoiding Jacobian instabilities and the computational cost of iterative gradient-based methods.

Method: Stage 1: Conditional Fourier Neural Operator with cross-attention learns a differentiable surrogate mapping sparse inputs to full ODE trajectories; spectral regularization suppresses high-frequency artifacts. Stage 2: Amortized Drifting Model learns a kernel-weighted velocity field in parameter space to transport random parameter samples toward ground truth without backpropagating through the surrogate, thus avoiding Jacobian issues in stiffness.

Result: On POLLU (25 parameters) and GRN (40 parameters), INO achieves superior parameter recovery accuracy compared to gradient-based and amortized baselines, with inference time ~0.23s and ~487x speedup over iterative gradient descent.

Conclusion: Two-stage INO effectively recovers ODE parameters from sparse data while preserving stability and speed, making it suitable for real-world stiff dynamical systems.

Abstract: We propose the Inverse Neural Operator (INO), a two-stage framework for recovering hidden ODE parameters from sparse, partial observations. In Stage 1, a Conditional Fourier Neural Operator (C-FNO) with cross-attention learns a differentiable surrogate that reconstructs full ODE trajectories from arbitrary sparse inputs, suppressing high-frequency artifacts via spectral regularization. In Stage 2, an Amortized Drifting Model (ADM) learns a kernel-weighted velocity field in parameter space, transporting random parameter initializations toward the ground truth without backpropagating through the surrogate, avoiding the Jacobian instabilities that afflict gradient-based inversion in stiff regimes. Experiments on a real-world stiff atmospheric chemistry benchmark (POLLU, 25 parameters) and a synthetic Gene Regulatory Network (GRN, 40 parameters) show that INO outperforms gradient-based and amortized baselines in parameter recovery accuracy while requiring only 0.23s inference time, a 487x speedup over iterative gradient descent.

</details>


### [198] [On the Role of Reversible Instance Normalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11869)
*Gaspard Berthelier,Tahar Nabil,Etienne Le Naour,Richard Niamke,Samir Perlaza,Giovanni Neglia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Study on normalization in time series forecasting; identifies temporal, spatial, and conditional distribution shifts; finds RevIN components can be redundant or harmful; proposes new directions to improve RevIN robustness and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify normalization's role in time-series forecasting and address distribution shift challenges; evaluate RevIN via ablations.

Method: Ablation studies on RevIN components; analysis of three distribution shift types; propose revised perspectives for RevIN design.

Result: Some RevIN components are redundant or detrimental; new perspectives proposed to enhance robustness and generalization of RevIN in time series forecasting.

Conclusion: Normalization remains critical; careful component selection matters; RevIN can be made more robust and generalizable.

Abstract: Data normalization is a crucial component of deep learning models, yet its role in time series forecasting remains insufficiently understood. In this paper, we identify three central challenges for normalization in time series forecasting: temporal input distribution shift, spatial input distribution shift, and conditional output distribution shift. In this context, we revisit the widely used Reversible Instance Normalization (RevIN), by showing through ablation studies that several of its components are redundant or even detrimental. Based on these observations, we draw new perspectives to improve RevIN's robustness and generalization.

</details>


### [199] [FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11901)
*Yijun Pan,Weikang Qiu,Qiyao Ma,Mingxuan Ju,Tong Zhao,Neil Shah,Rex Ying*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-training RL (FlexRec) improves autoregressive ranking with item-level counterfactual rewards and uncertainty-aware critic scaling, yielding substantial gains in NDCG@5 and Recall@5 across need-specific and general settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recommenders are typically optimized for a single static objective, while real-world systems demand dynamic, need-specific goals. LLM-based RL has promise for instruction-following and complex alignment, but applying RL to closed-set autoregressive ranking suffers from coarse sequence-level rewards and sparse/noisy feedback, leading to inefficient learning.

Method: FlexRec is a post-training RL framework for closed-set autoregressive ranking. It uses (i) a causally grounded item-level reward based on counterfactual swaps within the remaining candidate pool to provide fine-grained credit assignment, and (ii) critic-guided, uncertainty-aware reward scaling that models reward uncertainty and down-weights low-confidence rewards to stabilize learning under sparse supervision.

Result: Across diverse scenarios and objectives, FlexRec yields substantial improvements: NDCG@5 up to 59% and Recall@5 up to 109.4% in need-specific ranking, and up to 24.1% Recall@5 improvement under generalization settings, outperforming strong traditional recommenders and LLM-based baselines.

Conclusion: FlexRec effectively addresses the two main obstacles to RL for this setting—coarse credit assignment and sparse/noisy feedback—by delivering fine-grained, uncertainty-aware credit and stable learning, achieving significant performance gains and demonstrating the viability of post-training RL for customizable recommendation goals.

Abstract: Modern recommender systems must adapt to dynamic, need-specific objectives for diverse recommendation scenarios, yet most traditional recommenders are optimized for a single static target and struggle to reconfigure behavior on demand. Recent advances in reinforcement-learning-based post-training have unlocked strong instruction-following and reasoning capabilities in LLMs, suggesting a principled route for aligning them to complex recommendation goals. Motivated by this, we study closed-set autoregressive ranking, where an LLM generates a permutation over a fixed candidate set conditioned on user context and an explicit need instruction. However, applying RL to this setting faces two key obstacles: (i) sequence-level rewards yield coarse credit assignment that fails to provide fine-grained training signals, and (ii) interaction feedback is sparse and noisy, which together lead to inefficient and unstable updates. We propose FlexRec, a post-training RL framework that addresses both issues with (1) a causally grounded item-level reward based on counterfactual swaps within the remaining candidate pool, and (2) critic-guided, uncertainty-aware scaling that explicitly models reward uncertainty and down-weights low-confidence rewards to stabilize learning under sparse supervision. Across diverse recommendation scenarios and objectives, FlexRec achieves substantial gains: it improves NDCG@5 by up to \textbf{59\%} and Recall@5 by up to \textbf{109.4\%} in need-specific ranking, and further achieves up to \textbf{24.1\%} Recall@5 improvement under generalization settings, outperforming strong traditional recommenders and LLM-based baselines.

</details>


### [200] [Causal Representation Learning with Optimal Compression under Complex Treatments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11907)
*Wanting Liang,Haoang Chi,Zhiheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a multi-treatment ITE estimation framework addressing hyperparameter tuning and scalability; provides a generalization bound, a theoretical estimator for optimal balancing weight, compares Pairwise, OVA, and Treatment Aggregation strategies, and extends to a generative Multi-Treatment CausalEGM with Wasserstein-geodesic treatment manifolds; demonstrates improved accuracy and O(1) scalability on semi-synthetic and image datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle two core challenges in multi-treatment ITE: selecting balancing weights without expensive hyperparameter tuning and mitigating the curse of dimensionality to enable scalable estimation across many treatments.

Method: Derives a multi-treatment generalization bound; proposes a theoretical estimator for the optimal balancing weight α to avoid heuristic tuning; analyzes three balancing strategies—Pairwise, One-vs-All (OVA), and Treatment Aggregation; extends the framework to a generative architecture, Multi-Treatment CausalEGM, preserving Wasserstein geodesic structure of the treatment manifold; empirical validation on semi-synthetic and image datasets.

Result: OVA achieves superior precision in low-dimensional settings; Treatment Aggregation attains both accuracy and O(1) scalability as the treatment space expands; the generative Multi-Treatment CausalEGM improves estimation accuracy and efficiency, particularly for large-scale interventions; overall, the approach outperforms traditional models in estimation accuracy and scalability.

Conclusion: Presents a theoretically grounded and scalable approach to multi-treatment ITE estimation, with practical implications for large-scale interventions. The work clarifies when each balancing strategy is preferable, introduces a principled estimator for balancing weights, and showcases a generative model that preserves the treatment-manifold geometry, yielding improved accuracy and efficiency.

Abstract: Estimating Individual Treatment Effects (ITE) in multi-treatment scenarios faces two critical challenges: the Hyperparameter Selection Dilemma for balancing weights and the Curse of Dimensionality in computational scalability. This paper derives a novel multi-treatment generalization bound and proposes a theoretical estimator for the optimal balancing weight $α$, eliminating expensive heuristic tuning. We investigate three balancing strategies: Pairwise, One-vs-All (OVA), and Treatment Aggregation. While OVA achieves superior precision in low-dimensional settings, our proposed Treatment Aggregation ensures both accuracy and O(1) scalability as the treatment space expands. Furthermore, we extend our framework to a generative architecture, Multi-Treatment CausalEGM, which preserves the Wasserstein geodesic structure of the treatment manifold. Experiments on semi-synthetic and image datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms traditional models in estimation accuracy and efficiency, particularly in large-scale intervention scenarios.

</details>


### [201] [EnTransformer: A Deep Generative Transformer for Multivariate Probabilistic Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11909)
*Rajdeep Pathak,Rahul Goswami,Madhurima Panja,Palash Ghosh,Tanujit Chakraborty*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EnTransformer is a deep generative probabilistic forecasting framework that combines engression with Transformer-based sequence modeling to learn and sample conditional distributions for multivariate time series without assuming parametric forms, yielding coherent forecasts with improved calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable uncertainty quantification in multivariate time series forecasting is needed in domains like energy and transportation; existing probabilistic methods rely on restrictive parametric likelihoods or quantile objectives and struggle with joint distributions across multiple series.

Method: Introduces EnTransformer by injecting stochastic noise into model representations and optimizing an energy-based scoring objective (engression) to learn conditional distributions. Leverages Transformer architecture to capture long-range temporal dependencies and cross-series interactions, enabling nonparametric probabilistic forecasting without fixed parametric assumptions.

Result: Empirically, EnTransformer yields well-calibrated probabilistic forecasts and consistently outperforms benchmark models on standard multivariate probabilistic forecasting datasets including Electricity, Traffic, Solar, Taxi, KDD-cup, and Wikipedia.

Conclusion: EnTransformer enables coherent multivariate forecast trajectories with calibrated uncertainty without restricting the predictive distribution to a parametric form, preserving the strengths of Transformer-based sequence modeling.

Abstract: Reliable uncertainty quantification is critical in multivariate time series forecasting problems arising in domains such as energy systems and transportation networks, among many others. Although Transformer-based architectures have recently achieved strong performance for sequence modeling, most probabilistic forecasting approaches rely on restrictive parametric likelihoods or quantile-based objectives. They can struggle to capture complex joint predictive distributions across multiple correlated time series. This work proposes EnTransformer, a deep generative forecasting framework that integrates engression, a stochastic learning paradigm for modeling conditional distributions, with the expressive sequence modeling capabilities of Transformers. The proposed approach injects stochastic noise into the model representation and optimizes an energy-based scoring objective to directly learn the conditional predictive distribution without imposing parametric assumptions. This design enables EnTransformer to generate coherent multivariate forecast trajectories while preserving Transformers' capacity to effectively model long-range temporal dependencies and cross-series interactions. We evaluate our proposed EnTransformer on several widely used benchmarks for multivariate probabilistic forecasting, including Electricity, Traffic, Solar, Taxi, KDD-cup, and Wikipedia datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that EnTransformer produces well-calibrated probabilistic forecasts and consistently outperforms the benchmark models.

</details>


### [202] [Chem4DLLM: 4D Multimodal LLMs for Chemical Dynamics Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11924)
*Xinyu Li,Zhen Zhang,Qi Chen,Anton van den Hengel,Lina Yao,Javen Qinfeng Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes ChemDU, a dynamic chemical understanding task translating 4D molecular trajectories into natural-language explanations; introduces Chem4DBench dataset and Chem4DLLM model to benchmark and enable multimodal reasoning about dynamic chemistry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static molecular representations fail to capture dynamic phenomena (bond formation/breakage, conformational changes) essential for understanding chemical reactions; there is a need for interpretable narratives of molecular trajectories and a benchmark to evaluate such reasoning.

Method: Define a new task (ChemDU) that maps 4D trajectories to explanations; create Chem4DBench, a dataset pairing 4D trajectories with expert-authored explanations; propose Chem4DLLM, a unified model combining an equivariant graph encoder with a pretrained large language model to capture geometry and rotational dynamics.

Result: Introduction of Chem4DBench as the first dataset pairing 4D trajectories with expert explanations; proposal of Chem4DLLM model integrating geometry-aware encoding with language modeling.

Conclusion: Sets up a framework for dynamic chemical understanding and multimodal scientific reasoning, aiming to spur future research and development in dynamic chemistry tasks.

Abstract: Existing chemical understanding tasks primarily rely on static molecular representations, limiting their ability to model inherently dynamic phenomena such as bond breaking or conformational changes, which are essential for a chemist to understand chemical reactions. To address this gap, we introduce Chemical Dynamics Understanding (ChemDU), a new task that translates 4D molecular trajectories into interpretable natural-language explanations. ChemDU focuses on fundamental dynamic scenarios, including gas-phase and catalytic reactions, and requires models to reason about key events along molecular trajectories, such as bond formation and dissociation, and to generate coherent, mechanistically grounded narratives. To benchmark this capability, we construct Chem4DBench, the first dataset pairing 4D molecular trajectories with expert-authored explanations across these settings. We further propose Chem4DLLM, a unified model that integrates an equivariant graph encoder with a pretrained large language model to explicitly capture molecular geometry and rotational dynamics. We hope that ChemDU, together with Chem4DBench and Chem4DLLM, will stimulate further research in dynamic chemical understanding and multimodal scientific reasoning.

</details>


### [203] [MobileKernelBench: Can LLMs Write Efficient Kernels for Mobile Devices?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11935)
*Xingze Zou,Jing Wang,Yuhua Zheng,Xueyi Chen,Haolei Bai,Lingcheng Kong,Syed A. R. Abu-Bakar,Zhaode Wang,Chengfei Lv,Haoji Hu,Huan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs alone struggle to generate production-grade mobile kernels due to engineering complexity and data scarcity; a multi-agent MoKA system with MobileKernelBench yields high compilation success and measurable speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate whether LLMs can author efficient kernels for mobile devices and address the gap between LLM code generation and deployment in mobile frameworks by creating a dedicated benchmark and an automated host-device validation pipeline.

Method: Introduce MobileKernelBench, a benchmark prioritizing operator diversity and cross-framework interoperability, plus an automated pipeline to bridge host-device execution. Evaluate on the CPU backend of Mobile Neural Network (MNN). Propose MoKA, a multi-agent system with repository-aware reasoning and plan-and-execute paradigm, validated on MobileKernelBench.

Result: MoKA achieved 93.7% compilation success and enabled 27.4% of generated kernels to deliver measurable speedups over native libraries, addressing the high failure rates seen with standard and fine-tuned LLMs.

Conclusion: A well-designed multi-agent architecture with domain grounding and repository awareness can unlock production-grade mobile kernel generation, making LLM-assisted development more viable for mobile backends.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in code generation, yet their potential for generating kernels specifically for mobile de- vices remains largely unexplored. In this work, we extend the scope of automated kernel generation to the mobile domain to investigate the central question: Can LLMs write efficient kernels for mobile devices? To enable systematic investigation, we introduce MobileKernelBench, a comprehensive evaluation framework comprising a benchmark prioritizing operator diversity and cross-framework interoperability, coupled with an automated pipeline that bridges the host-device gap for on-device verification. Leveraging this framework, we conduct extensive evaluation on the CPU backend of Mobile Neural Network (MNN), revealing that current LLMs struggle with the engineering complexity and data scarcity inher-ent to mobile frameworks; standard models and even fine-tuned variants exhibit high compilation failure rates (over 54%) and negligible performance gains due to hallucinations and a lack of domain-specific grounding. To overcome these limitations, we propose the Mobile K ernel A gent (MoKA), a multi-agent system equipped with repository-aware reasoning and a plan-and-execute paradigm.Validated on MobileKernelBench, MoKA achieves state-of-the-art performance, boosting compilation success to 93.7% and enabling 27.4% of generated kernelsto deliver measurable speedups over native libraries.

</details>


### [204] [Exhaustive Circuit Mapping of a Single-Cell Foundation Model Reveals Massive Redundancy, Heavy-Tailed Hub Architecture, and Layer-Dependent Differentiation Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11940)
*Ihor Kendiukhov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Exhaustive circuit tracing and higher-order ablation with trajectory steering reveal heavy-tailed, largely unannotated connectivity; redundancy grows with interaction order and model layering imposes causal direction on differentiation in a single-cell transformer (Geneformer).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address biases in mechanistic interpretability that rely on selective sampling, pairwise testing, and observational trajectories by implementing exhaustive feature tracing, higher-order combinatorics, and causal trajectory steering in a single-cell foundation model.

Method: 1) Exhaustive tracing of all 4065 active sparse autoencoder features at layer 5 to obtain downstream edges; 2) Three-way combinatorial ablation across 8 feature triplets to quantify redundancy and synergy; 3) Trajectory-guided feature steering to assess causal impact of layer-derived features on cell differentiation directionality.

Result: 1) 1,393,850 significant downstream edges, a 27-fold expansion over selective sampling; heavy-tailed hub distribution with 1.8% of features driving most connections; 40% of top-20 hubs lack biological annotation. 2) Three-way ablation shows increasing redundancy with interaction order (three-way ratio 0.59 vs pairwise 0.74) and zero synergy; model is subadditive at all tested orders. 3) Trajectory-guided steering reveals a causal link: late-layer features (L17) push toward maturity (fraction positive = 1.0); early/mid layers (L0, L11) push away (fraction positive 0.00–0.58).

Conclusion: The work provides causal, layer-dependent control of cell state in Geneformer, uncovers annotation bias in prior analyses, and demonstrates that higher-order combinatorics reveal substantial redundancy and subadditivity in the model's architecture, informing interpretability practices and future foundation-model design in biology.

Abstract: Mechanistic interpretability of biological foundation models has relied on selective feature sampling, pairwise interaction testing, and observational trajectory analysis. Each of these can introduce systematic bias. Here we present three experiments that address these limitations through exhaustive circuit tracing, higher order combinatorial ablation, and causal trajectory steering in Geneformer, a transformer based single cell foundation model. First, exhaustive tracing of all 4065 active sparse autoencoder features at layer 5 yields 1393850 significant downstream edges, a 27 fold expansion over selective sampling. This reveals a heavy tailed hub distribution in which 1.8 percent of features account for disproportionate connectivity and 40 percent of the top 20 hubs lack biological annotation. These results indicate systematic annotation bias in prior selective analyses. Second, three way combinatorial ablation across 8 feature triplets shows that redundancy deepens monotonically with interaction order, with a three way ratio of 0.59 versus a pairwise ratio of 0.74, and with zero synergy. This confirms that the model architecture is subadditive at all tested orders. Third, trajectory guided feature steering establishes a causal link between layer position and differentiation directionality. Late layer features at L17 consistently push cell states toward maturity, with fraction positive equal to 1.0. Early and mid layer features at L0 and L11 mostly push away from maturity, with fraction positive ranging from 0.00 to 0.58. Together these results move from correlation toward causal evidence for layer dependent control of cell state.

</details>


### [205] [Causal Matrix Completion under Multiple Treatments via Mixed Synthetic Nearest Neighbors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11942)
*Minrui Luo,Zhiheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MSNN extends Synthetic Nearest Neighbors to integrate information across treatment levels, improving causal matrix completion under MNAR while preserving SNN’s theoretical guarantees; it enhances performance in data-scarce treatment settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SNN relies on sufficient data within each treatment level for accurate causal inference under MNAR. With multiple or complex treatments, data per level can be scarce, limiting effectiveness. MSNN aims to pool information across levels to mitigate this issue.

Method: MSNN is an entry-wise causal identification estimator that aggregates information across treatment levels, enlarging the effective sample size while preserving the finite-sample error bounds and asymptotic normality of SNN.

Result: Theoretical guarantees (finite-sample error bounds and asymptotic normality) are maintained for MSNN. Empirical results on synthetic and real-world data demonstrate improved performance, particularly when some treatment levels have limited data.

Conclusion: MSNN broadens the applicability of SNN to settings with data-scarce treatment levels by leveraging cross-treatment information, without sacrificing theoretical guarantees, and improves empirical performance.

Abstract: Synthetic Nearest Neighbors (SNN) provides a principled solution to causal matrix completion under missing-not-at-random (MNAR) by exploiting local low-rank structure through fully observed anchor submatrices. However, its effectiveness critically relies on sufficient data availability within each treatment level, a condition that often fails in settings with multiple or complex treatments. In this work, we propose Mixed Synthetic Nearest Neighbors (MSNN), a new entry-wise causal identification estimator that integrates information across treatment levels. We show that MSNN retains the finite-sample error bounds and asymptotic normality guarantees of SNN, while enlarging the effective sample size available for estimation. Empirical results on synthetic and real-world datasets illustrate the efficacy of the proposed approach, especially under data-scarce treatment levels.

</details>


### [206] [Effective Resistance Rewiring: A Simple Topological Correction for Over-Squashing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11944)
*Bertran Miquel-Oliver,Manel Gil-Sorribes,Victor Guallar,Alexis Molina*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ERR uses effective resistance as a global rewiring signal to mitigate over-squashing by adding high-resistance connections and removing low-resistance ones under a fixed budget, improving long-range communication and predictive performance; it reveals a trade-off with oversmoothing and benefits from pairing with normalization (e.g., PairNorm) for stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph Neural Networks suffer from over-squashing: information from exponentially growing neighborhoods bottlenecks through few edges. Local rewiring criteria miss global connectivity constraints, limiting long-range message propagation.

Method: Compute effective resistance between node pairs as a global connectivity measure. Iteratively connect the pairs with the largest resistance and remove edges with the smallest resistance, under a fixed rewiring budget. The approach is parameter-free aside from the budget and relies on a single global metric. Analyze propagation by tracking cosine similarity of node embeddings across layers and compare rewired vs. original graphs, including directed settings with DirGCN.

Result: ERR strengthens weak communication pathways and improves signal propagation, yielding better predictive performance on both homophilic and heterophilic graphs. It can accelerate embedding mixing in deeper GNNs. The approach reveals a trade-off: improved connectivity can increase oversmoothing unless counteracted by normalization. Combining ERR with normalization techniques like PairNorm stabilizes the trade-off and enhances performance.

Conclusion: A global, topology-correcting rewiring via effective resistance can mitigate over-squashing and enhance information flow in GNNs, but care must be taken to manage oversmoothing; integrating ERR with normalization provides robust gains across diverse graph types and directed settings.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks struggle to capture long-range dependencies due to over-squashing, where information from exponentially growing neighborhoods must pass through a small number of structural bottlenecks. While recent rewiring methods attempt to alleviate this limitation, many rely on local criteria such as curvature, which can overlook global connectivity constraints that restrict information flow. We introduce Effective Resistance Rewiring (ERR), a simple topology correction strategy that uses effective resistance as a global signal to detect structural bottlenecks. ERR iteratively adds edges between node pairs with the largest resistance while removing edges with minimal resistance, strengthening weak communication pathways while controlling graph densification under a fixed edge budget. The procedure is parameter-free beyond the rewiring budget and relies on a single global measure aggregating all paths between node pairs. Beyond predictive performance with GCN models, we analyze how rewiring affects message propagation. By tracking cosine similarity between node embeddings across layers, we examine how the relationship between initial node features and learned representations evolves during message passing, comparing graphs with and without rewiring. This analysis helps determine whether improvements arise from better long-range communication rather than changes in embedding geometry. Experiments on homophilic and heterophilic graphs, including directed settings with DirGCN, reveal a trade-off between over-squashing and oversmoothing, where oversmoothing corresponds to the loss of representation diversity across layers. Resistance-guided rewiring improves connectivity and signal propagation but can accelerate representation mixing in deep models. Combining ERR with normalization techniques such as PairNorm stabilizes this trade-off and improves performance.

</details>


### [207] [Geometry-Aware Probabilistic Circuits via Voronoi Tessellations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11946)
*Sahil Sidheekh,Sriraam Natarajan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adds Voronoi tessellations to probabilistic circuits to model geometry, providing inference bounds, a structural condition for exact tractability, and a differentiable VT for gradient-based learning, with empirical validation on density estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Probabilistic circuits enable exact, tractable inference but rely on data-independent mixture weights, which fail to capture local geometric structure of data. Incorporating geometry into PCs could improve modeling, but naive approaches risk losing tractability.

Method: Introduce Voronoi tessellations (VT) into the sum nodes of a PC to encode geometry. Identify incompatibility between geometry and tractability, and propose two solutions: (1) an approximate inference framework that yields guaranteed lower/upper bounds, and (2) a structural condition on VT that preserves exact tractable inference. Additionally, present a differentiable relaxation of VT to enable gradient-based learning.

Result: Formalizes the incompatibility, provides a bounded-inference framework, establishes a structural VT condition ensuring exact tractable inference, and introduces a differentiable VT relaxation; empirically validated on standard density estimation tasks.

Conclusion: Geometric structure can be integrated into probabilistic circuits without sacrificing tractability under appropriate conditions, and differentiable VT enables learning. The proposed framework offers reliable inference bounds and practical learning capabilities demonstrated on density estimation benchmarks.

Abstract: Probabilistic circuits (PCs) enable exact and tractable inference but employ data independent mixture weights that limit their ability to capture local geometry of the data manifold. We propose Voronoi tessellations (VT) as a natural way to incorporate geometric structure directly into the sum nodes of a PC. However, naïvely introducing such structure breaks tractability. We formalize this incompatibility and develop two complementary solutions: (1) an approximate inference framework that provides guaranteed lower and upper bounds for inference, and (2) a structural condition for VT under which exact tractable inference is recovered. Finally, we introduce a differentiable relaxation for VT that enables gradient-based learning and empirically validate the resulting approach on standard density estimation tasks.

</details>


### [208] [Statistical and structural identifiability in representation learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11970)
*Walter Nelson,Marco Fumero,Theofanis Karaletsos,Francesco Locatello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two notions of representation stability are formalized as statistical identifiability (consistency across runs) and structural identifiability (alignment with an unobserved ground truth), introduced as ε-near-identifiability. The authors prove ε-near-identifiability for models with nonlinear decoders, extending identifiability beyond last-layer representations to intermediate layers in MAEs and supervised learners. ICA can resolve most remaining linear ambiguities, enabling a practical disentanglement workflow (ICA post-processing). With additional data-generating assumptions, they extend to structural identifiability, achieving state-of-the-art disentanglement on synthetic benchmarks and improved generalization in a biology-focused MAE by separating biological variation from batch effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap where existing identifiability notions focus on idealized or single aspects of representations. Propose model-agnostic ε-based definitions for both statistical and structural identifiability, extend to intermediate representations, and provide a practical ICA-based disentanglement recipe with empirical validation in synthetic and real-world (biology) settings.

Method: Define statistical identifiability and structural identifiability up to tolerance ε. Prove a statistical ε-near-identifiability result for representations learned with nonlinear decoders (broad class including MAEs and supervised models). Show ICA can resolve remaining linear ambiguities. Propose ICA post-processing as a practical disentanglement recipe. Under stronger data-generating assumptions, elevate statistical to structural identifiability. Validate through synthetic benchmarks (state-of-the-art disentanglement on vanilla autoencoder) and a foundation-model-scale MAE for cell microscopy to separate biological variation from technical batch effects.

Result: Theoretical ε-near-identifiability guarantee for nonlinear-decoder representations; ICA post-processing substantially reduces linear ambiguities; empirical evidence of strong disentanglement on synthetic tasks and improved generalization in a biology-oriented MAE by separating batch effects from true variation.

Conclusion: ICA-based post-processing offers a practical path to disentangled, near-identifiable representations across intermediate layers, extending identifiability theory beyond last-layer representations and enabling better generalization in real-world domains.

Abstract: Representation learning models exhibit a surprising stability in their internal representations. Whereas most prior work treats this stability as a single property, we formalize it as two distinct concepts: statistical identifiability (consistency of representations across runs) and structural identifiability (alignment of representations with some unobserved ground truth). Recognizing that perfect pointwise identifiability is generally unrealistic for modern representation learning models, we propose new model-agnostic definitions of statistical and structural near-identifiability of representations up to some error tolerance $ε$. Leveraging these definitions, we prove a statistical $ε$-near-identifiability result for the representations of models with nonlinear decoders, generalizing existing identifiability theory beyond last-layer representations in e.g. generative pre-trained transformers (GPTs) to near-identifiability of the intermediate representations of a broad class of models including (masked) autoencoders (MAEs) and supervised learners. Although these weaker assumptions confer weaker identifiability, we show that independent components analysis (ICA) can resolve much of the remaining linear ambiguity for this class of models, and validate and measure our near-identifiability claims empirically. With additional assumptions on the data-generating process, statistical identifiability extends to structural identifiability, yielding a simple and practical recipe for disentanglement: ICA post-processing of latent representations.
