On synthetic benchmarks, this approach achieves state-of-the-art disentanglement using a vanilla autoencoder. With a foundation model-scale MAE for cell microscopy, it disentangles biological variation from technical batch effects, substantially improving downstream generalization.

</details>


### [209] [Topological DeepONets and a generalization of the Chen-Chen operator approximation theorem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11972)
*Vugar Ismailov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends DeepONet operator learning to inputs from arbitrary Hausdorff locally convex spaces via dual measurements, proving a uniform approximation of continuous operators G:V→C(K;R^m) by topological DeepONets, thus generalizing Chen-Chen to non-Banach settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalize operator learning beyond Banach spaces by allowing inputs from any Hausdorff locally convex space X, enabling new applications and theoretical insights. Leverage dual pairings to build a branch that processes linear measurements on X while the trunk handles the Euclidean output domain.

Method: Construct topological feedforward networks on X using continuous linear functionals from X*, and define topological DeepONets where the branch uses these measurements on X and the trunk operates on K⊂R^d. Prove a universal approximation-type result: continuous G:V→C(K;R^m) with V⊂X and K compact can be uniformly approximated by such networks.

Result: Establishes a branch-trunk approximation theorem for operator learning in locally convex spaces, extending the classical Chen-Chen result from function spaces (Banach) to a broader topological setting.

Conclusion: Broadens the scope of operator learning with DeepONets to non-Banach input spaces, connecting topological functional analysis with neural operator architectures. Opens avenues for further study on rates, architectures, and practical implementations in general locally convex spaces.

Abstract: Deep Operator Networks (DeepONets) provide a branch-trunk neural architecture for approximating nonlinear operators acting between function spaces. In the classical operator approximation framework, the input is a function $u\in C(K_1)$ defined on a compact set $K_1$ (typically a compact subset of a Banach space), and the operator maps $u$ to an output function $G(u)\in C(K_2)$ defined on a compact Euclidean domain $K_2\subset\mathbb{R}^d$. In this paper, we develop a topological extension in which the operator input lies in an arbitrary Hausdorff locally convex space $X$. We construct topological feedforward neural networks on $X$ using continuous linear functionals from the dual space $X^*$ and introduce topological DeepONets whose branch component acts on $X$ through such linear measurements, while the trunk component acts on the Euclidean output domain. Our main theorem shows that continuous operators $G:V\to C(K;\mathbb{R}^m)$, where $V\subset X$ and $K\subset\mathbb{R}^d$ are compact, can be uniformly approximated by such topological DeepONets. This extends the classical Chen-Chen operator approximation theorem from spaces of continuous functions to locally convex spaces and yields a branch-trunk approximation theorem beyond the Banach-space setting.

</details>


### [210] [On-Average Stability of Multipass Preconditioned SGD and Effective Dimension](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11989)
*Simon Vary,Tyler Farghly,Ilja Kuzborskij,Patrick Rebeschini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how preconditioning in multipass PSGD interacts with curvature and gradient-noise geometry to affect generalisation, introducing an on-average stability analysis that yields excess risk bounds depending on an effective dimension and showing that poor preconditioners hurt optimization and generalisation, with matching instance-dependent lower bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand generalisation of PSGD under data reuse, where the interaction between population curvature and noise geometry can degrade performance; existing one-pass stability methods are insufficient for multipass settings.

Method: Develop on-average stability analysis for multipass PSGD that accounts for correlations from data reuse; derive excess risk bounds that depend on an effective dimension reflecting curvature and noise geometry; analyze how preconditioners influence this effective dimension; provide matching instance-dependent lower bounds.

Result: Excess risk bounds expressed via an effective dimension; demonstration that ill-chosen preconditioners can worsen both optimization and generalisation; first theoretical results extending on-average stability to multipass SGD; matching lower bounds that are instance-dependent.

Conclusion: Preconditioner choice and the alignment between curvature and noise geometry are central to generalisation in PSGD; the stability-based bounds via effective dimension offer a complete picture, highlighting intrinsic trade-offs and guiding practical preconditioner design.

Abstract: We study trade-offs between the population risk curvature, geometry of the noise, and preconditioning on the generalisation ability of the multipass Preconditioned Stochastic Gradient Descent (PSGD). Many practical optimisation heuristics implicitly navigate this trade-off in different ways -- for instance, some aim to whiten gradient noise, while others aim to align updates with expected loss curvature. When the geometry of the population risk curvature and the geometry of the gradient noise do not match, an aggressive choice that improves one aspect can amplify instability along the other, leading to suboptimal statistical behavior. In this paper we employ on-average algorithmic stability to connect generalisation of PSGD to the effective dimension that depends on these sources of curvature. While existing techniques for on-average stability of SGD are limited to a single pass, as first contribution we develop a new on-average stability analysis for multipass SGD that handles the correlations induced by data reuse. This allows us to derive excess risk bounds that depend on the effective dimension. In particular, we show that an improperly chosen preconditioner can yield suboptimal effective dimension dependence in both optimisation and generalisation. Finally, we complement our upper bounds with matching, instance-dependent lower bounds.

</details>


### [211] [Deep Learning-Based Metamodeling of Nonlinear Stochastic Dynamic Systems under Parametric and Predictive Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12012)
*Haimiti Atila,Seymour M. J. Spence*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Three metamodeling frameworks combining a feature-extraction module with LSTM (MLP-LSTM, MPNN-LSTM, AE-LSTM) are proposed to handle high-dimensional, nonlinear structural dynamics under natural hazards with loading and parameter uncertainties, using MC dropout and negative log-likelihood loss. They are validated on a Bouc-Wen multi-DOF system and a 37-story fiber-discretized nonlinear frame; predictive variance tracks actual error, enabling active learning and confidence assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the computational and predictive-uncertainty challenges in modeling high-dimensional, nonlinear dynamic structural systems under natural hazards, where both external loads (natural hazards) and structural parameters are uncertain. Existing work covers external-load uncertainty but rarely addresses joint loading and parameter uncertainties with explicit neural-network predictive uncertainty.

Method: Develop three architectures by coupling a feature-extraction module with an LSTM: MLP-LSTM, MPNN-LSTM, and AE-LSTM. All use Monte Carlo dropout for predictive uncertainty and train with a negative log-likelihood loss. Validate on two case studies: a multi-DOF Bouc-Wen system and a 37-story fiber-discretized nonlinear steel moment-resisting frame, both under stochastic seismic excitation and parameter uncertainty.

Result: All three approaches achieve low prediction errors. MLP-LSTM is most accurate for the lower-dimensional Bouc-Wen system, while MPNN-LSTM and AE-LSTM outperform on the more complex steel-frame model. A consistent correlation between predictive variance and actual error is observed, supporting their use in active-learning strategies and in quantifying model confidence for structural-response predictions.

Conclusion: The MLP-LSTM, MPNN-LSTM, and AE-LSTM frameworks effectively fuse feature extraction with LSTM under uncertainty, providing accurate predictions and reliable uncertainty estimates. They are well-suited for active learning and confidence quantification in high-dimensional, nonlinear dynamic structural systems subject to natural hazards.

Abstract: Modeling high-dimensional, nonlinear dynamic structural systems under natural hazards presents formidable computational challenges, especially when simultaneously accounting for uncertainties in external loads and structural parameters. Studies have successfully incorporated uncertainties related to external loads from natural hazards, but few have simultaneously addressed loading and parameter uncertainties within structural systems while accounting for prediction uncertainty of neural networks. To address these gaps, three metamodeling frameworks were formulated, each coupling a feature-extraction module implemented through a multi-layer perceptron (MLP), a message-passing neural network (MPNN), or an autoencoder (AE) with a long short-term memory (LSTM) network using Monte Carlo dropout and a negative log-likelihood loss. The resulting architectures (MLP-LSTM, MPNN-LSTM, and AE-LSTM) were validated on two case studies: a multi-degree-of-freedom Bouc-Wen system and a 37-story fiber-discretized nonlinear steel moment-resisting frame, both subjected to stochastic seismic excitation and structural parameter uncertainty. All three approaches achieved low prediction errors: the MLP-LSTM yielded the most accurate results for the lower-dimensional Bouc-Wen system, whereas the MPNN-LSTM and AE-LSTM provided superior performance on the more complex steel-frame model. Moreover, a consistent correlation between predictive variance and actual error confirms the suitability of these frameworks for active-learning strategies and for assessing model confidence in structural response predictions.

</details>


### [212] [Flowcean - Model Learning for Cyber-Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12015)
*Maximilian Schmidt,Swantje Plambeck,Markus Knitt,Hendrik Rose,Goerschwin Fey,Jan Christian Wieck,Stephan Balduin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flowcean is a modular, data-driven CPS modeling framework that integrates various learning strategies and tools to streamline model generation and evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CPS modeling is complex and time-consuming; data-driven approaches can accelerate development, but require modular, interoperable tooling to combine diverse ML methods.

Method: A modular architecture offering configurable learning strategies, data processing pipelines, evaluation metrics, and plug-in integration with multiple libraries, enabling flexible, reusable CPS model generation workflows.

Result: Expected outcomes include increased efficiency, accessibility, and adaptability in CPS model creation and evaluation, with the ability to compose diverse modeling approaches.

Conclusion: Flowcean provides a practical and extensible framework that facilitates rapid, modular CPS model generation and assessment through data-driven learning.

Abstract: Effective models of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) are crucial for their design and operation. Constructing such models is difficult and time-consuming due to the inherent complexity of CPS. As a result, data-driven model generation using machine learning methods is gaining popularity. In this paper, we present Flowcean, a novel framework designed to automate the generation of models through data-driven learning that focuses on modularity and usability. By offering various learning strategies, data processing methods, and evaluation metrics, our framework provides a comprehensive solution, tailored to CPS scenarios. Flowcean facilitates the integration of diverse learning libraries and tools within a modular and flexible architecture, ensuring adaptability to a wide range of modeling tasks. This streamlines the process of model generation and evaluation, making it more efficient and accessible.

</details>


### [213] [Efficient Generative Modeling with Unitary Matrix Product States Using Riemannian Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12026)
*Haotong Duan,Zhongming Chen,Ngai Wong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unitary MPS with Riemannian optimization enables efficient, stable unsupervised generative modeling of high-dimensional distributions, demonstrated on Bars-and-Stripes and EMNIST.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve training efficiency and interpretability of matrix product states (MPS) for generative modeling by addressing gradient-based training inefficiencies and parameter update ambiguity.

Method: Propose a unitary MPS architecture; cast probabilistic modeling as optimization on a manifold; derive a space-decoupling algorithm via Riemannian optimization to ease updates.

Result: Demonstrates fast adaptation to data structure, stable parameter updates, and strong performance while preserving MPS efficiency and expressive power on benchmark datasets (Bars-and-Stripes, EMNIST).

Conclusion: Unitary MPS with manifold-constrained optimization provides an effective, efficient framework for high-dimensional generative modeling with clear interpretability and training stability.

Abstract: Tensor networks, which are originally developed for characterizing complex quantum many-body systems, have recently emerged as a powerful framework for capturing high-dimensional probability distributions with strong physical interpretability. This paper systematically studies matrix product states (MPS) for generative modeling and shows that unitary MPS, which is a tensor-network architecture that is both simple and expressive, offers clear benefits for unsupervised learning by reducing ambiguity in parameter updates and improving efficiency. To overcome the inefficiency of standard gradient-based MPS training, we develop a Riemannian optimization approach that casts probabilistic modeling as an optimization problem with manifold constraints, and further derive an efficient space-decoupling algorithm. Experiments on Bars-and-Stripes and EMNIST datasets demonstrate fast adaptation to data structure, stable updates, and strong performance while maintaining the efficiency and expressive power of MPS.

</details>


### [214] [Frequentist Consistency of Prior-Data Fitted Networks for Causal Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12037)
*Valentyn Melnychuk,Vahid Balazadeh,Stefan Feuerriegel,Rahul G. Krishnan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PFN-based causal estimators can exhibit prior-induced confounding and lack frequentist consistency; propose a one-step posterior calibration (OSPC) using martingale posteriors to restore consistency and achieve a Bernstein-von Mises limit; empirical results show calibrated PFNs achieve asymptotic frequentist equivalence and good finite-sample calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap that PFN-based causal estimators may not yield uncertainty that aligns with classical frequentist ATE estimators due to prior-driven bias; establish conditions under which PFN uncertainty is consistent and calibratable.

Method: (1) Analyze PFNs as Bayesian ATE estimators and identify prior-induced confounding bias that prevents data from overwriting the prior; (2) propose one-step posterior calibration (OSPC); (3) implement OSPC via tailoring martingale posteriors on PFNs to recover functional nuisance posteriors; (4) derive a semi-parametric Bernstein-von Mises result for calibrated PFNs; (5) validate through (semi-)synthetic experiments comparing calibration and uncertainty with classical estimators.

Result: OSPC mitigates prior-induced bias and restores frequentist consistency; calibrated PFNs with martingale posteriors yield asymptotic distribution matching the semi-parametric efficient estimator; finite-sample uncertainty is well-calibrated relative to other Bayesian ATE estimators.

Conclusion: Calibration of PFN-based ATE estimators via OSPC with martingale posteriors is necessary to achieve frequentist-consistent uncertainty; the approach yields a Bernstein-von Mises-type result and empirical calibration advantages.

Abstract: Foundation models based on prior-data fitted networks (PFNs) have shown strong empirical performance in causal inference by framing the task as an in-context learning problem.However, it is unclear whether PFN-based causal estimators provide uncertainty quantification that is consistent with classical frequentist estimators. In this work, we address this gap by analyzing the frequentist consistency of PFN-based estimators for the average treatment effect (ATE). (1) We show that existing PFNs, when interpreted as Bayesian ATE estimators, can exhibit prior-induced confounding bias: the prior is not asymptotically overwritten by data, which, in turn, prevents frequentist consistency. (2) As a remedy, we suggest employing a calibration procedure based on a one-step posterior correction (OSPC). We show that the OSPC helps to restore frequentist consistency and can yield a semi-parametric Bernstein-von Mises theorem for calibrated PFNs (i.e., both the calibrated PFN-based estimators and the classical semi-parametric efficient estimators converge in distribution with growing data size). (3) Finally, we implement OSPC through tailoring martingale posteriors on top of the PFNs. In this way, we are able to recover functional nuisance posteriors from PFNs, required by the OSPC. In multiple (semi-)synthetic experiments, PFNs calibrated with our martingale posterior OSPC produce ATE uncertainty that (i) asymptotically matches frequentist uncertainty and (ii) is well calibrated in finite samples in comparison to other Bayesian ATE estimators.

</details>


### [215] [Slow-Fast Inference: Training-Free Inference Acceleration via Within-Sentence Support Stability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12038)
*Xingyu Xie,Zhaochen Yu,Yue Liao,Tao Wang,Kim-Chuan Toh,Shuicheng Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A training-free decoding framework (SFI) reduces inference cost for long-context autoregressive models by alternating frequent fast steps with occasional dense-attention slow steps. Fast steps reuse a compact sparse memory; slow steps revisit broader context near semantic boundaries to refresh memory, via a Selector. This yields substantial throughput gains with quality close to the full-KV baseline across long-context and long-CoT tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context autoregressive decoding is computationally expensive due to growing histories. The authors observe that within semantically coherent spans, dominant attention patterns remain largely stable. Exploiting this, they aim to cut recomputation without retraining.

Method: Decouple generation into fast steps and slow steps. Fast steps reuse a compact sparse memory for efficient decoding. Slow steps, triggered near semantic boundaries, revisit the broader context and use a Selector to refresh the selected memory for subsequent fast steps. The approach is training-free and compatible with existing checkpoints.

Result: Across tested context lengths, SFI achieves approximately 1.6× to 14.4× higher decoding throughput while generally maintaining quality on par with the full-KV baseline for long-context and long-CoT scenarios.

Conclusion: SFI offers a practical, training-free path to reducing inference costs in long-context autoregressive reasoning. By separating fast, sparse-decoding steps from occasional dense-attention slow steps that refresh memory, it enables substantial speedups without retraining, applicable to long-horizon and agentic workloads.

Abstract: Long-context autoregressive decoding remains expensive because each decoding step must repeatedly process a growing history. We observe a consistent pattern during decoding: within a sentence, and more generally within a short semantically coherent span, the dominant attention support often remains largely stable. Motivated by this observation, we propose Slow-Fast Inference (SFI), a training-free decoding framework that decouples generation into frequent low-cost fast steps and occasional dense-attention slow steps. Fast steps reuse a compact sparse memory for efficient decoding. Slow steps are triggered near semantic boundaries. At slow steps, the model revisits the broader context and uses the Selector to refresh the selected memory for subsequent fast steps. Across the evaluated context lengths, SFI delivers approximately $1.6\times$--$14.4\times$ higher decoding throughput while generally maintaining quality on par with the full-KV baseline across long-context and long-CoT settings. Because SFI is training-free and applies directly to existing checkpoints, it offers a practical path to reducing inference cost for contemporary autoregressive reasoning models in long-context, long-horizon, and agentic workloads.

</details>


### [216] [Chemical Reaction Networks Learn Better than Spiking Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12060)
*Sophie Jaffard,Ivo F. Sbalzarini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CRNs without hidden layers can learn tasks that require hidden layers in spiking neural networks; provides theoretical bounds (regret, asymptotics, VC dimension) and empirical digit classification showing CRNs outperform SNNs with hidden layers; supports ML in chemical computers and biological efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess the learning capabilities of chemical reaction networks (CRNs) and provide a mathematical explanation for efficient learning in biochemical networks, contrasting them with spiking neural networks (SNNs).

Method: Analytical proof using deterministic mass-action kinetics; constructs a no-hidden-layer CRN that solves a task previously shown achievable by a spiking neural network with hidden layers; derives regret bounds, asymptotic behavior, and VC dimension; includes a numerical experiment on handwritten digit classification.

Result: Demonstrates that a CRN without hidden layers can solve the specified task; provides analytical performance bounds and VC dimension insights; numerical results indicate higher accuracy and efficiency for CRNs compared to SNNs with hidden layers.

Conclusion: Provides a mathematical basis for learning efficiency in biochemical networks, motivating machine learning in chemical computation and offering an explanation for potentially superior learning in cellular biochemical networks vs neuronal networks.

Abstract: We mathematically prove that chemical reaction networks without hidden layers can solve tasks for which spiking neural networks require hidden layers. Our proof uses the deterministic mass-action kinetics formulation of chemical reaction networks. Specifically, we prove that a certain reaction network without hidden layers can learn a classification task previously proved to be achievable by a spiking neural network with hidden layers. We provide analytical regret bounds for the global behavior of the network and analyze its asymptotic behavior and Vapnik-Chervonenkis dimension. In a numerical experiment, we confirm the learning capacity of the proposed chemical reaction network for classifying handwritten digits in pixel images, and we show that it solves the task more accurately and efficiently than a spiking neural network with hidden layers. This provides a motivation for machine learning in chemical computers and a mathematical explanation for how biological cells might exhibit more efficient learning behavior within biochemical reaction networks than neuronal networks.

</details>


### [217] [A Multi-Label Temporal Convolutional Framework for Transcription Factor Binding Characterization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12073)
*Pietro Demurtas,Ferdinando Zanchetta,Giovanni Perini,Rita Fioresi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-label deep learning approach using Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs) predicts binding profiles for multiple transcription factors (TFs) on DNA sequences, uncovering co-binding motifs and interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TFs often act cooperatively and cover complex regulatory logic; existing methods largely address single TFs and binary outcomes, leaving interaction patterns underexplored.

Method: Frame TF binding site recognition as a multi-label classification problem and train Temporal Convolutional Networks to predict multiple TF binding profiles from DNA sequences, capturing correlations among TFs and their cooperative regulation.

Result: TCNs achieve reliable multi-TF binding predictions; identified motifs and co-binding patterns align with known TF interactions and suggest novel TF cooperation relationships.

Conclusion: Multi-label learning with TCNs can reveal biologically meaningful TF motifs and interactions, enabling discovery of cooperative regulatory relationships among TFs.

Abstract: Transcription factors (TFs) regulate gene expression through complex and co-operative mechanisms. While many TFs act together, the logic underlying TFs binding and their interactions is not fully understood yet. Most current approaches for TF binding site prediction focus on individual TFs and binary classification tasks, without a full analysis of the possible interactions among various TFs. In this paper we investigate DNA TF binding site recognition as a multi-label classification problem, achieving reliable predictions for multiple TFs on DNA sequences retrieved in public repositories. Our deep learning models are based on Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs), which are able to predict multiple TF binding profiles, capturing correlations among TFs andtheir cooperative regulatory mechanisms. Our results suggest that multi-label learning leading to reliable predictive performances can reveal biologically meaningful motifs and co-binding patterns consistent with known TF interactions, while also suggesting novel relationships and cooperation among TFs.

</details>


### [218] [Cross-Domain Policy Optimization via Bellman Consistency and Hybrid Critics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12087)
*Ming-Hong Chen,Kuan-Chen Pan,You-De Huang,Xi Liu,Ping-Chun Hsieh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QAvatar is a cross-domain RL method that uses cross-domain Bellman consistency to measure transferability and a hyperparameter-free, adaptive hybrid critic to fuse source and target Q-functions, yielding reliable transfer and strong performance on locomotion and robot arm tasks; code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-domain RL faces two key issues: (1) differing state/action spaces between source and target domains blocking direct transfer; (2) uncertain transferability of source models which can cause negative transfer. The work seeks principled transferability assessment and robust transfer via a hybrid critic.

Method: Introduce cross-domain Bellman consistency as a transferability metric. Propose QAvatar, which combines source and target Q-functions using an adaptive, hyperparameter-free weighting scheme, enabling robust knowledge transfer without manual tuning. Analyze convergence properties.

Result: The approach provides convergence guarantees and demonstrates favorable transferability across RL benchmarks (locomotion and robot arm manipulation), indicating effective cross-domain knowledge transfer.

Conclusion: QAvatar offers a principled, hyperparameter-free framework for cross-domain transfer in RL, with theoretical convergence insights and empirical success; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Cross-domain reinforcement learning (CDRL) is meant to improve the data efficiency of RL by leveraging the data samples collected from a source domain to facilitate the learning in a similar target domain. Despite its potential, cross-domain transfer in RL is known to have two fundamental and intertwined challenges: (i) The source and target domains can have distinct state space or action space, and this makes direct transfer infeasible and thereby requires more sophisticated inter-domain mappings; (ii) The transferability of a source-domain model in RL is not easily identifiable a priori, and hence CDRL can be prone to negative effect during transfer. In this paper, we propose to jointly tackle these two challenges through the lens of \textit{cross-domain Bellman consistency} and \textit{hybrid critic}. Specifically, we first introduce the notion of cross-domain Bellman consistency as a way to measure transferability of a source-domain model. Then, we propose $Q$Avatar, which combines the Q functions from both the source and target domains with an adaptive hyperparameter-free weight function. Through this design, we characterize the convergence behavior of $Q$Avatar and show that $Q$Avatar achieves reliable transfer in the sense that it effectively leverages a source-domain Q function for knowledge transfer to the target domain. Through experiments, we demonstrate that $Q$Avatar achieves favorable transferability across various RL benchmark tasks, including locomotion and robot arm manipulation. Our code is available at https://rl-bandits-lab.github.io/Cross-Domain-RL/.

</details>


### [219] [Resource-Efficient Iterative LLM-Based NAS with Feedback Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12091)
*Xiaojie Gu,Dmitry Ignatov,Radu Timofte*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM-guided NAS on a single GPU using a memory-based, closed-loop search with structured failure signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce NAS cost and enable hardware-efficient edge models by leveraging offline LLMs without fine-tuning, avoiding cloud infrastructure.

Method: A sliding window (K=5) of diagnostic triples (problem, modification, outcome) guides iterative learning. Dual LLM roles: Code Generator for executable PyTorch architectures and Prompt Improver for diagnostic reasoning. Frozen instruction-tuned LLMs (≤7B) operate in a shared limited VRAM space, evaluating with one-epoch proxy accuracy over up to 2000 iterations on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNette.

Result: On CIFAR-10, DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B: 28.2%→69.2%; Qwen2.5-7B: 50.0%→71.5%; GLM-5: 43.2%→62.0%. Full search ~2000 iterations in ~18 GPU hours on a single RTX 4090.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a low-budget, reproducible, and hardware-aware paradigm for LLM-driven NAS that operates without cloud services and targets edge-deployable models.

Abstract: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) automates network design, but conventional methods demand substantial computational resources. We propose a closed-loop pipeline leveraging large language models (LLMs) to iteratively generate, evaluate, and refine convolutional neural network architectures for image classification on a single consumer-grade GPU without LLM fine-tuning. Central to our approach is a historical feedback memory inspired by Markov chains: a sliding window of $K{=}5$ recent improvement attempts keeps context size constant while providing sufficient signal for iterative learning. Unlike prior LLM optimizers that discard failure trajectories, each history entry is a structured diagnostic triple -- recording the identified problem, suggested modification, and resulting outcome -- treating code execution failures as first-class learning signals. A dual-LLM specialization reduces per-call cognitive load: a Code Generator produces executable PyTorch architectures while a Prompt Improver handles diagnostic reasoning. Since both the LLM and architecture training share limited VRAM, the search implicitly favors compact, hardware-efficient models suited to edge deployment. We evaluate three frozen instruction-tuned LLMs (${\leq}7$B parameters) across up to 2000 iterations in an unconstrained open code space, using one-epoch proxy accuracy on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNette as a fast ranking signal. On CIFAR-10, DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B improves from 28.2% to 69.2%, Qwen2.5-7B from 50.0% to 71.5%, and GLM-5 from 43.2% to 62.0%. A full 2000-iteration search completes in ${\approx}18$ GPU hours on a single RTX~4090, establishing a low-budget, reproducible, and hardware-aware paradigm for LLM-driven NAS without cloud infrastructure.

</details>


### [220] [Taming the Adversary: Stable Minimax Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient via Fractional Objectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12110)
*Taeho Lee,Donghwan Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A minimax framework (MMDDPG) trains disturbance-resilient policies for continuous control by pitting a user policy against an adversarial disturbance policy, using a fractional objective to balance performance and disturbance magnitude; experiments in MuJoCo show improved robustness to external perturbations and model variations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL agents are sensitive to unexpected disturbances and model uncertainties; robust performance under such conditions is essential for reliable deployment.

Method: Formulates training as a minimax optimization between a user policy and an adversarial disturbance policy. Introduces a fractional objective that balances task performance and disturbance magnitude to stabilize the interaction.

Result: MMDDPG demonstrates significantly improved robustness against external force perturbations and model parameter variations in MuJoCo environments.

Conclusion: The MMDDPG framework provides disturbance-resilient policies in continuous control by combining adversarial disturbance training with a fractional objective, enhancing robustness to disturbances and parameter uncertainties.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved remarkable success in a wide range of control and decision-making tasks. However, RL agents often exhibit unstable or degraded performance when deployed in environments subject to unexpected external disturbances and model uncertainties. Consequently, ensuring reliable performance under such conditions remains a critical challenge. In this paper, we propose minimax deep deterministic policy gradient (MMDDPG), a framework for learning disturbance-resilient policies in continuous control tasks. The training process is formulated as a minimax optimization problem between a user policy and an adversarial disturbance policy. In this problem, the user learns a robust policy that minimizes the objective function, while the adversary generates disturbances that maximize it. To stabilize this interaction, we introduce a fractional objective that balances task performance and disturbance magnitude. This objective prevents excessively aggressive disturbances and promotes robust learning. Experimental evaluations in MuJoCo environments demonstrate that the proposed MMDDPG achieves significantly improved robustness against both external force perturbations and model parameter variations.

</details>


### [221] [Cornserve: A Distributed Serving System for Any-to-Any Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12118)
*Jae-Won Chung,Jeff J. Ma,Jisang Ahn,Yizhuo Liang,Akshay Jajoo,Myungjin Lee,Mosharaf Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cornserve is a Kubernetes-based distributed serving system for generic Any-to-Any multimodal models that enables component disaggregation, independent scaling, and direct producer-to-consumer tensor forwarding, yielding substantial throughput and latency improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Any-to-Any models combine multiple modalities and have diverse input/output requirements and component-level scaling characteristics, making efficient serving challenging.

Method: Introduces a flexible task abstraction for expressing Any-to-Any computation graphs, enables component disaggregation and independent scaling, and uses a record-and-replay execution model to track data dependencies. The runtime forwards tensor data directly from producer to consumer, avoiding unnecessary materialization. Implemented on Kubernetes with ~23k lines of Python.

Result: Demonstrates up to 3.81× higher throughput and 5.79× lower tail latency; open-source project with a demo video on YouTube.

Conclusion: Shows that Cornserve provides an efficient and scalable serving platform for generic Any-to-Any models and is ready for broader adoption.

Abstract: Any-to-Any models are an emerging class of multimodal models that accept combinations of multimodal data (e.g., text, image, video, audio) as input and generate them as output. Serving these models are challenging; different requests with different input and output modalities traverse different paths through the model computation graph, and each component of the model have different scaling characteristics.
  We present Cornserve, a distributed serving system for generic Any-to-Any models. Cornserve provides a flexible task abstraction for expressing Any-to-Any model computation graphs, enabling component disaggregation and independent scaling. The distributed runtime dispatches compute to the data plane via an efficient record-and-replay execution model that keeps track of data dependencies, and forwards tensor data between components directly from the producer to the consumer. Built on Kubernetes with approximately 23K new lines of Python, Cornserve supports diverse Any-to-Any models and delivers up to 3.81$\times$ higher throughput and 5.79$\times$ lower tail latency. Cornserve is open-source, and the demo video is available on YouTube.

</details>


### [222] [Automatic Generation of High-Performance RL Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12145)
*Seth Karten,Rahul Dev Appapogu,Chi Jin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Presents a reusable recipe combining prompts, hierarchical verification, and iterative agent-assisted repair to convert RL environments into high-performance implementations across five environments, achieving semantic equivalence and zero sim-to-sim gap, and enabling new environment creation with low overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Significantly reduce engineering effort to produce high-performance RL environments and ensure reproducible, verifiable translations with minimal compute cost.

Method: Three workflows: (1) direct translation with prompts (EmuRust, PokeJAX); (2) translation verification against existing implementations with parity/throughput improvements (MJX, Brax, HalfCheetah JAX, Puffer Pong); (3) new environment creation (TCGJax) synthesized from web specs. Core components include a generic prompt template, hierarchical verification (property, interaction, rollout tests), and iterative agent-assisted repair; cross-backend policy transfer checks; contamination control via a private reference.

Result: Five environments translated/constructed with semantic equivalence confirmed by hierarchical verification; zero sim-to-sim gap across backends; substantial speedups and throughput gains (e.g., EmuRust 1.5x PPO; PokeJAX up to 22,320x over TS reference; Brax parity/5x; 42x PPO on Puffer Pong; TCGJax 717K SPS random, 153K SPS PPO; overhead under 4% at 200M params). Demonstrates reproducibility promises with detailed prompts, verification methodology, and complete results enabling replication.

Conclusion: The work delivers a practical, well-documented workflow for translating and creating high-performance RL environments with verifiable semantic equivalence and low overhead, supported by cross-backend transfer and a synthesis conduit (TCGJax) that also functions as contamination control for pretraining data.

Abstract: Translating complex reinforcement learning (RL) environments into high-performance implementations has traditionally required months of specialized engineering. We present a reusable recipe - a generic prompt template, hierarchical verification, and iterative agent-assisted repair - that produces semantically equivalent high-performance environments for <$10 in compute cost. We demonstrate three distinct workflows across five environments. Direct translation (no prior performance implementation exists): EmuRust (1.5x PPO speedup via Rust parallelism for a Game Boy emulator) and PokeJAX, the first GPU-parallel Pokemon battle simulator (500M SPS random action, 15.2M SPS PPO; 22,320x over the TypeScript reference). Translation verified against existing performance implementations: throughput parity with MJX (1.04x) and 5x over Brax at matched GPU batch sizes (HalfCheetah JAX); 42x PPO (Puffer Pong). New environment creation: TCGJax, the first deployable JAX Pokemon TCG engine (717K SPS random action, 153K SPS PPO; 6.6x over the Python reference), synthesized from a web-extracted specification. At 200M parameters, the environment overhead drops below 4% of training time. Hierarchical verification (property, interaction, and rollout tests) confirms semantic equivalence for all five environments; cross-backend policy transfer confirms zero sim-to-sim gap for all five environments. TCGJax, synthesized from a private reference absent from public repositories, serves as a contamination control for agent pretraining data concerns. The paper contains sufficient detail - including representative prompts, verification methodology, and complete results - that a coding agent could reproduce the translations directly from the manuscript.

</details>


### [223] [IsoCompute Playbook: Optimally Scaling Sampling Compute for LLM RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12151)
*Zhoujun Cheng,Yutao Xie,Yuxiao Qu,Amrith Setlur,Shibo Hao,Varad Pimpalkhute,Tongtong Liang,Feng Yao,Zhengzhong Liu,Eric Xing,Virginia Smith,Ruslan Salakhutdinov,Zhiting Hu,Taylor Killian,Aviral Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compute-optimal allocation for on-policy RL post-training of LLMs, showing a saturating benefit for parallel rollouts per problem and distinct mechanisms for easy (solution sharpening) vs hard (coverage expansion) problems, with training stability and interference considerations guiding compute-efficient practice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in scaling laws for RL post-training of large language models by providing prescriptive, compute-constrained allocation rules across three resources (parallel rollouts per problem, problems per batch, update steps).

Method: Formulate compute-constrained optimization over three resources for on-policy RL; analyze how the number of parallel rollouts per problem, problems per batch, and update steps interact under compute budgets; examine both easy and hard problems to identify mechanisms (solution sharpening vs coverage expansion) and evaluate interference and stability; validate across base models and data distributions.

Result: The compute-optimal parallel rollouts per problem rise with available compute and then saturate; easy problems improve via solution sharpening, hard problems via coverage expansion; more rollouts reduce cross-problem interference; number of problems per batch mainly affects training stability and is flexible; results generalize across models and data.

Conclusion: RL scaling laws for LLM post-training can be reframed as prescriptive allocation rules, offering practical guidance for compute-efficient, on-policy RL post-training of LLMs.

Abstract: While scaling laws guide compute allocation for LLM pre-training, analogous prescriptions for reinforcement learning (RL) post-training of large language models (LLMs) remain poorly understood. We study the compute-optimal allocation of sampling compute for on-policy RL methods in LLMs, framing scaling as a compute-constrained optimization over three resources: parallel rollouts per problem, number of problems per batch, and number of update steps. We find that the compute-optimal number of parallel rollouts per problem increases predictably with compute budget and then saturates. This trend holds across both easy and hard problems, though driven by different mechanisms: solution sharpening on easy problems and coverage expansion on hard problems. We further show that increasing the number of parallel rollouts mitigates interference across problems, while the number of problems per batch primarily affects training stability and can be chosen within a broad range. Validated across base models and data distributions, our results recast RL scaling laws as prescriptive allocation rules and provide practical guidance for compute-efficient LLM RL post-training.

</details>


### [224] [A Quantitative Characterization of Forgetting in Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12163)
*Krishnakumar Balasubramanian,Shiva Prasad Kasiviswanathan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Forgetting in continual post-training with two-mode mixtures (old/new tasks) is governed by the divergence direction (forward vs reverse KL), the overlap between old and new modes, sampling regime, and past-visibility. Mass forgetting (old weight collapse) occurs under forward-KL; old-component drift arises under reverse-KL and is exponentially suppressed by mode separation and conditioned geometry. Replay interacts differently with the objectives: for forward-KL it must alter the training distribution to shift the population optimum; for reverse-KL it preserves the population objective but mitigates finite-batch starvation via bounded importance weighting. Three near-on-policy methods (SDFT, TTT-Discover, OAPL) retain old mass and exhibit overlap-controlled drift under explicit conditions. Overall, forgetting can be precisely quantified through the interplay of divergence direction, overlap, sampling, and visibility of past behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a principled, quantitative understanding of when and why forgetting occurs during continual post-training of generative models, and to connect this forgetting to the choice of divergence objective, model geometry, data replay, and recently proposed post-training methods.

Method: Two-mode mixture abstraction (old vs new tasks) with equal-covariance Gaussian modes. Analyze forward-KL and reverse-KL objectives on data from the new distribution, derive population optima, and quantify mass forgetting (old weight decay) and drift (old mean shift). Introduce Bhattacharyya coefficient as the key quantity governing overlap-driven misassignment and drift. Extend analysis to replay interactions and to three methods (SDFT, TTT-Discover, OAPL) under the same framework, deriving conditions for mass retention and drift behavior.

Result: Forward-KL objectives trained on new-data drive the old weight to zero (mass forgetting); reverse-KL objectives converge to the target without mass forgetting but cause drift in the old component, scaled by overlap via Bhattacharyya coefficient and decaying exponentially with mode separation; the geometry is locally well-conditioned with exponential convergence. Replay can mitigate or alter these effects: forward-KL requires replay to modify the training distribution to shift population optima, whereas reverse-KLReplay does not alter the population objective but provides bounded importance weighting to prevent finite-batch starvation. For SDFT, TTT-Discover, and OAPL, explicit conditions are given under which old mass is retained and drift is overlap-controlled. Overall, forgetting can be precisely quantified via divergence direction, overlap, sampling regime, and visibility of past behavior.

Conclusion: Forgetting in continual post-training is not binary but a tractable phenomenon: its presence and magnitude depend on divergence direction, mode overlap, replay strategy, and visibility of past behavior. The two-mode abstraction yields explicit, testable predictions about when and how mass forgets and how drift emerges, with practical guidance for choosing post-training methods and replay strategies.

Abstract: Continual post-training of generative models is widely used, yet a principled understanding of when and why forgetting occurs remains limited. We develop theoretical results under a two-mode mixture abstraction (representing old and new tasks), proposed by Chen et al. (2025) (arXiv:2510.18874), and formalize forgetting in two forms: (i) mass forgetting, where the old mixture weight collapses to zero, and (ii) old-component drift, where an already-correct old component shifts during training. For equal-covariance Gaussian modes, we prove that forward-KL objectives trained on data from the new distribution drive the old weight to zero, while reverse-KL objectives converge to the true target (thereby avoiding mass forgetting) and perturb the old mean only through overlap-gated misassignment probabilities controlled by the Bhattacharyya coefficient, yielding drift that decays exponentially with mode separation and a locally well-conditioned geometry with exponential convergence. We further quantify how replay interacts with these objectives. For forward-KL, replay must modify the training distribution to change the population optimum; for reverse-KL, replay leaves the population objective unchanged but prevents finite-batch old-mode starvation through bounded importance weighting. Finally, we analyze three recently proposed near-on-policy post-training methods, SDFT (arxiv:2601.19897), TTT-Discover (arxiv:2601.16175), and OAPL (arxiv:2602.19362), via the same lens and derive explicit conditions under which each retains old mass and exhibits overlap-controlled drift. Overall, our results show that forgetting can by precisely quantified based on the interaction between divergence direction, geometric behavioral overlap, sampling regime, and the visibility of past behavior during training.

</details>


### [225] [Neural Thickets: Diverse Task Experts Are Dense Around Pretrained Weights](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12228)
*Yulu Gan,Phillip Isola*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A distributional view of pretraining: large models host dense neighborhoods of task-experts near pretrained weights, enabling a simple, parallel post-training method that samples perturbations and ensembles top-K proposals, competitive with established methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reinterpret pretraining as a distribution over parameter vectors, highlighting a shift from a single starting point to abundant task-experts in large models, and to exploit this for a simple post-training strategy.

Method: Sample N random parameter perturbations around pretrained weights, select the top K by some criterion, and ensemble their predictions via majority vote; evaluates against PPO, GRPO, and ES on contemporary large-scale models.

Result: The simple perturbation-ensemble method achieves performance competitive with standard post-training methods on large-scale models.

Conclusion: Large pretraining creates a dense landscape of task-experts near the pretrained weights, enabling effective, scalable post-training via parallel perturbation and ensemble, potentially reducing reliance on gradient-based fine-tuning.

Abstract: Pretraining produces a learned parameter vector that is typically treated as a starting point for further iterative adaptation. In this work, we instead view the outcome of pretraining as a distribution over parameter vectors, whose support already contains task-specific experts. We show that in small models such expert solutions occupy a negligible fraction of the volume of this distribution, making their discovery reliant on structured optimization methods such as gradient descent. In contrast, in large, well-pretrained models the density of task-experts increases dramatically, so that diverse, task-improving specialists populate a substantial fraction of the neighborhood around the pretrained weights. Motivated by this perspective, we explore a simple, fully parallel post-training method that samples $N$ parameter perturbations at random, selects the top $K$, and ensembles predictions via majority vote. Despite its simplicity, this approach is competitive with standard post-training methods such as PPO, GRPO, and ES for contemporary large-scale models.

</details>


### [226] [Security Considerations for Artificial Intelligence Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12230)
*Ninghui Li,Kaiyuan Zhang,Kyle Polley,Jerry Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The abstract surveys security of frontier AI agents, identifying attack surfaces, evaluating layered defenses, and outlining standards gaps and research needs aligned with NIST risk management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and mitigate confidentiality, integrity, and availability risks in agent architectures that blur code–data boundaries and execution control, drawing on Perplexity’s experience with real-world deployment of general-purpose agentic systems.

Method: Observational analysis from Perplexity’s deployments. It maps principal attack surfaces across tools, connectors, hosting boundaries, and multi-agent coordination, focusing on indirect prompt injection, confused-deputy behavior, and cascading failures in long-running workflows. It then assesses current defenses as a layered stack (input-level and model-level mitigations, sandboxed execution, deterministic policy enforcement) and identifies standards/gaps.

Result: Provides observations and recommendations on the security of frontier AI agents, emphasizing the identified attack surfaces and the effectiveness of a layered defense approach, along with actionable guidance and areas for standardization and further research.

Conclusion: Calls for adaptive security benchmarks, policy models for delegation and privilege control, and secure multi-agent system design guidance aligned with NIST risk management principles.

Abstract: This article, a lightly adapted version of Perplexity's response to NIST/CAISI Request for Information 2025-0035, details our observations and recommendations concerning the security of frontier AI agents. These insights are informed by Perplexity's experience operating general-purpose agentic systems used by millions of users and thousands of enterprises in both controlled and open-world environments. Agent architectures change core assumptions around code-data separation, authority boundaries, and execution predictability, creating new confidentiality, integrity, and availability failure modes. We map principal attack surfaces across tools, connectors, hosting boundaries, and multi-agent coordination, with particular emphasis on indirect prompt injection, confused-deputy behavior, and cascading failures in long-running workflows. We then assess current defenses as a layered stack: input-level and model-level mitigations, sandboxed execution, and deterministic policy enforcement for high-consequence actions. Finally, we identify standards and research gaps, including adaptive security benchmarks, policy models for delegation and privilege control, and guidance for secure multi-agent system design aligned with NIST risk management principles.

</details>


### [227] [Temporal Straightening for Latent Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12231)
*Ying Wang,Oumayma Bounou,Gaoyue Zhou,Randall Balestriero,Tim G. J. Rudner,Yann LeCun,Mengye Ren*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Temporal straightening learns latent representations with locally straight trajectories to improve latent planning, improving geodesic-proxy distance and planning stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pretrained encoders capture semantic features not tailored to planning; misalignment harms planning performance. Inspired by perceptual straightening, the authors aim to align latent geometry with planning needs.

Method: Jointly train an encoder and a predictor with a curvature regularizer that encourages locally straightened latent trajectories, reducing curvature so Euclidean distance better approximates geodesic distance and improves planning conditioning.

Result: Empirical evidence shows reduced curvature leads to more stable gradient-based planning and higher success rates on various goal-reaching tasks.

Conclusion: Temporal straightening is a viable strategy to tailor representations for latent planning, enhancing planning stability and success by shaping latent geometry toward planning objectives.

Abstract: Learning good representations is essential for latent planning with world models. While pretrained visual encoders produce strong semantic visual features, they are not tailored to planning and contain information irrelevant -- or even detrimental -- to planning. Inspired by the perceptual straightening hypothesis in human visual processing, we introduce temporal straightening to improve representation learning for latent planning. Using a curvature regularizer that encourages locally straightened latent trajectories, we jointly learn an encoder and a predictor. We show that reducing curvature this way makes the Euclidean distance in latent space a better proxy for the geodesic distance and improves the conditioning of the planning objective. We demonstrate empirically that temporal straightening makes gradient-based planning more stable and yields significantly higher success rates across a suite of goal-reaching tasks.

</details>


### [228] [STAMP: Selective Task-Aware Mechanism for Text Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12237)
*Fengwei Tian,Payel Bhattacharjee,Heidi Hanson,Geoffrey D. Rubin,Joseph Y. Lo,Ravi Tandon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STAMP is a token-level privacy framework for text privatization that allocates per-token privacy budgets based on downstream task importance and privacy sensitivity; it introduces the polar mechanism to perturb only token embedding directions on the unit sphere (preserving magnitude) and decodes via cosine nearest-neighbor search, enabling semantic-preserving, task-aware noise; experiments on SQuAD, Yelp, and AG News show superior privacy-utility trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve privacy-utility trade-offs in text privatization by accounting for token-level variation in task relevance and privacy sensitivity, and by using geometry-aware perturbations that preserve semantic structure.

Method: Per-token privacy budgets are allocated by jointly considering token importance (via task- or query-specific representations) and token privacy sensitivity; perturb individual token embeddings with the polar mechanism (direction perturbation on the unit sphere while magnitude remains intact); decoding via cosine nearest-neighbor search; evaluation under varying per-token privacy budgets, with normalization.

Result: Empirical results on SQuAD, Yelp, and AG News show STAMP with the normalized polar mechanism consistently achieving superior privacy-utility trade-offs across different per-token privacy budgets.

Conclusion: Token-aware privatization with geometry-aligned perturbations improves downstream utility while maintaining privacy by enabling fine-grained control over per-token privacy budgets and preserving semantic neighborhoods in embedding space.

Abstract: We present STAMP (Selective Task-Aware Mechanism for Text Privacy), a new framework for task-aware text privatization that achieves an improved privacy-utility trade-off. STAMP selectively allocates privacy budgets across tokens by jointly considering (i) each token's importance to the downstream task (as measured via a task- or query-specific representation), and (ii) its privacy sensitivity (e.g., names, dates, identifiers). This token-level partitioning enables fine-grained, group-wise control over the level of noise applied to different parts of the input, balancing privacy protection with task relevance. To privatize individual token embeddings, we introduce the polar mechanism, which perturbs only the direction of embeddings on the unit sphere while preserving their magnitude. Decoding is performed via cosine nearest-neighbor search, aligning the perturbation geometry with the decoding geometry. Unlike isotropic noise mechanisms, the polar mechanism maintains semantic neighborhoods in the embedding space and better preserves downstream utility. Experimental evaluations on SQuAD, Yelp, and AG News datasets demonstrate that STAMP, when combined with the normalized polar mechanism, consistently achieves superior privacy-utility trade-offs across varying per-token privacy budgets.

</details>


### [229] [Separable neural architectures as a primitive for unified predictive and generative intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12244)
*Reza T. Batley,Apurba Sarker,Rajib Mostakim,Andrew Klichine,Sourav Saha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Separable Neural Architecture (SNA) to factorize high‑dimensional mappings into low‑arity components via separability constraints, unifying additive, quadratic, and tensor‑decomposed models for domain‑agnostic predictive and generative tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many intelligent systems exhibit structure that remains untapped by monolithic networks. Enforcing separability provides a scalable inductive bias, reveals a coordinate‑level analogy between chaotic spatiotemporal dynamics and linguistic autoregression, and enables distributional modelling while reducing nonphysical drift.

Method: Define a representational class that unifies additive, quadratic, and tensor‑decomposed neural models; constrain interaction order and tensor rank to impose separability; treat continuous physical states as smooth separable embeddings; demonstrate versatility across autonomous navigation, inverse multifunctional microstructure generation, turbulent flow distributional modelling, and neural language modelling.

Result: Demonstrates that separable architectures offer a domain‑agnostic, compositional primitive with a flexible bias that supports both deterministic and distributional representations and improves predictive/generative capabilities across the tested domains (navigation, materials design, fluid dynamics, language).

Conclusion: Separable Neural Architecture provides a unifying, domain‑agnostic framework for predictive and generative intelligence by capitalising on separable representations to factorise complex mappings into simpler, low‑arity components; this bridges deterministic operators with distributional modelling across diverse domains.

Abstract: Intelligent systems across physics, language and perception often exhibit factorisable structure, yet are typically modelled by monolithic neural architectures that do not explicitly exploit this structure. The separable neural architecture (SNA) addresses this by formalising a representational class that unifies additive, quadratic and tensor-decomposed neural models. By constraining interaction order and tensor rank, SNAs impose a structural inductive bias that factorises high-dimensional mappings into low-arity components. Separability need not be a property of the system itself: it often emerges in the coordinates or representations through which the system is expressed. Crucially, this coordinate-aware formulation reveals a structural analogy between chaotic spatiotemporal dynamics and linguistic autoregression. By treating continuous physical states as smooth, separable embeddings, SNAs enable distributional modelling of chaotic systems. This approach mitigates the nonphysical drift characteristics of deterministic operators whilst remaining applicable to discrete sequences. The compositional versatility of this approach is demonstrated across four domains: autonomous waypoint navigation via reinforcement learning, inverse generation of multifunctional microstructures, distributional modelling of turbulent flow and neural language modelling. These results establish the separable neural architecture as a domain-agnostic primitive for predictive and generative intelligence, capable of unifying both deterministic and distributional representations.

</details>


### [230] [Matching Features, Not Tokens: Energy-Based Fine-Tuning of Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12248)
*Samy Jelassi,Mujin Kwun,Rosie Zhao,Yuanzhi Li,Nicolo Fusi,Yilun Du,Sham M. Kakade,Carles Domingo-Enrich*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EBFT is a principled, efficient fine-tuning framework for language models that optimizes a feature-matching, energy-based objective over sequence-level completions using strided block-parallel rollouts and an on-policy gradient; it matches RLVR and surpasses SFT in downstream accuracy with lower validation cross-entropy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard CE training optimizes next-token prediction with teacher forcing, not sequence-level rollout behavior. There is a need for dense, semantic supervision at the sequence level without task-specific verifiers or preference models, and a principled energy-based/KL-regularized perspective.

Method: Introduce a feature-matching objective over sequence-level statistics of the completion distribution. EBFT employs strided block-parallel sampling to generate multiple rollouts from nested prefixes concurrently, batches feature extraction over rollouts, and uses embeddings to perform an on-policy policy-gradient update. The approach is connected theoretically to KL-regularized feature matching and energy-based modeling.

Result: Empirically, across Q&A coding, unstructured coding, and translation tasks, EBFT matches RLVR and outperforms SFT on downstream accuracy, while achieving a lower validation cross-entropy than both RLVR and SFT.

Conclusion: EBFT provides dense sequence-level supervision via an energy-based, KL-regularized feature-matching objective. Its efficient rollout sampling and on-policy updates yield strong, cross-task gains, offering a viable alternative to RL-based and standard supervised fine-tuning.

Abstract: Cross-entropy (CE) training provides dense and scalable supervision for language models, but it optimizes next-token prediction under teacher forcing rather than sequence-level behavior under model rollouts. We introduce a feature-matching objective for language-model fine-tuning that targets sequence-level statistics of the completion distribution, providing dense semantic feedback without requiring a task-specific verifier or preference model. To optimize this objective efficiently, we propose energy-based fine-tuning (EBFT), which uses strided block-parallel sampling to generate multiple rollouts from nested prefixes concurrently, batches feature extraction over these rollouts, and uses the resulting embeddings to perform an on-policy policy-gradient update. We present a theoretical perspective connecting EBFT to KL-regularized feature-matching and energy-based modeling. Empirically, across Q&A coding, unstructured coding, and translation, EBFT matches RLVR and outperforms SFT on downstream accuracy while achieving a lower validation cross-entropy than both methods.

</details>


### [231] [The Latent Color Subspace: Emergent Order in High-Dimensional Chaos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12261)
*Mateusz Pach,Jessica Bader,Quentin Bouniot,Serge Belongie,Zeynep Akata*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Identifies a Latent Color Subspace (LCS) in the FLUX VAE latent space that maps to Hue, Saturation, and Lightness, enabling training-free, latent-space color control for text-to-image generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how semantic color information is encoded in FLUX's latent space to achieve fine-grained, training-free color control.

Method: Latent-space analysis of FLUX's VAE to uncover an LCS aligned with Hue, Saturation, and Lightness; validate by predicting and manipulating color via closed-form latent-space operations; no additional training required.

Result: Demonstrates that the LCS can predict color and enables explicit, training-free color control; provides a training-free manipulation method and released code.

Conclusion: LCS offers an interpretable, intuitive means to control color in FLUX latent space, advancing fine-grained control in text-to-image generation without extra training.

Abstract: Text-to-image generation models have advanced rapidly, yet achieving fine-grained control over generated images remains difficult, largely due to limited understanding of how semantic information is encoded. We develop an interpretation of the color representation in the Variational Autoencoder latent space of FLUX.1 [Dev], revealing a structure reflecting Hue, Saturation, and Lightness. We verify our Latent Color Subspace (LCS) interpretation by demonstrating that it can both predict and explicitly control color, introducing a fully training-free method in FLUX based solely on closed-form latent-space manipulation. Code is available at https://github.com/ExplainableML/LCS.

</details>


<div id='cs.CG'></div>

# cs.CG [[Back]](#toc)

### [232] [Approximate Dynamic Nearest Neighbor Searching in a Polygonal Domain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11775)
*Joost van der Laan,Frank Staals,Lorenzo Theunissen*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Dynamic approximate nearest neighbor in a 2D polygonal domain with n-vertex boundary; supports a dynamic set of m sites with insertions/deletions. Achieves (1+ε)-approximation with space and time tradeoffs: Space for distance oracle: O(n/ε log n); combined NN structure: O((n+m)/ε log n + (m/ε) log m). Query/update times: roughly O((1/ε^2) log n + (1/ε) log n log m + (1/ε) log^2 m).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficiently solve NN queries in geometric settings where exact geodesic distances are expensive and the domain is complex (polygonal with n vertices). Dynamic updates required for the set of sites; approximations reduce space and time.

Method: First, build an ε-approximate distance oracle for pairs (q,s) in the polygonal domain with space O(n/ε log n) and query time O((1/ε^2) log n). Then integrate this oracle with a dynamic data structure for the site set S to report an ε-approximate nearest site to a query point q, achieving combined space O((n+m)/ε log n + (m/ε) log m) and update/query times as stated.

Result: The authors obtain a data structure that reports a site s ∈ S within (1+ε) of the true nearest neighbor of q, with the stated space and time bounds. Updates are amortized with the described complexity.

Conclusion: The work provides efficient dynamic, approximate NN search in polygonal domains, leveraging an ε-distance oracle and a merged data structure to balance space and time. It also mentions extension to approximate 2-point shortest path queries, indicating broader applicability in computational geometry.

Abstract: We present efficient data structures for approximate nearest neighbor searching and approximate 2-point shortest path queries in a two-dimensional polygonal domain $P$ with $n$ vertices. Our goal is to store a dynamic set of $m$ point sites $S$ in $P$ so that we can efficiently find a site $s \in S$ closest to an arbitrary query point $q$. We will allow both insertions and deletions in the set of sites $S$. However, as even just computing the distance between an arbitrary pair of points $q,s \in P$ requires a substantial amount of space, we allow for approximating the distances. Given a parameter $\varepsilon > 0$, we build an $O(\frac{n}{\varepsilon}\log n)$ space data structure that can compute a $1+\varepsilon$-approximation of the distance between $q$ and $s$ in $O(\frac{1}{\varepsilon^2}\log n)$ time. Building on this, we then obtain an $O(\frac{n+m}{\varepsilon}\log n + \frac{m}{\varepsilon}\log m)$ space data structure that allows us to report a site $s \in S$ so that the distance between query point $q$ and $s$ is at most $(1+\varepsilon)$-times the distance between $q$ and its true nearest neighbor in $O(\frac{1}{\varepsilon^2}\log n + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\log n \log m + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\log^2 m)$ time. Our data structure supports updates in $O(\frac{1}{\varepsilon^2}\log n + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\log n \log m + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\log^2 m)$ amortized time.

</details>


### [233] [Fast and exact visibility on digitized shapes and application to saliency-aware normal estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11851)
*Romain Negro,Jacques-Olivier Lachaud*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Proposes an interval-based representation of digital sets to efficiently compute the full visibility graph among lattice points and uses this visibility information to estimate the normal vector field of a digital shape with accuracy and convergence, preserving sharp features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visibility computation on geometric objects is computationally intensive due to pairwise visibility checks. An efficient, robust method is sought to obtain complete visibility graphs and to derive stable normal estimates that respect salient features of digital shapes.

Method: Adopts a representation of digital sets based on lists of integral intervals to efficiently enumerate visibility between lattice points, enabling a complete visibility graph; uses the resulting visibility information to estimate the normal vector field of the digital shape with accuracy and convergence while preserving sharp features.

Result: Demonstrates efficient computation of the complete visibility graph for lattice points within a digital shape and shows that visibility-based normal estimation is accurate and convergent, preserving salient and sharp shape features.

Conclusion: The proposed interval-based visibility framework provides an effective tool for visibility analysis on digital shapes and for robust normal vector estimation, with potential benefits for shape analysis and rendering tasks.

Abstract: Computing visibility on a geometric object requires heavy computations since it requires to identify pairs of points that are visible to each other, i.e. there is a straight segment joining them that stays in the close vicinity of the object boundary. We propose to exploit a specic representation of digital sets based on lists of integral intervals in order to compute eciently the complete visibility graph between lattice points of the digital shape. As a quite direct application, we show then how we can use visibility to estimate the normal vector eld of a digital shape in an accurate and convergent manner while staying aware of the salient and sharp features of the shape.

</details>


### [234] [On strictly output sensitive color frequency reporting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11898)
*Erwin Glazenburg,Frank Staals*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Introduces space–time trade-offs for color frequency reporting on colored point sets under axis-aligned and dominance queries, achieving strictly output-sensitive query times with tunable space, near-optimal lower bounds, plus space-reduction techniques and a dominance-query algorithm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Color frequency reporting asks to report all colors appearing in a query region together with their frequencies. The goal is strictly output-sensitive query time (dependent on k, the number of colors found) and scalable space, for natural query families like axis-aligned boxes and dominance ranges.

Method: 1) A parameterized 2D data structure with space O(n s log_s n) and query time O(log n + k log_s n) for any s ∈ {2,...,n}. 2) A lower bound in the arithmetic model showing with O(m) space one cannot achieve sub-Ω(φ log(n/φ)/log(m/n)) query time for the weighted version (φ colors), implying near-optimality of the first result. 3) Extension to higher dimensions. 4) A transformation that reduces space to O(n (s φ)^ε log_s n). 5) An O(n^{1+ε} + m log n + K)-time algorithm for m dominance queries in R^2 with total output K and linear working space.

Result: The proposed data structures deliver strictly output-sensitive query performance with tunable space, and the lower bound establishes near-optimality under space constraints. The techniques extend to higher dimensions, and a space-reducing transformation is provided. The dominance-query algorithm achieves near-linear time in the total output size with linear working space.

Conclusion: The work advances color frequency reporting by presenting tunable, strictly output-sensitive structures, matching near-optimal lower bounds and offering practical extensions to higher dimensions and dominance queries, along with a space-reduction technique and a scalable dominance-query algorithm.

Abstract: Given a set of $n$ colored points $P \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ we wish to store $P$ such that, given some query region $Q$, we can efficiently report the colors of the points appearing in the query region, along with their frequencies. This is the \emph{color frequency reporting} problem. We study the case where query regions $Q$ are axis-aligned boxes or dominance ranges. If $Q$ contains $k$ colors, the main goal is to achieve ``strictly output sensitive'' query time $O(f(n) + k)$. Firstly, we show that, for every $s \in \{ 2, \dots, n \}$, there exists a simple $O(ns\log_s n)$ size data structure for points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ that allows frequency reporting queries in $O(\log n + k\log_s n)$ time. Secondly, we give a lower bound for the weighted version of the problem in the arithmetic model of computation, proving that with $O(m)$ space one can not achieve query times better than $Ω\left(φ\frac{\log (n / φ)}{\log (m / n)}\right)$, where $φ$ is the number of possible colors. This means that our data structure is near-optimal. We extend these results to higher dimensions as well. Thirdly, we present a transformation that allows us to reduce the space usage of the aforementioned datastructure to $O(n(s φ)^\varepsilon \log_s n)$. Finally, we give an $O(n^{1+\varepsilon} + m \log n + K)$-time algorithm that can answer $m$ dominance queries $\mathbb{R}^2$ with total output complexity $K$, while using only linear working space.

</details>


### [235] [Space-Efficient Approximate Spherical Range Counting in High Dimensions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12106)
*Andreas Kalavas,Ioannis Psarros*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Subquadratic/near-linear space, approximate high-dimensional range searching with time depending on t_q; first to achieve sublinear query time for any sublinear t_q and near-linear space; includes query-driven preprocessing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome curse of dimensionality in range searching by relaxing exactness to (1+epsilon)-approximate radius; achieve better space/time trade-offs than exact methods.

Method: Construct a data structure using approximate range counting with (1+epsilon) factor; theory yields space near-linear in |P| and d, with query time n^{1-Θ(ε^4/ log(1/ε))} + t_q^{Θ(ε^2)} n^{1-Θ(ε^2)}; defines ambiguity zone and leverages t_q; includes a query-driven preprocessing variant.

Result: Achieves near-linear space and sublinear query time for sublinear t_q; first to obtain subquadratic/near-linear space with sublinear query time for all ε>0; provides worst-case bounds and a distribution-adapted variant.

Conclusion: Advances high-dimensional approximate range searching by balancing space and query time; opens avenues for practical implementations and further optimization of constants; suggests relying on ambiguity-zone-aware strategies and query distribution profiling.
