Abstract: We study the following range searching problem in high-dimensional Euclidean spaces: given a finite set $P\subset \mathbb{R}^d$, where each $p\in P$ is assigned a weight $w_p$, and radius $r>0$, we need to preprocess $P$ into a data structure such that when a new query point $q\in \mathbb{R}^d$ arrives, the data structure reports the cumulative weight of points of $P$ within Euclidean distance $r$ from $q$. Solving the problem exactly seems to require space usage that is exponential to the dimension, a phenomenon known as the curse of dimensionality. Thus, we focus on approximate solutions where points up to $(1+\varepsilon)r$ away from $q$ may be taken into account, where $\varepsilon>0$ is an input parameter known during preprocessing. We build a data structure with near-linear space usage, and query time in $n^{1-Θ(\varepsilon^4/\log(1/\varepsilon))}+t_q^{\varrho}\cdot n^{1-\varrho}$, for some $\varrho=Θ(\varepsilon^2)$, where $t_q$ is the number of points of $P$ in the ambiguity zone, i.e., at distance between $r$ and $(1+\varepsilon)r$ from the query $q$. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first data structure with efficient space usage (subquadratic or near-linear for any $\varepsilon>0$) and query time that remains sublinear for any sublinear $t_q$. We supplement our worst-case bounds with a query-driven preprocessing algorithm to build data structures that are well-adapted to the query distribution.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [236] [TinyNav: End-to-End TinyML for Real-Time Autonomous Navigation on Microcontrollers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11071)
*Pooria Roy,Nourhan Jadallah. Tomer Lapid,Shahzaib Ahmad,Armita Afroushe,Mete Bayrak*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TinyNav is a 23k-parameter end-to-end TinyML navigation stack that runs real-time on an ESP32 using a quantized 2D CNN over a 20-frame depth window to output steering and throttle with 30 ms latency; no 3D convs or RNNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable affordable, energy-efficient autonomous navigation on microcontrollers; overcome power and compute constraints that limit deep learning-based navigation on edge devices.

Method: Train and quantize a 2D CNN; feed a 20-frame depth data window; perform end-to-end inference to predict steering and throttle; implement on ESP32; avoid 3D convolutions and recurrent layers; measure latency (30 ms) and use correlation analysis and Grad-CAM to validate spatial awareness/obstacle avoidance.

Result: Achieves real-time control with 30 ms latency; 23k parameters; demonstrates consistent spatial awareness and obstacle avoidance using Grad-CAM and correlation analysis; effective deployment on constrained edge device reducing reliance on external compute.

Conclusion: Shows feasibility of edge-deployed autonomous control on microcontrollers; potential for low-cost, responsive robotics; further work may explore sensor fusion, robustness, and generalization.

Abstract: Autonomous navigation typically relies on power-intensive processors, limiting accessibility in low-cost robotics. Although microcontrollers offer a resource-efficient alternative, they impose strict constraints on model complexity. We present TinyNav, an end-to-end TinyML system for real-time autonomous navigation on an ESP32 microcontroller. A custom-trained, quantized 2D convolutional neural network processes a 20-frame sliding window of depth data to predict steering and throttle commands. By avoiding 3D convolutions and recurrent layers, the 23k-parameter model achieves 30 ms inference latency. Correlation analysis and Grad-CAM validation indicate consistent spatial awareness and obstacle avoidance behavior. TinyNav demonstrates that responsive autonomous control can be deployed directly on highly constrained edge devices, reducing reliance on external compute resources.

</details>


### [237] [OA-NBV: Occlusion-Aware Next-Best-View Planning for Human-Centered Active Perception on Mobile Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11072)
*Boxun Hu,Chang Chang,Jiawei Ge,Man Namgung,Xiaomin Lin,Axel Krieger,Tinoosh Mohsenin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OA-NBV proposes occlusion-aware Next-Best-View planning for mobile robots to obtain complete views of occluded humans; uses a target-centric visibility model; achieves >90% success and large gains in observed area and keypoints; acts as a drop-in module for human-centered tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NBV methods often optimize generic exploration or long-horizon coverage and do not target obtaining a single usable observation of a partially occluded human under real motion constraints; occlusions and motion limits hinder perception in cluttered environments.

Method: An occlusion-aware NBV pipeline (OA-NBV) that selects the next traversable viewpoint to obtain a more complete view of an occluded human. It uses a target-centric visibility model that accounts for occlusion, target scale, and target completeness, while restricting candidates to feasible robot poses; integrates perception and motion planning.

Result: OA-NBV achieves over 90% success rate in both simulation and real-world trials; baseline NBV methods degrade under occlusion. It improves observation quality, increasing normalized target area by at least 81% and keypoint visibility by at least 58% compared to the strongest baseline; serves as a drop-in view-selection module for diverse downstream tasks.

Conclusion: OA-NBV effectively enables occlusion-aware, perception-driven NBV for mobile robots, improving both success and observation quality of occluded human targets and enabling integration with downstream, human-centered tasks.

Abstract: We naturally step sideways or lean to see around the obstacle when our view is blocked, and recover a more informative observation. Enabling robots to make the same kind of viewpoint choice is critical for human-centered operations, including search, triage, and disaster response, where cluttered environments and partial visibility frequently degrade downstream perception. However, many Next-Best-View (NBV) methods primarily optimize generic exploration or long-horizon coverage, and do not explicitly target the immediate goal of obtaining a single usable observation of a partially occluded person under real motion constraints. We present Occlusion-Aware Next-Best-View Planning for Human-Centered Active Perception on Mobile Robots (OA-NBV), an occlusion-aware NBV pipeline that autonomously selects the next traversable viewpoint to obtain a more complete view of an occluded human. OA-NBV integrates perception and motion planning by scoring candidate viewpoints using a target-centric visibility model that accounts for occlusion, target scale, and target completeness, while restricting candidates to feasible robot poses. OA-NBV achieves over 90% success rate in both simulation and real-world trials, while baseline NBV methods degrade sharply under occlusion. Beyond success rate, OA-NBV improves observation quality: compared to the strongest baseline, it increases normalized target area by at least 81% and keypoint visibility by at least 58% across settings, making it a drop-in view-selection module for diverse human-centered downstream tasks.

</details>


### [238] [DRAFTO: Decoupled Reduced-space and Adaptive Feasibility-repair Trajectory Optimization for Robotic Manipulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11074)
*Yichang Feng,Xiao Liang,Minghui Zheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces DRAFTO, a decoupled reduced-space trajectory optimization method blending GN descent with constrained QP for feasibility, showing efficient, reliable performance across optimization- and sampling-based planners and in complex manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve trajectory optimization by ensuring smoothness, safety, and feasibility while reducing computation, especially for high-dimensional manipulation tasks.

Method: Decouples optimization into reduced-space Gauss-Newton descent for main iterations and a constrained quadratic program for initialization and terminal feasibility repair; uses a two-phase, non-monotone acceptance rule with hinge-squared penalties for inequality constraints; optimizes basis-function coefficients that parameterize trajectories.

Result: Benchmarked against optimization-based planners (CHOMP, TrajOpt, GPMP2, FACTO) and sampling-based planners (RRT-Connect, RRT*, PRM); demonstrated high efficiency and reliability across diverse tasks, including a drawer-object grabbing scenario; supplemental video provided.

Conclusion: The proposed DRAFTO framework effectively combines fast reduced-space optimization with feasibility repair to achieve robust, efficient trajectory optimization across varied scenarios, with potential applicability to complex manipulation tasks.

Abstract: This paper introduces a new algorithm for trajectory optimization, Decoupled Reduced-space and Adaptive Feasibility-repair Trajectory Optimization (DRAFTO). It first constructs a constrained objective that accounts for smoothness, safety, joint limits, and task requirements. Then, it optimizes the coefficients, which are the coordinates of a set of basis functions for trajectory parameterization. To reduce the number of repeated constrained optimizations while handling joint-limit feasibility, the optimization is decoupled into a reduced-space Gauss-Newton (GN) descent for the main iterations and constrained quadratic programming for initialization and terminal feasibility repair. The two-phase acceptance rule with a non-monotone policy is applied to the GN model, which uses a hinge-squared penalty for inequality constraints, to ensure globalizability. The results of our benchmark tests against optimization-based planners, such as CHOMP, TrajOpt, GPMP2, and FACTO, and sampling-based planners, such as RRT-Connect, RRT*, and PRM, validate the high efficiency and reliability across diverse scenarios and tasks. The experiment involving grabbing an object from a drawer further demonstrates the potential for implementation in complex manipulation tasks. The supplemental video is available at https://youtu.be/XisFI37YyTQ.

</details>


### [239] [TATIC: Task-Aware Temporal Learning for Human Intent Inference from Physical Corrections in Human-Robot Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11077)
*Jiurun Song,Xiao Liang,Minghui Zheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified, torque-based HRC framework (TATIC) that combines torque-based contact force estimation with a task-aware Temporal Convolutional Network to infer discrete task intent and continuous motion parameters from brief physical corrections; includes task-aligned feature canonicalization and an intent-driven adaptation mechanism; achieves Macro-F1 0.904 for intent recognition and validates on hardware (collaborative disassembly).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap between vision/language foundation-model approaches and traditional physical HRI in interpreting brief physical corrections for task-level semantics. A need exists for online, robust interpretation of human intent from physical feedback to enable task-level adaptation in dynamic HRC.

Method: Proposes TATIC: (i) torque-based contact force estimation to extract physical feedback; (ii) a task-aware Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) to jointly infer discrete task-level intent and estimate continuous motion-level parameters; (iii) task-aligned feature canonicalization for cross-layout robustness; (iv) an intent-driven adaptation scheme translating inferred intent into robot motion adjustments.

Result: Macro-F1 score of 0.904 in intent recognition. Hardware validation demonstrated in a collaborative disassembly scenario. An experiment video is provided.

Conclusion: The approach enables robust interpretation of brief physical corrections into task-level intent and continuous motion parameters, enabling online adaptation in HRC with good generalization across layouts and practical hardware demonstration.

Abstract: In human-robot collaboration (HRC), robots must adapt online to dynamic task constraints and evolving human intent. While physical corrections provide a natural, low-latency channel for operators to convey motion-level adjustments, extracting task-level semantic intent from such brief interactions remains challenging. Existing foundation-model-based approaches primarily rely on vision and language inputs and lack mechanisms to interpret physical feedback. Meanwhile, traditional physical human-robot interaction (pHRI) methods leverage physical corrections for trajectory guidance but struggle to infer task-level semantics. To bridge this gap, we propose TATIC, a unified framework that utilizes torque-based contact force estimation and a task-aware Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) to jointly infer discrete task-level intent and estimate continuous motion-level parameters from brief physical corrections. Task-aligned feature canonicalization ensures robust generalization across diverse layouts, while an intent-driven adaptation scheme translates inferred human intent into robot motion adaptations. Experiments achieve a 0.904 Macro-F1 score in intent recognition and demonstrate successful hardware validation in collaborative disassembly (see experimental video at https://youtu.be/xF8A52qwEc8).

</details>


### [240] [SELF-VLA: A Skill Enhanced Agentic Vision-Language-Action Framework for Contact-Rich Disassembly](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11080)
*Chang Liu,Sibo Tian,Xiao Liang,Minghui Zheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SELF-VLA: an agentic VLA framework that embeds explicit disassembly skills into vision-language-action models, achieving superior performance over end-to-end VLA baselines on two contact-rich disassembly tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disassembly robotics currently relies on task- and component-specific subtasks with heavy data preparation, training, and limited generalization, hindering deployment. Foundation-model-based VLA models show promise but struggle with long-horizon, contact-rich, sequential manipulation needed for disassembly.

Method: Proposes SELF-VLA, an agentic VLA framework that integrates explicit disassembly skills into the VLA architecture to support sequential, precise manipulation in disassembly tasks.

Result: Experimental studies show that SELF-VLA significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art end-to-end VLA models on two contact-rich disassembly tasks; a video demonstration is provided.

Conclusion: SELF-VLA demonstrates effectiveness in challenging disassembly scenarios and offers a path toward more generalizable, automation-ready robotic disassembly.

Abstract: Disassembly automation has long been pursued to address the growing demand for efficient and proper recovery of valuable components from the end-of-life (EoL) electronic products. Existing approaches have demonstrated promising and regimented performance by decomposing the disassembly process into different subtasks. However, each subtask typically requires extensive data preparation, model training, and system management. Moreover, these approaches are often task- and component-specific, making them poorly suited to handle the variability and uncertainty of EoL products and limiting their generalization capabilities. All these factors restrict the practical deployment of current robotic disassembly systems and leave them highly reliant on human labor. With the recent development of foundation models in robotics, vision-language-action (VLA) models have shown impressive performance on standard robotic manipulation tasks, but their applicability to complex, contact-rich, and long-horizon industrial practices like disassembly, which requires sequential and precise manipulation, remains limited. To address this challenge, we propose SELF-VLA, an agentic VLA framework that integrates explicit disassembly skills. Experimental studies demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art end-to-end VLA models on two contact-rich disassembly tasks. The video illustration can be found via https://zh.engr.tamu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/310/2026/03/IROS-VLA-Video.mp4.

</details>


### [241] [Edge-Assisted Multi-Robot Visual-Inertial SLAM with Efficient Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11085)
*Xin Liu,Shuhuan Wen,Jing Zhao,Tony Z. Qiu,Hong Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a cloud-edge-robot centralized SLAM framework with lightweight optical-flow tracking and lossless feature transmission to reduce bandwidth while maintaining accuracy, validated on EuRoC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate bandwidth and latency bottlenecks in cloud-edge multi-robot SLAM while keeping real-time performance and accuracy with limited onboard computation.

Method: Introduce a lightweight SLAM method using optical-flow tracking guided by pyramid IMU predictions; implement a robot–edge–cloud layered architecture; transmit only feature points and keyframe descriptors with lossless encoding; validate on EuRoC and compare against state-of-the-art local feature compression and centralized SLAM schemes.

Result: Achieves lower data-volume for feature transmission than local compression methods and maintains same or better positioning accuracy under low computational load compared to centralized schemes.

Conclusion: The proposed cloud-edge-centered multi-robot SLAM framework enables real-time collaborative SLAM on bandwidth-limited links with constrained onboard compute, without sacrificing accuracy, via selective data transmission and efficient encoding.

Abstract: The integration of cloud computing and edge computing is an effective way to achieve global consistent and real-time multi-robot Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM). Cloud computing effectively solves the problem of limited computing, communication and storage capacity of terminal equipment. However, limited bandwidth and extremely long communication links between terminal devices and the cloud result in serious performance degradation of multi-robot SLAM systems. To reduce the computational cost of feature tracking and improve the real-time performance of the robot, a lightweight SLAM method of optical flow tracking based on pyramid IMU prediction is proposed. On this basis, a centralized multi-robot SLAM system based on a robot-edge-cloud layered architecture is proposed to realize real-time collaborative SLAM. It avoids the problems of limited on-board computing resources and low execution efficiency of single robot. In this framework, only the feature points and keyframe descriptors are transmitted and lossless encoding and compression are carried out to realize real-time remote information transmission with limited bandwidth resources. This design reduces the actual bandwidth occupied in the process of data transmission, and does not cause the loss of SLAM accuracy caused by data compression. Through experimental verification on the EuRoC dataset, compared with the current most advanced local feature compression method, our method can achieve lower data volume feature transmission, and compared with the current advanced centralized multi-robot SLAM scheme, it can achieve the same or better positioning accuracy under low computational load.

</details>


### [242] [Thousand-GPU Large-Scale Training and Optimization Recipe for AI-Native Cloud Embodied Intelligence Infrastructure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11101)
*Chen Zhou,Haoran Sun,Hedan Yang,Jing Long,Junwu Xiong,Luqiao Wang,Mingxi Luo,Qiming Yang,Shuai Di,Song Wang,Tianyun Zhao,Wanting Xu,Wen Huang,Xiaodong Bai,Xiaomeng Tian,Xiaolong Xiang,Yicheng Gong,Yongjian Guo,Yucheng Guo,Yunxuan Ma,Yu Wei,Zhong Guan,Zhen Sun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Industrial-scale cloud-based training platform for embodied intelligence enabling massive speedups and end-to-end pipeline integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address bottlenecks in data, frameworks, infrastructure, and evaluation that hinder progress toward embodied intelligence and AGI; need a scalable, validated end-to-end pipeline for industrial deployment.

Method: LeRobot-based thousand-GPU platform with restructured data pipeline; GR00T-N1.5 model trained on hundreds of millions of data; optimizations include variable-length FlashAttention + Data Packing for sequence integration, π-0.5 attention optimization, and FP8 quantization; infrastructure combines high-performance storage, a 3.2T RDMA network, and a Ray-driven elastic AI data lake; end-to-end evaluation loop from training to simulation to assessment.

Result: Single-round training time for GR00T-N1.5 reduced from 15 hours to 22 minutes (≈40x); 188% speedup from variable-length FlashAttention + Data Packing; 165% from π-0.5 attention optimization; 140% from FP8 quantization; validated on thousand-GPU clusters; achieved deep synergy among data, storage, communication, and computation; establishes a foundation for next-generation autonomous robots.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates a scalable, end-to-end platform for embodied intelligence that is industrially validated and poised to accelerate the development of autonomous robots and the broader human–machine integration paradigm.

Abstract: Embodied intelligence is a key step towards Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), yet its development faces multiple challenges including data, frameworks, infrastructure, and evaluation systems. To address these issues, we have, for the first time in the industry, launched a cloud-based, thousand-GPU distributed training platform for embodied intelligence, built upon the widely adopted LeRobot framework, and have systematically overcome bottlenecks across the entire pipeline. At the data layer, we have restructured the data pipeline to optimize the flow of embodied training data. In terms of training, for the GR00T-N1.5 model, utilizing thousand-GPU clusters and data at the scale of hundreds of millions, the single-round training time has been reduced from 15 hours to just 22 minutes, achieving a 40-fold speedup. At the model layer, by combining variable-length FlashAttention and Data Packing, we have moved from sample redundancy to sequence integration, resulting in a 188% speed increase; π-0.5 attention optimization has accelerated training by 165%; and FP8 quantization has delivered a 140% speedup. On the infrastructure side, relying on high-performance storage, a 3.2T RDMA network, and a Ray-driven elastic AI data lake, we have achieved deep synergy among data, storage, communication, and computation. We have also built an end-to-end evaluation system, creating a closed loop from training to simulation to assessment. This framework has already been fully validated on thousand-GPU clusters, laying a crucial technical foundation for the development and application of next-generation autonomous intelligent robots, and is expected to accelerate the arrival of the era of human-machine integration.

</details>


### [243] [ResWM: Residual-Action World Model for Visual RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11110)
*Jseen Zhang,Gabriel Adineera,Jinzhou Tan,Jinoh Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes ResWM with residual-action space and an Observation Difference Encoder, integrated into a Dreamer-style model, achieving improved sample efficiency, stability, and smoother control on DM Control Suite; outperforms Dreamer and TD-MPC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stabilize model-based RL optimization for continuous control by avoiding reliance on absolute actions, which are task-dependent and hard to optimize over long horizons; leverage smooth, incremental control in real-world robotics.

Method: Introduce residual actions (a_t as increments relative to a_{t-1}) and an Observation Difference Encoder to model frame-to-frame changes; embed these into a Dreamer-like latent dynamics framework with minimal modifications and no extra hyperparameters; train imagination rollouts and policy in the residual-action space.

Result: Empirical evaluation on the DeepMind Control Suite shows better sample efficiency, higher asymptotic returns, and smoother, more energy-efficient action trajectories; significant improvements over Dreamer and TD-MPC.

Conclusion: Residual-action modeling provides a simple yet effective principle for aligning RL with robotic control requirements, enabling stable long-horizon planning and smoother policies without added hyperparameter burden.

Abstract: Learning predictive world models from raw visual observations is a central challenge in reinforcement learning (RL), especially for robotics and continuous control. Conventional model-based RL frameworks directly condition future predictions on absolute actions, which makes optimization unstable: the optimal action distributions are task-dependent, unknown a priori, and often lead to oscillatory or inefficient control. To address this, we introduce the Residual-Action World Model (ResWM), a new framework that reformulates the control variable from absolute actions to residual actions -- incremental adjustments relative to the previous step. This design aligns with the inherent smoothness of real-world control, reduces the effective search space, and stabilizes long-horizon planning. To further strengthen the representation, we propose an Observation Difference Encoder that explicitly models the changes between adjacent frames, yielding compact latent dynamics that are naturally coupled with residual actions. ResWM is integrated into a Dreamer-style latent dynamics model with minimal modifications and no extra hyperparameters. Both imagination rollouts and policy optimization are conducted in the residual-action space, enabling smoother exploration, lower control variance, and more reliable planning. Empirical results on the DeepMind Control Suite demonstrate that ResWM achieves consistent improvements in sample efficiency, asymptotic returns, and control smoothness, significantly surpassing strong baselines such as Dreamer and TD-MPC. Beyond performance, ResWM produces more stable and energy-efficient action trajectories, a property critical for robotic systems deployed in real-world environments. These findings suggest that residual action modeling provides a simple yet powerful principle for bridging algorithmic advances in RL with the practical requirements of robotics.

</details>


### [244] [Robust Co-design Optimisation for Agile Fixed-Wing UAVs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11130)
*Adrian Andrei Buda,Xavier Chen,Nicolò Botteghi,Urban Fasel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A robust co-design framework for agile fixed-wing UAVs that embeds parametric uncertainty and wind disturbances into a concurrent optimization, using a bi-level process: high-level physical design search and a constrained trajectory planner with Monte Carlo evaluation via feedback LQR; shows improved robustness and adaptation of wing geometry over three missions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing co-design methods often neglect robustness to perturbations and model mismatch in unstructured environments, causing fragile performance for agile UAVs operating at the flight envelope edge.

Method: Bi-level optimization: upper loop optimizes physical design; lower loop discovers nominal trajectories via a constrained planner; performance is evaluated over a stochastic Monte Carlo ensemble using feedback LQR control, incorporating parametric uncertainty and wind disturbances directly into the optimization.

Result: The approach consistently outperforms deterministic baselines across three agile flight missions, and the robust co-design implicitly tailors aerodynamic features (e.g., wing placement and aspect ratio) to balance mission performance with disturbance rejection.

Conclusion: Robust co-design enables aero-structural trade-offs that enhance disturbance rejection while maintaining mission effectiveness, validating the integration of uncertainty and disturbances into concurrent design.

Abstract: Co-design optimisation of autonomous systems has emerged as a powerful alternative to sequential approaches by jointly optimising physical design and control strategies. However, existing frameworks often neglect the robustness required for autonomous systems navigating unstructured, real-world environments. For agile Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) operating at the edge of the flight envelope, this lack of robustness yields designs that are sensitive to perturbations and model mismatch. To address this, we propose a robust co-design framework for agile fixed-wing UAVs that integrates parametric uncertainty and wind disturbances directly into the concurrent optimisation process. Our bi-level approach optimises physical design in a high-level loop while discovering nominal solutions via a constrained trajectory planner and evaluating performance across a stochastic Monte Carlo ensemble using feedback LQR control. Validated across three agile flight missions, our strategy consistently outperforms deterministic baselines. The results demonstrate that our robust co-design strategy inherently tailors aerodynamic features, such as wing placement and aspect ratio, to achieve an optimal trade-off between mission performance and disturbance rejection.

</details>


### [245] [A Causal Approach to Predicting and Improving Human Perceptions of Social Navigation Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11290)
*Maximilian Diehl,Nathan Tsoi,Gustavo Chavez,Karinne Ramirez-Amaro,Marynel Vázquez*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A causal Bayesian network for predicting perceived robot competence and intent in mobile-robot navigation, with a combinatorial search to improve navigation behaviors guided by the model; achieves competitive predictive performance and notable perceptual gains in user studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predicting human perception is data-limited and interpretability is essential for safe, effective HRI. Understanding causally how factors influence perception enables explanation and controllable improvements, particularly when robots are deemed incompetent.

Method: Construct a Causal Bayesian Network to model perceptions of competence and intent. Propose a combinatorial search guided by the causal model to identify navigation behaviors that improve perceptions. Generate counterfactual robot motions to enhance interpretability. Evaluate predictive performance (F1) for competence and intention and conduct an online study with a 5-point Likert scale.

Result: Predictive F1 scores: 0.78 for competence and 0.75 for intention (binary). Online study shows a significant increase in perceived competence for low-competence behaviors, by 83%. Methods comparable or superior to state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: Causal modeling improves interpretability and provides a framework for counterfactual reasoning and behavior optimization in social navigation. The approach enhances user-perceived competence while maintaining strong predictive performance.

Abstract: As mobile robots are increasingly deployed in human environments, enabling them to predict how people perceive them is critical for socially adaptable navigation. Predicting perceptions is challenging for two main reasons: (1) HRI prediction models must learn from limited data, and (2) the obtained models must be interpretable to enable safe and effective interactions. Interpretability is particularly important when a robot is perceived as incompetent (e.g., when the robot suddenly stops or rotates away from the goal), as it allows the robot to explain its reasoning and identify controllable factors to improve performance, requiring causal rather than associative reasoning. To address these challenges, we propose a Causal Bayesian Network designed to predict how people perceive a mobile robot's competence and how they interpret its intent during navigation. Additionally, we introduce a novel method to improve perceived robot competence employing a combinatorial search, guided by the proposed causal model, to identify better navigation behaviors. Our method enhances interpretability and generates counterfactual robot motions while achieving comparable or superior predictive performance to state-of-the-art methods, reaching an F1-score of 0.78 and 0.75 for competence and intention on a binary scale. To further assess our method's ability to improve the perceived robot competence, we conducted an online evaluation in which users rated robot behaviors on a 5-point Likert scale. Our method statistically significantly increased the perceived competence of low-competent robot behavior by 83%.

</details>


### [246] [Distributed Kalman--Consensus Filtering with Adaptive Uncertainty Weighting for Multi-Object Tracking in Mobile Robot Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11328)
*Niusha Khosravi,Rodrigo Ventura,Meysam Basiri*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A Distributed Kalman–Consensus Filter (DKCF) for multi-object tracking (MOT) in mobile robot networks with partial observability and varying localization quality, incorporating uncertainty-aware adaptive consensus weighting and MOTLEE frame alignment; shows modest MOTA gain but latency limits performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fusing information from agents with heterogeneous localization accuracy causes frame misalignment, track duplicates, and ghost tracks; robust distributed MOT under partial observability is needed, leveraging transient landmarks and adaptive fusion.

Method: Extend MOTLEE-based frame alignment by adding an uncertainty-aware adaptive consensus weighting that scales neighbor information by the covariance of transmitted estimates; perform local tracking with a Kalman Filter (Constant Velocity Model) and Global Nearest Neighbor data association; validate via simulations under localization drift and communication latency.

Result: Adaptive weighting reduces the impact of unreliable data, yielding a MOTA improvement of 0.09 for drifted agents; overall performance remains constrained by communication latency.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-aware adaptive consensus improves robustness of distributed MOT in networks with heterogeneous localization; benefits are limited by latency, but the approach demonstrates effective mitigation of inconsistencies and track issues in DKCF-based MOT.

Abstract: This paper presents an implementation and evaluation of a Distributed Kalman--Consensus Filter (DKCF) for Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) in mobile robot networks operating under partial observability and heterogeneous localization uncertainty. A key challenge in such systems is the fusion of information from agents with differing localization quality, where frame misalignment can lead to inconsistent estimates, track duplication, and ghost tracks. To address this issue, we build upon the MOTLEE framework and retain its frame-alignment methodology, which uses consistently tracked dynamic objects as transient landmarks to improve relative pose estimates between robots. On top of this framework, we propose an uncertainty-aware adaptive consensus weighting mechanism that dynamically adjusts the influence of neighbor information based on the covariance of the transmitted estimates, thereby reducing the impact of unreliable data during distributed fusion. Local tracking is performed using a Kalman Filter (KF) with a Constant Velocity Model (CVM) and Global Nearest Neighbor (GNN) data association. simulation results demonstrate that adaptive weighting effectively protects local estimates from inconsistent data, yielding a MOTA improvement of 0.09 for agents suffering from localization drift, although system performance remains constrained by communication latency.

</details>


### [247] [ADMM-based Continuous Trajectory Optimization in Graphs of Convex Sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11335)
*Lukas Pries,Jon Arrizabalaga,Zachary Manchester,Markus Ryll*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ADMM-based trajectory solver for non-convex environments using polynomial trajectories and a spatio-temporal allocation graph to jointly optimize discrete spatial and continuous temporal domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compute continuous, high-quality trajectories in non-convex environments reliably, without heavy warm-start and with a broader search space.

Method: Polynomial trajectory parameterization; closed-form primal update as a minimum-control-effort problem; mixed-integer spatio-temporal allocation graph with slack update as a shortest-path problem; ADMM framework.

Result: Claims improved search space over decoupled methods and robust convergence from naive initializations, reducing reliance on warm starts; better trajectory discovery in challenging environments.

Conclusion: Joint discrete-continuous optimization via ADMM with the proposed components yields robust and more capable trajectory planning in non-convex settings.

Abstract: This paper presents a numerical solver for computing continuous trajectories in non-convex environments. Our approach relies on a customized implementation of the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) built upon two key components: First, we parameterize trajectories as polynomials, allowing the primal update to be computed in closed form as a minimum-control-effort problem. Second, we introduce the concept of a spatio-temporal allocation graph based on a mixed-integer formulation and pose the slack update as a shortest-path search. The combination of these ingredients results in a solver with several distinct advantages over the state of the art. By jointly optimizing over both discrete spatial and continuous temporal domains, our method accesses a larger search space than existing decoupled approaches, enabling the discovery of superior trajectories. Additionally, the solver's structural robustness ensures reliable convergence from naive initializations, removing the bottleneck of complex warm starting in non-convex environments.

</details>


### [248] [Novelty Adaptation Through Hybrid Large Language Model (LLM)-Symbolic Planning and LLM-guided Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11351)
*Hong Lu,Pierrick Lorang,Timothy R. Duggan,Jivko Sinapov,Matthias Scheutz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Neuro-symbolic framework combines symbolic planning, reinforcement learning, and LLMs to discover missing operators and learn control policies for novel objects in dynamic open-world robotics, outperforming current operator discovery and learning methods in continuous domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In dynamic open-worlds, traditional planners fail when encountering novel objects due to missing operators; there is a need to automatically discover operators and adapt policies to interact with new objects.

Method: Integrate symbolic planning with reinforcement learning and a large language model: the LLM identifies missing operators through common-sense reasoning, guides plan generation with the symbolic planner, and writes reward functions to steer RL for learning policies for the newly identified operators.

Result: The approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both operator discovery and operator learning in continuous robotic domains.

Conclusion: The proposed neuro-symbolic architecture enables robust adaptation to novel objects by bridging symbolic planning and learning, improving planning and control in open-world environments and showing potential for broader applicability.

Abstract: In dynamic open-world environments, autonomous agents often encounter novelties that hinder their ability to find plans to achieve their goals. Specifically, traditional symbolic planners fail to generate plans when the robot's planning domain lacks the operators that enable it to interact appropriately with novel objects in the environment. We propose a neuro-symbolic architecture that integrates symbolic planning, reinforcement learning, and a large language model (LLM) to learn how to handle novel objects. In particular, we leverage the common sense reasoning capability of the LLM to identify missing operators, generate plans with the symbolic AI planner, and write reward functions to guide the reinforcement learning agent in learning control policies for newly identified operators. Our method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in operator discovery as well as operator learning in continuous robotic domains.

</details>


### [249] [MirrorDrift: Actuated Mirror-Based Attacks on LiDAR SLAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11364)
*Rokuto Nagata,Kenji Koide,Kazuma Ikeda,Ozora Sako,Shion Horie,Kentaro Yoshioka*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel injection-free LiDAR-SLAM attack, MirrorDrift, uses an actuated planar mirror to create ghost reflections that bias scan matching. Optimizing mirror placement, alignment, and actuation yields large pose errors, including up to 6.03 m real-world localization error, revealing vulnerabilities in secure LiDARs to specular-reflection attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LiDAR SLAM is highly accurate but fragile to point-cloud corruption. Prior attacks rely on LiDAR spoofing with timing knowledge and defenses are improving. This work explores a physically grounded, injection-free threat vector leveraging specular reflections to undermine scan matching.

Method: Deploy an actuated planar mirror to generate ghost points in LiDAR scans. Optimize mirror position, orientation, and actuation to maximize misalignment in scan correspondences. Validate via simulations across multiple SLAM systems and real-world experiments on modern, defense-aware LiDARs.

Result: Simulation shows average pose error (APE) increased by 6.1x compared to random placement, with degraded SLAM performance to 2.29–3.31 m mean APE across three systems. Real-world tests yield localization errors up to 6.03 m on a modern LiDAR with defense mechanisms.

Conclusion: MirrorDrift demonstrates a practical, injection-free attack on production-grade secure LiDARs by exploiting specular reflections, underscoring the need for robust defenses against physical-reflection-based attacks on LiDAR-based SLAM.

Abstract: LiDAR SLAM provides high-accuracy localization but is fragile to point-cloud corruption because scan matching assumes geometric consistency. Prior physical attacks on LiDAR SLAM largely rely on LiDAR spoofing via external signal injection, which requires sensor-specific timing knowledge and is increasingly mitigated by modern defense mechanisms such as timing obfuscation and injection rejection. In this work, we show that specular reflection offers an injection-free alternative and demonstrate an attack, MirrorDrift, that uses an actuated planar mirror to cause ghost points in LiDAR scans and systematically bias scan-matching correspondences. MirrorDrift optimizes mirror placement, alignment, and actuation. In simulation, it increases the average pose error (APE) by 6.1x over random placement, degrading three SLAM systems to 2.29-3.31 m mean APE. In real-world experiments on a modern LiDAR with state-of-the-art interference mitigation, it induces localization errors of up to 6.03 m. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first successful SLAM-targeted attack against production-grade secure LiDARs.

</details>


### [250] [D-SLAMSpoof: An Environment-Agnostic LiDAR Spoofing Attack using Dynamic Point Cloud Injection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11365)
*Rokuto Nagata,Kenji Koide,Kazuma Ikeda,Ozora Sako,Kentaro Yoshioka*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Dynamic LiDAR spoofing (D-SLAMSpoof) attacks SLAM in feature-rich environments; ISD-SLAM is a practical inertial-based defense to detect spoofing and mitigate drift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LiDAR-based SLAM is vulnerable to spoofing, especially in urban/indoor feature-rich settings; a defense using only onboard sensors is highly desirable.

Method: Design spatially shaped and temporally dynamic spoofing injections guided by scan-matching principles; evaluate in real-world environments; propose ISD-SLAM that uses inertial dead reckoning signals to detect spoofing and mitigate drift.

Result: D-SLAMSpoof significantly improves attack success rates in feature-rich environments; ISD-SLAM detects LiDAR spoofing, including D-SLAMSpoof, and effectively mitigates position drift using only inertial data.

Conclusion: First practical defense against LiDAR-based SLAM spoofing using only standard onboard sensors; highlights vulnerabilities and provides a security-improving approach for autonomous systems.

Abstract: In this work, we introduce Dynamic SLAMSpoof (D-SLAMSpoof), a novel attack that compromises LiDAR SLAM even in feature-rich environments. The attack leverages LiDAR spoofing, which injects spurious measurements into LiDAR scans through external laser interference. By designing both spatial injection shapes and temporally coordinated dynamic injection patterns guided by scan-matching principles, D-SLAMSpoof significantly improves attack success rates in real-world, feature-rich environments such as urban areas and indoor spaces, where conventional LiDAR spoofing methods often fail. Furthermore, we propose a practical defense method, ISD-SLAM, that relies solely on inertial dead reckoning signals commonly available in autonomous systems. We demonstrate that ISD-SLAM accurately detects LiDAR spoofing attacks, including D-SLAMSpoof, and effectively mitigates the resulting position drift. Our findings expose inherent vulnerabilities in LiDAR-based SLAM and introduce the first practical defense against LiDAR-based SLAM spoofing using only standard onboard sensors, providing critical insights for improving the security and reliability of autonomous systems.

</details>


### [251] [Vision-Based Hand Shadowing for Robotic Manipulation via Inverse Kinematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11383)
*Hendrik Chiche,Antoine Jamme,Trevor Rigoberto Martinez*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Offline hand-shadowing and retargeting from a single egocentric RGB-D camera to a 6-DOF robot (SO-ARM101) using MediaPipe Hands for 3D hand pose, damped-least-squares IK in PyBullet, and a gripper aperture mapping; achieves 90% success on a structured pick-and-place benchmark but only 9.3% in unstructured real-world scenes due to occlusion, illustrating promise and current limitations of marker-free analytical retargeting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable marker-free teleoperation of low-cost manipulators by converting hand articulations into robot joint commands via an offline pipeline, reducing reliance on large datasets and expensive markers, while highlighting the gap between controlled benchmarks and real-world robustness.

Method: 1) Detect 21 landmarks per hand with MediaPipe Hands; 2) deproject to 3D using depth sensing; 3) transform to robot coordinate frame; 4) solve damped-least-squares IK to generate 6-DOF joint commands for SO-ARM101; 5) gripper control maps thumb-index geometry to aperture with a four-level fallback; 6) preview actions in a physics simulation; 7) replay on the physical robot via LeRobot; 8) evaluation against four vision-language-action baselines (ACT, SmolVLA, pi0.5, GR00T N1.5); 9) tests in unstructured environments (grocery store, pharmacy) measuring hand occlusion impact.

Result: Structured benchmark (5-tile grid, 10 grasps per tile) achieved 90% success in IK retargeting and execution. In unstructured real-world environments, success drops to 9.3% (N=75) due to hand occlusion by surroundings, indicating current limitations of marker-free analytical retargeting in unconstrained settings.

Conclusion: The pipeline demonstrates the feasibility of marker-free offline hand-to-robot retargeting for controlled tasks, with strong performance in structured environments, but is significantly challenged by occlusion and real-world variability. Improvements are needed for robustness, real-time feedback, and occlusion-handling to close the gap between controlled benchmarks and everyday use.

Abstract: Teleoperation of low-cost robotic manipulators remains challenging due to the complexity of mapping human hand articulations to robot joint commands. We present an offline hand-shadowing and retargeting pipeline from a single egocentric RGB-D camera mounted on 3D-printed glasses. The pipeline detects 21 hand landmarks per hand using MediaPipe Hands, deprojects them into 3D via depth sensing, transforms them into the robot coordinate frame, and solves a damped-least-squares inverse kinematics problem in PyBullet to produce joint commands for the 6-DOF SO-ARM101 robot. A gripper controller maps thumb-index finger geometry to grasp aperture with a four-level fallback hierarchy. Actions are first previewed in a physics simulation before replay on the physical robot through the LeRobot framework. We evaluate the IK retargeting pipeline on a structured pick-and-place benchmark (5-tile grid, 10 grasps per tile) achieving a 90% success rate, and compare it against four vision-language-action policies (ACT, SmolVLA, pi0.5, GR00T N1.5) trained on leader-follower teleoperation data. We also test the IK pipeline in unstructured real-world environments (grocery store, pharmacy), where hand occlusion by surrounding objects reduces success to 9.3% (N=75), highlighting both the promise and current limitations of marker-free analytical retargeting.

</details>


### [252] [Deployment-Time Reliability of Learned Robot Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11400)
*Christopher Agia*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Deployment-time mechanisms to improve reliability of learning-based robot policies: runtime monitoring, data-centric interpretability via influence functions, and probability-based long-horizon planning for robust execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliability at deployment is hampered by distribution shift, compounding errors, and complex task dependencies; there's a need for mechanisms that operate around learned policies to ensure trustworthy real-world use.

Method: 1) Runtime monitoring detects impending failures from inconsistencies in policy behavior and task progress without failure data or task-specific supervision. 2) A data-centric framework uses influence functions to trace deployment successes/failures to influential training demonstrations, enabling diagnosis and dataset curation. 3) Policy coordination is cast as estimating and maximizing the probability of successful behavior sequences, extended to open-ended, language-specified tasks via feasibility-aware task planning.

Result: The abstract presents a coherent trio of deployment-time strategies and a unifying perspective on reliability, but provides no empirical results; it articulates conceptual mechanisms and expected benefits for robust real-world robot autonomy.

Conclusion: Targeting deployment-time reliability through monitoring, interpretability, and planning is positioned as essential for trustworthy, scalable robot autonomy; continued work along these lines is needed to translate these foundations into practice.

Abstract: Recent advances in learning-based robot manipulation have produced policies with remarkable capabilities. Yet, reliability at deployment remains a fundamental barrier to real-world use, where distribution shift, compounding errors, and complex task dependencies collectively undermine system performance. This dissertation investigates how the reliability of learned robot policies can be improved at deployment time through mechanisms that operate around them. We develop three complementary classes of deployment-time mechanisms. First, we introduce runtime monitoring methods that detect impending failures by identifying inconsistencies in closed-loop policy behavior and deviations in task progress, without requiring failure data or task-specific supervision. Second, we propose a data-centric framework for policy interpretability that traces deployment-time successes and failures to influential training demonstrations using influence functions, enabling principled diagnosis and dataset curation. Third, we address reliable long-horizon task execution by formulating policy coordination as the problem of estimating and maximizing the success probability of behavior sequences, and we extend this formulation to open-ended, language-specified tasks through feasibility-aware task planning. By centering on core challenges of deployment, these contributions advance practical foundations for the reliable, real-world use of learned robot policies. Continued progress on these foundations will be essential for enabling trustworthy and scalable robot autonomy in the future.

</details>


### [253] [Real-time Rendering-based Surgical Instrument Tracking via Evolutionary Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11404)
*Hanyang Hu,Zekai Liang,Florian Richter,Michael C. Yip*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CMA-ES based joint pose and joint configuration tracking for surgical instruments using batch rendering to improve speed and robustness; supports joint-angle-free and bi-manual tracking; outperforms prior methods in accuracy and runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate, robust, and fast tracking of surgical instruments is challenging due to partial visibility, degraded visual quality, data scarcity, and specialized articulation; markerless pose estimation helps but existing methods struggle with unreliable feature detections and computation; a robust optimization-based approach is needed.

Method: A CMA-ES evolutionary optimization integrated into a versatile tracking pipeline that jointly estimates instrument pose and joint configurations. Batch rendering evaluates multiple pose candidates in parallel to accelerate inference. The framework generalizes to joint-angle-free and bi-manual tracking settings for vision feedback control and online calibration.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show significant improvements in accuracy and runtime compared with prior approaches, with enhanced convergence robustness.

Conclusion: The proposed framework generalizes across tracking settings (joint-angle-free and bi-manual) and is suitable for real-time vision-based control and online calibration in robot-assisted MIS.

Abstract: Accurate and efficient tracking of surgical instruments is fundamental for Robot-Assisted Minimally Invasive Surgery. Although vision-based robot pose estimation has enabled markerless calibration without tedious physical setups, reliable tool tracking for surgical robots still remains challenging due to partial visibility and specialized articulation design of surgical instruments. Previous works in the field are usually prone to unreliable feature detections under degraded visual quality and data scarcity, whereas rendering-based methods often struggle with computational costs and suboptimal convergence. In this work, we incorporate CMA-ES, an evolutionary optimization strategy, into a versatile tracking pipeline that jointly estimates surgical instrument pose and joint configurations. Using batch rendering to efficiently evaluate multiple pose candidates in parallel, the method significantly reduces inference time and improves convergence robustness. The proposed framework further generalizes to joint angle-free and bi-manual tracking settings, making it suitable for both vision feedback control and online surgery video calibration. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms prior approaches in both accuracy and runtime.

</details>


### [254] [Grounding Robot Generalization in Training Data via Retrieval-Augmented VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11426)
*Jensen Gao,Dorsa Sadigh,Sandy Huang,Dhruv Shah*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RADAR is a two-stage retrieval + vision-language analysis framework that compares test-time robotics tasks to training data to classify the type of policy generalization required; scalable and aligned with human benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable precise evaluation of policy generalization in robotics by mapping test-time tasks to the distribution of the training data and identifying what generalization is needed.

Method: Stage 1: Retrieve relevant training examples for a given evaluation task using generalist policy embeddings. Stage 2: Use vision-language models to compare the evaluation task with the retrieved data, producing axis-based analyses and an overall classification of the required generalization.

Result: VLMs effectively analyze data for generalization; the retrieval step identifies the necessary examples to make accurate classifications; scaling RADAR to large-scale datasets yields agreement with prior human-defined benchmark conditions.

Conclusion: RADAR provides a scalable, interpretable framework for diagnosing and categorizing policy generalization, enabling more precise evaluation and aligning with established benchmarks.

Abstract: Recent work on robot manipulation has advanced policy generalization to novel scenarios. However, it is often difficult to characterize how different evaluation settings actually represent generalization from the training distribution of a given policy. To work towards more precise evaluation of generalization in robotics, we propose RADAR, a scalable framework for directly comparing test-time evaluation tasks to policy training data, to determine what form of policy generalization is required. RADAR consists of a two-stage pipeline: first, retrieval using generalist policy embeddings identifies which training examples are relevant for a given evaluation task. Next, vision-language models (VLMs) analyze the evaluation task against the retrieved data, outputting interpretable analysis on how they compare along a variety of axes, and an overall classification of what type of policy generalization is required. Through controlled experiments, we demonstrate that VLMs are effective at analyzing data for generalization, and that our retrieval step effectively identifies examples needed to make accurate classifications with respect to the training data. Furthermore, we scale RADAR to large-scale datasets, where we observe agreement with human-defined benchmark conditions from prior work. We provide demonstrations at radar-analysis.github.io.

</details>


### [255] [A Generalized Theory of Load Distribution in Redundantly-actuated Robotic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11431)
*Joshua Flight,Clément Gosselin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Presents a generalized, scalable theory for distribution of applied loads (wrenches) among redundantly actuated, multi-chain robotic systems; provides explicit, efficient solutions for wrench synthesis and analysis with linear scaling; critiques current approaches and offers corrections, supported by examples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable robust, efficient force-control and manipulation in overconstrained robotic systems (multifingered grippers, legged robots, cooperative robots) by fully characterizing feasible wrench distributions and providing practical solution methods.

Method: Develops a mathematical framework that characterizes the feasible wrench distributions for a given resultant wrench across independent closed-loop kinematic chains. Derives explicit, closed-form solutions for wrench synthesis and analysis that scale linearly with the number of wrenches and avoid numerical methods or large matrix inversions. Identifies deficiencies in existing methods and proposes corrections, validated by illustrative examples.

Result: Explicit, computationally efficient solutions to wrench synthesis and analysis; a complete description of the feasible wrench set for given loads; linear-time scaling with respect to the number of applied wrenches; avoidance of numerical solvers and large matrix inversions; demonstration of improvements over current approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed framework advances force-control in complex, redundantly actuated robots, with broad applicability to multifingered grippers, legged robotics, and cooperative manipulation; it also highlights and corrects shortcomings in contemporary methods.

Abstract: This paper presents a generalized theory which describes how applied loads are distributed within rigid bodies handled by redundantly-actuated robotic systems composed of multiple independent closed-loop kinematic chains. The theory fully characterizes the feasible set of manipulating wrench distributions for a given resultant wrench applied to the rigid body and has important implications for the force-control of multifingered grippers, legged robots, cooperating robots, and other overconstrained mechanisms. We also derive explicit solutions to the wrench synthesis and wrench analysis problems. These solutions are computationally efficient and scale linearly with the number of applied wrenches, requiring neither numerical methods nor the inversion of large matrices. Finally, we identify significant shortcomings in current state-of-the-art approaches and propose corrections. These are supported by illustrative examples that demonstrate the advantages of the improved methods.

</details>


### [256] [Enhancing Lightweight Vision Language Models through Group Competitive Learning for Socially Compliant Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11447)
*Xinyu Zhang,Atsushi Konno,Toshihiko Yamasaki,Ling Xiao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GCL introduces Group Competitive Learning to boost lightweight vision-language models (VLMs) for social navigation, claiming significant performance gains (F1 up to 0.968) over vanilla fine-tuning while maintaining efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between accuracy and efficiency in socially aware robot navigation by enabling lightweight VLMs to achieve competitive reasoning and decision-making with reduced computational cost.

Method: Proposes Group Competitive Learning (GCL) comprising two components: Group Competitive Objective (GCO) that balances global semantic alignment with distributional regularization, and Asymmetric Group Optimization (AGO) to push model performance toward its upper limits. Implemented on lightweight VLMs (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL-3B, Qwen3-VL-4B).

Result: Empirical results on social navigation benchmarks show substantial improvements over vanilla supervised fine-tuning: F1 up to 0.968 for 3B model with GCL and 0.914 for 4B model. The 3B model surpasses the 8B baseline under GCL by 28% despite starting lower under standard SFT. 

Conclusion: GCL effectively enhances both accuracy and efficiency for lightweight VLMs in real-world social navigation, suggesting a viable path to deploy capable models without the computational burden of larger architectures.

Abstract: Social robot navigation requires a sophisticated integration of scene semantics and human social norms. Scaling up Vision Language Models (VLMs) generally improves reasoning and decision-making capabilities for socially compliant navigation. However, increased model size incurs substantial computational overhead, limiting suitability for real-time robotic deployment. Conversely, lightweight VLMs enable efficient inference but often exhibit weaker reasoning and decision-making performance in socially complex environments. Achieving both strong reasoning ability and efficiency remains an open challenge. To bridge this gap, we propose Group Competitive Learning (GCL), a strategy designed to amplify the capabilities of lightweight VLMs. Our strategy introduces the Group Competitive Objective (GCO) to harmonize global semantics with distributional regularization, alongside Asymmetric Group Optimization (AGO) to explore the upper limits of model performance. Empirical evaluations on social navigation benchmarks demonstrate that GCL significantly elevates VLM performance. Specifically, GCL enables the Qwen2.5-VL-3B learner model and guide Qwen3-VL-4B to achieve an F1 score of 0.968 and 0.914, representing 40\% and 12\% improvement over vanilla supervised fine-tuning (SFT). Notably, under vanilla SFT, the 3B model initially trails the 8B model (F1: 0.692 vs. 0.755). However, through the GCL, the 3B model outperforms (28\%) the 8B baseline model. These results suggest that GCL provides an effective solution for achieving both high accuracy and computational efficiency in real-world deployment.

</details>


### [257] [CoViLLM: An Adaptive Human-Robot Collaborative Assembly Framework Using Large Language Models for Manufacturing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11461)
*Jiabao Zhao,Jonghan Lim,Hongliang Li,Ilya Kovalenko*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A CoViLLM framework enables adaptive human-robot collaborative assembly handling customized/unseen products via depth-based localization, operator classification, and LLM-based planning; validated on NIST Assembly Task Board shows extended HRC beyond predefined settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mass customization demands flexible assembly robots; rule-based systems and predefined pipelines hinder handling new variants. HRC helps adaptability but current approaches rely on fixed perception/manipulation pipelines; need autonomous task planning for unfamiliar products.

Method: Integrates depth-camera localization for object pose estimation, human operator classification to identify new components, and an LLM to plan assembly tasks from natural language instructions; tested on NIST Assembly Task Board across known, customized, and new product cases.

Result: Demonstrates flexible collaborative assembly and extended HRC capabilities beyond predefined product/task settings; supports assembly of customized and unseen products.

Conclusion: CoViLLM provides a viable architecture for adaptive HRC with perception, human-in-the-loop identification, and language-based planning; suggests broader applicability to flexible manufacturing and mass customization.

Abstract: With increasing demand for mass customization, traditional manufacturing robots that rely on rule-based operations lack the flexibility to accommodate customized or new product variants. Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC) has demonstrated potential to improve system adaptability by leveraging human versatility and decision-making capabilities. However, existing HRC frame- works typically depend on predefined perception-manipulation pipelines, limiting their ability to autonomously generate task plans for new product assembly. In this work, we propose CoViLLM, an adaptive human-robot collaborative assembly frame- work that supports the assembly of customized and previously unseen products. CoViLLM combines depth-camera-based localization for object position estimation, human operator classification for identifying new components, and an Large Language Model (LLM) for assembly task planning based on natural language instructions. The framework is validated on the NIST Assembly Task Board for known, customized, and new product cases. Experimental results show that the proposed framework enables flexible collaborative assembly by extending HRC beyond predefined product and task settings.

</details>


### [258] [NFPO: Stabilized Policy Optimization of Normalizing Flow for Robotic Policy Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11470)
*Diyuan Shi,Yiqi Tang,Zifeng Zhuang,Donglin Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: NF-based policy parameterization for DRL in robotics enables multimodal policy representations and stable online training (NFPO); demonstrates strong simulation performance and real-world transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diagonal Gaussian policies cannot capture multimodal action distributions; Normalizing Flows provide multimodal modeling, closed-form log-probability, and efficiency, motivating their use for robotic control.

Method: Proposes replacing Gaussian policy with a Normalizing Flow-based policy (NFPO) and analyzes instability in online RL; implements a simple stabilization technique to address training instability; evaluates across multiple simulations and real robots.

Result: NFPO achieves robust, strong performance across diverse benchmarks and transfers to real-world robots.

Conclusion: NF-based policy parameterization is a viable alternative to Gaussian policies in DRL for robotics, enabling multimodality and stable online training, with demonstrated sim-to-real transfer.

Abstract: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has experienced significant advancements in recent years and has been widely used in many fields. In DRL-based robotic policy learning, however, current de facto policy parameterization is still multivariate Gaussian (with diagonal covariance matrix), which lacks the ability to model multi-modal distribution. In this work, we explore the adoption of a modern network architecture, i.e. Normalizing Flow (NF) as the policy parameterization for its ability of multi-modal modeling, closed form of log probability and low computation and memory overhead. However, naively training NF in online Reinforcement Learning (RL) usually leads to training instability. We provide a detailed analysis for this phenomenon and successfully address it via simple but effective technique. With extensive experiments in multiple simulation environments, we show our method, NFPO could obtain robust and strong performance in widely used robotic learning tasks and successfully transfer into real-world robots.

</details>


### [259] [SPARK: Skeleton-Parameter Aligned Retargeting on Humanoid Robots with Kinodynamic Trajectory Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11480)
*Hanwen Wang,Qiayuan Liao,Bike Zhang,Kunzhao Ren,Koushil Sreenath,Xiaobin Xiong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A two-stage pipeline converts human motion into a URDF-based skeleton aligned to a target humanoid, followed by progressive kinodynamic trajectory optimization, to generate dynamic, feasible motion references for learning-based controllers across diverse humanoid platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Raw human demonstrations often do not map directly to a robot due to kinematic/dynamic differences, leading to IK errors and poor-quality references. A unified, dynamically feasible reference pipeline is needed for cross-platform humanoid control.

Method: 1) Skeleton calibration: convert human motion into a URDF-based skeleton and scale to the target humanoid, aligning structure to reduce IK error and tuning effort. 2) Progressive kinodynamic trajectory optimization: three-stage TO—kinematic TO, inverse dynamics, and full kinodynamic TO—with warm-starts from prior solutions to obtain dynamically consistent trajectories and torque profiles.

Result: Produces dynamically consistent state trajectories and joint torque profiles, providing high-quality motion references for learning-based controllers. Skeleton calibration reduces IK error and tuning effort, enabling natural motion references across diverse humanoids.

Conclusion: Skeleton-style alignment (URDF-based skeleton calibration) plus staged kinodynamic TO enables natural, physically plausible motion references that generalize across diverse humanoid platforms.

Abstract: Human motion provides rich priors for training general-purpose humanoid control policies, but raw demonstrations are often incompatible with a robot's kinematics and dynamics, limiting their direct use. We present a two-stage pipeline for generating natural and dynamically feasible motion references from task-space human data. First, we convert human motion into a unified robot description format (URDF)-based skeleton representation and calibrate it to the target humanoid's dimensions. By aligning the underlying skeleton structure rather than heuristically modifying task-space targets, this step significantly reduces inverse kinematics error and tuning effort. Second, we refine the retargeted trajectories through progressive kinodynamic trajectory optimization (TO), solved in three stages: kinematic TO, inverse dynamics, and full kinodynamic TO, each warm-started from the previous solution. The final result yields dynamically consistent state trajectories and joint torque profiles, providing high-quality references for learning-based controllers. Together, skeleton calibration and kinodynamic TO enable the generation of natural, physically consistent motion references across diverse humanoid platforms.

</details>


### [260] [MiNI-Q: A Miniature, Wire-Free Quadruped with Unbounded, Independently Actuated Leg Joints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11537)
*Daniel Koh,Suraj Shah,Yufeng Wu,Dennis Hong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MiNI-Q^2 is a miniature, wire-free quadruped with independently actuated, mechanically unbounded 2-DOF leg joints that fold to 2.5 cm height. It achieves up to 0.46 m/s and supports diverse locomotion (crawling, stair climbing, inverted motion, jumping, backflips); open-source mechanical/electrical designs extend prior Q8bot to improve miniature assembly reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physical joint limits in legged robots constrain workspace and gait design, increase hardware risk, and hinder miniaturization. Developing a miniature, wire-free, highly capable quadruped with unbounded joints enables versatile locomotion and easier fabrication, while open-sourcing designs supports reproducibility and further research.

Method: Design and analyze a miniature quadruped (MiNI-Q^2) with independently actuated, mechanically unbounded 2-DOF joints per leg, wire-free architecture, and a compact foldable form factor. Provide kinematic analysis, experimental validation across locomotion modes, compare to Q8bot, and release mechanical/electrical files as open source.

Result: The robot demonstrates versatile locomotion including low-clearance crawling, stair climbing, inverted locomotion, jumping, and backflipping. It folds to 2.5 cm height and reaches speeds up to 0.46 m/s. Open-source files enhance reproducibility and assembly reliability at miniature scale, representing an advancement over the authors’ previous Q8bot design.

Conclusion: MiNI-Q^2 validates that unbounded 2-DOF leg joints in a miniature, wire-free quadruped enable versatile and robust locomotion while maintaining a compact form factor. The open-source release fosters rapid adoption and further innovation in miniature legged robotics.

Abstract: Physical joint limits are common in legged robots and can restrict workspace, constrain gait design, and increase the risk of hardware damage. This paper introduces MiNI-Q^2, a miniature, wire-free quadruped robot with independently actuated, mechanically unbounded 2-DOF leg joints. We present the mechanical design, kinematic analysis, and experimental validation of the proposed robot. The leg mechanism enables both oscillatory gaits and rotary locomotion while allowing the robot to fold to a minimum height of 2.5 cm. Experimentally, MiNI-Q achieves speeds up to 0.46 m/s and demonstrates low-clearance crawling, stair climbing, inverted locomotion, jumping, and backflipping. The wire-free architecture extends our previous Q8bot design, improving assembly reliability at miniature scale. All mechanical and electrical design files are released open source to support reproducibility and further research.

</details>


### [261] [RoboClaw: An Agentic Framework for Scalable Long-Horizon Robotic Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11558)
*Ruiying Li,Yunlang Zhou,YuYao Zhu,Kylin Chen,Jingyuan Wang,Sukai Wang,Kongtao Hu,Minhui Yu,Bowen Jiang,Zhan Su,Jiayao Ma,Xin He,Yongjian Shen,Yangyang,Guanghui Ren,Maoqing Yao,Wenhao Wang,Yao Mu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoboClaw unifies data collection, policy learning, and execution under a single VLM-driven controller, introducing Entangled Action Pairs to create self-resetting data loops. It enables continuous on-policy data collection, iterative policy refinement, and dynamic task orchestration for long-horizon manipulation. Real-world experiments show higher stability and scalability than open-loop pipelines, with a 25% higher success rate and 53.7% less human time investment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon vision-language-action (VLA) tasks in robotics are hindered by pipeline fragmentation: data collection, policy learning, and deployment are disjoint, requiring manual resets and brittle multi-policy execution. A unified, self-resetting framework could reduce human effort and improve robustness by maintaining consistent context across collection and execution.

Method: RoboClaw presents an agentic framework that uses a single VLM-driven controller for both data collection and deployment. It introduces Entangled Action Pairs (EAP), coupling forward manipulation with inverse recovery actions to form self-resetting loops that enable autonomous on-policy data collection and iterative policy refinement. At deployment, the agent performs high-level reasoning and dynamically orchestrates learned policy primitives to achieve long-horizon goals, maintaining consistent contextual semantics across phases.

Result: Empirical evaluation on real-world manipulation tasks shows improved stability and scalability over conventional open-loop pipelines. RoboClaw achieves a 25% improvement in success rate on long-horizon tasks and reduces human time investment by 53.7%.

Conclusion: Maintaining stable, context-consistent semantics across collection and execution reduces mismatch between phases and enhances multi-policy robustness. A unified VLM-driven controller with EAPs enables scalable, low-supervision learning and deployment for long-horizon robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) systems have shown strong potential for language-driven robotic manipulation. However, scaling them to long-horizon tasks remains challenging. Existing pipelines typically separate data collection, policy learning, and deployment, resulting in heavy reliance on manual environment resets and brittle multi-policy execution. We present RoboClaw, an agentic robotics framework that unifies data collection, policy learning, and task execution under a single VLM-driven controller. At the policy level, RoboClaw introduces Entangled Action Pairs (EAP), which couple forward manipulation behaviors with inverse recovery actions to form self-resetting loops for autonomous data collection. This mechanism enables continuous on-policy data acquisition and iterative policy refinement with minimal human intervention. During deployment, the same agent performs high-level reasoning and dynamically orchestrates learned policy primitives to accomplish long-horizon tasks. By maintaining consistent contextual semantics across collection and execution, RoboClaw reduces mismatch between the two phases and improves multi-policy robustness. Experiments in real-world manipulation tasks demonstrate improved stability and scalability compared to conventional open-loop pipelines, while significantly reducing human effort throughout the robot lifecycle, achieving a 25% improvement in success rate over baseline methods on long-horizon tasks and reducing human time investment by 53.7%.

</details>


### [262] [Unsupervised LiDAR-Based Multi-UAV Detection and Tracking Under Extreme Sparsity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11586)
*Nivand Khosravi,Rodrigo Ventura,Meysam Basiri*

Main category: cs.RO
