TL;DR: Unsupervised LiDAR-only pipeline for detection and tracking of UAVs in highly sparse, non-repetitive solid-state LiDAR data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Airborne UAV detection/tracking with extremely sparse measurements challenges traditional detection/association, especially without labeled data; need a robust, unsupervised method and proper evaluation beyond GNSS-limited baselines.

Method: Range-adaptive DBSCAN clustering combined with a three-stage temporal consistency check for detection; evaluation across eight parameter configurations. For tracking, compare deterministic Hungarian assignment vs Joint Probabilistic Data Association (JPDA) each with Interacting Multiple Model (IMM) filtering, in four simulated scenarios with increasing ambiguity; a two-environment evaluation strategy pairing real-world detection (RTK-GPS ground truth) with simulation-based tracking (identity-annotated ground truth).

Result: Best detection setup achieves 0.891 precision, 0.804 recall, and 0.63 m RMSE; most scans contain at most 1-2 target points, quantifying sparsity; JPDA reduces identity switches by 64% with negligible impact on MOTA.

Conclusion: Probabilistic data association (JPDA) outperforms deterministic assignment in close-proximity UAV trajectories; the proposed unsupervised LiDAR-only pipeline is effective under severe sparsity, and the two-environment evaluation strategy provides robust validation beyond GNSS-only metrics.

Abstract: Non-repetitive solid-state LiDAR scanning leads to an extremely sparse measurement regime for detecting airborne UAVs: a small quadrotor at 10-25 m typically produces only 1-2 returns per scan, which is far below the point densities assumed by most existing detection approaches and inadequate for robust multi-target data association. We introduce an unsupervised, LiDAR-only pipeline that addresses both detection and tracking without the need for labeled training data. The detector integrates range-adaptive DBSCAN clustering with a three-stage temporal consistency check and is benchmarked on real-world air-to-air flight data under eight different parameter configurations. The best setup attains 0.891 precision, 0.804 recall, and 0.63 m RMSE, and a systematic minPts sweep verifies that most scans contain at most 1-2 target points, directly quantifying the sparsity regime. For multi-target tracking, we compare deterministic Hungarian assignment with joint probabilistic data association (JPDA), each coupled with Interacting Multiple Model filtering, in four simulated scenarios with increasing levels of ambiguity. JPDA cuts identity switches by 64% with negligible impact on MOTA, demonstrating that probabilistic association is advantageous when UAV trajectories approach one another closely. A two-environment evaluation strategy, combining real-world detection with RTK-GPS ground truth and simulation-based tracking with identity-annotated ground truth, overcomes the limitations of GNSS-only evaluation at inter-UAV distances below 2 m.

</details>


### [263] [Diversity You Can Actually Measure: A Fast, Model-Free Diversity Metric for Robotics Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11634)
*Sreevardhan Sirigiri,Nathan Samuel de Lara,Christopher Agia,Florian Shkurti,Fabio Ramos*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Entropy-based diversity metrics for robot imitation learning using signature transform on Gram matrices, and a model-free data-curation method (FAKTUAL) that selects diverse demonstrations to maximize entropy, improving generalization with efficient computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify and leverage dataset diversity in long-horizon imitation learning where trajectories and high-dimensional observations complicate diversity assessment.

Method: Extend Shannon/von Neumann entropy to Gram matrices derived from a signature kernel over demonstrations; introduce FAKTUAL to select a subset of demonstrations by maximizing entropy under a subset-size budget; fully model-free with no policy/rollouts required; efficient.

Result: Across RoboMimic, MetaWorld, and four real-world tasks, diversity-aware curation via FAKTUAL improves downstream success rates vs random selection and is more computationally efficient than recent methods.

Conclusion: Entropy of demonstration datasets is a practical tool for understanding and improving dataset diversity in robot imitation learning; FAKTUAL provides an effective, model-free approach for diverse data curation that enhances generalization.

Abstract: Robotics datasets for imitation learning typically consist of long-horizon trajectories of different lengths over states, actions, and high-dimensional observations (e.g., RGB video), making it non-trivial to quantify diversity in a way that respects the underlying trajectory structure and geometry. We extend Shannon and von Neumann entropy to this setting by defining signature transform-based entropy on the Gram matrix of a signature kernel over demonstrations, yielding entropy and diversity metrics that operate directly on the demonstration dataset. Building on these metrics, we study how dataset diversity affects generalization performance in robot imitation learning and propose a simple, model-free way to curate diverse demonstrations. We introduce FAKTUAL (FAst trajectory Kernel enTropy cUration for imitation Learning), a data curation algorithm that selects a subset of demonstrations maximizing entropy given a subset-size budget. FAKTUAL is fully model-free, requires no access to the imitation policy or rollouts, and adds negligible overhead relative to policy training. We evaluate our approach on image and state-based RoboMimic and MetaWorld benchmarks, as well as four real-world manipulation tasks. Across tasks and architectures, diversity-aware curation with FAKTUAL consistently improves downstream success rates over random selection, while being substantially more computationally efficient compared to recent robot data curation methods. Our results suggest that the entropy of demonstration datasets is a practical tool for understanding and improving dataset diversity in robot imitation learning.

</details>


### [264] [Learn Structure, Adapt on the Fly: Multi-Scale Residual Learning and Online Adaptation for Aerial Manipulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11638)
*Samaksh Ujjawal,Naveen Sudheer Nair,Shivansh Pratap Singh,Rishabh Dev Yadav,Wei Pan,Spandan Roy*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A predictive-adaptive residual modeling framework for AAMs using a Factorized Dynamics Transformer and Latent Residual Adapter to handle cross-variable coupling and multi-scale dynamics under payload changes, enabling real-time, improved control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous Aerial Manipulators exhibit strongly coupled, nonlinear, nonstationary dynamics that shift with configuration changes and payload variations. Fixed-parametric models and stationary data-driven methods degrade under such changes, and existing learning architectures often ignore cross-variable couplings and multi-scale temporal effects, hindering robustness and deployment under distribution shifts.

Method: Introduce Factorized Dynamics Transformer (FDT) that treats physical variables as independent tokens to enable explicit cross-variable attention and separate short-horizon inertial dynamics from long-horizon aerodynamic effects. Pair with a Latent Residual Adapter (LRA) that performs rapid linear adaptation in latent space via Recursive Least Squares to address deployment-time distribution shifts, preserving the offline nonlinear representation. Integrate the adapted residual forecast into a residual-compensated adaptive controller. Validate on a real aerial manipulator under unseen payloads.

Result: Real-world experiments show higher prediction fidelity, faster disturbance attenuation, and superior closed-loop tracking precision compared with state-of-the-art learning baselines, while maintaining strict real-time feasibility.

Conclusion: The combination of factorized, attention-based dynamics modeling and fast latent adaptation yields robust, real-time residual compensation for AAMs across nonstationary, multiscale dynamics and payload variations, with demonstrated practical effectiveness and potential generalization to similar nonstationary systems.

Abstract: Autonomous Aerial Manipulators (AAMs) are inherently coupled, nonlinear systems that exhibit nonstationary and multiscale residual dynamics, particularly during manipulator reconfiguration and abrupt payload variations. Conventional analytical dynamic models rely on fixed parametric structures, while static data-driven model assume stationary dynamics and degrade under configuration changes and payload variations. Moreover, existing learning architectures do not explicitly factorize cross-variable coupling and multi-scale temporal effects, conflating instantaneous inertial dynamics with long-horizon regime evolution. We propose a predictive-adaptive framework for real-time residual modeling and compensation in AAMs. The core of this framework is the Factorized Dynamics Transformer (FDT), which treats physical variables as independent tokens. This design enables explicit cross-variable attention while structurally separating short-horizon inertial dependencies from long-horizon aerodynamic effects. To address deployment-time distribution shifts, a Latent Residual Adapter (LRA) performs rapid linear adaptation in the latent space via Recursive Least Squares, preserving the offline nonlinear representation without prohibitive computational overhead. The adapted residual forecast is directly integrated into a residual-compensated adaptive controller. Real-world experiments on an aerial manipulator subjected to unseen payloads demonstrate higher prediction fidelity, accelerated disturbance attenuation, and superior closed-loop tracking precision compared to state-of-the-art learning baselines, all while maintaining strict real-time feasibility.

</details>


### [265] [Chunk-Boundary Artifact in Action-Chunked Generative Policies: A Noise-Sensitive Failure Mechanism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11642)
*Rui Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Chunk-boundary artifacts in pretrained action-chunked policies are noise-driven failures that correlate with task outcomes and can be steered via noise directions to modulate performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why execution discontinuities occur at action-chunk boundaries in generative visuomotor policies and whether they are unavoidable or controllable.

Method: Analyze a frozen pretrained action-chunked policy during rollout. Use a permutation test to link artifact to task failure (p<1e-4). Show artifact emerges during rollout. Under a fixed observation context, vary latent noise to modulate artifact magnitude. Identify artifact-related directions in noise space and perform trajectory-level steering to alter artifact magnitude across tasks. Evaluate in hard tasks (with headroom) and near-ceiling tasks.

Result: Artifact is strongly associated with task failure and emerges during rollout. Latent noise modulates artifact magnitude. Steering along artifact-related noise directions reliably alters artifact magnitude across tasks. In hard tasks, success/failure distribution shifts; in near-ceiling tasks, positive gains saturate due to policy limits, while negative causal effects remain visible.

Conclusion: Boundaries in action chunking are a noise-dominated, intervenable failure mechanism rather than an unavoidable execution nuisance; recognizing and steering this artifact can improve performance of visuomotor policies.

Abstract: Action chunking has become a central design choice for generative visuomotor policies, yet the execution discontinuities that arise at chunk boundaries remain poorly understood. In a frozen pretrained action-chunked policy, we identify chunk-boundary artifact as a noise-sensitive failure mechanism. First, artifact is strongly associated with task failure (p < 1e-4, permutation test) and emerges during the rollout rather than only as a post-hoc symptom. Second, under a fixed observation context, changing only latent noise systematically modulates artifact magnitude. Third, by identifying artifact-related directions in noise space and applying trajectory-level steering, we reliably alter artifact magnitude across all evaluated tasks. In hard-task settings with remaining outcome headroom, the success/failure distribution shifts accordingly; on near-ceiling tasks, positive gains are compressed by policy saturation, while the negative causal effect remains visible. Overall, we recast boundary discontinuity from an unavoidable execution nuisance into an analyzable, noise-dominated, and intervenable failure mechanism.

</details>


### [266] [A Hybrid Neural-Assisted Unscented Kalman Filter for Unmanned Ground Vehicle Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11649)
*Gal Versano,Itzik Klein*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hybrid UKF with a neural network that predicts process and measurement noise covariances from raw IMU/GNSS data, trained in simulation, evaluated on real datasets with 12.7% position improvement over adaptive model-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed, constant noise covariances struggle under dynamic real-world conditions; integrating learned uncertainty estimation into classical state estimation can improve robustness and accuracy for autonomous navigation.

Method: Keep the unscented Kalman filter equations unchanged. Use a dedicated neural network to predict Q (process noise) and R (measurement noise) from raw inertial and GNSS measurements. Train the network in a simulational environment with perfect ground truth. Evaluate generalization on real data (three datasets, 160 minutes total) across different vehicle types and conditions.

Result: Achieves a 12.7% improvement in position accuracy compared to an adaptive, model-based approach across all three real datasets, demonstrating improved robustness and generalization.

Conclusion: The approach offers a scalable, robust hybrid estimation framework for unmanned ground vehicle navigation by augmenting classical filtering with data-driven uncertainty estimation, with demonstrated sim-to-real transfer across diverse conditions.

Abstract: Modern autonomous navigation for unmanned ground vehicles relies on different estimators to fuse inertial sensors and GNSS measurements. However, the constant noise covariance matrices often struggle to account for dynamic real-world conditions. In this work we propose a hybrid estimation framework that bridges classical state estimation foundations with modern deep learning approaches. Instead of altering the fundamental unscented Kalman filter equations, a dedicated deep neural network is developed to predict the process and measurement noise uncertainty directly from raw inertial and GNSS measurements. We present a sim2real approach, with training performed only on simulative data. In this manner, we offer perfect ground truth data and relieves the burden of extensive data recordings. To evaluate our proposed approach and examine its generalization capabilities, we employed a 160-minutes test set from three datasets each with different types of vehicles (off-road vehicle, passenger car, and mobile robot), inertial sensors, road surface, and environmental conditions. We demonstrate across the three datasets a position improvement of $12.7\%$ compared to the adaptive model-based approach. Thus, offering a scalable and a more robust solution for unmanned ground vehicles navigation tasks.

</details>


### [267] [Concurrent Prehensile and Nonprehensile Manipulation: A Practical Approach to Multi-Stage Dexterous Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11655)
*Hao Jiang,Yue Wu,Yue Wang,Gaurav S. Sukhatme,Daniel Seita*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DexMulti enables sample-efficient dexterous multi-task manipulation by decomposing demonstrations into object-centric skills with defined boundaries and a retrieve-align-execute loop, achieving 66% success on training objects with only 3–4 demos per object, and generalizing to held-out objects and +/-25 cm spatial variations across two hands, outperforming diffusion-policy baselines by 2–3x.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the data inefficiency and generalization gap in learning long-horizon, contact-rich dexterous manipulation. End-to-end policies require extensive demonstrations and struggle to generalize across object geometries and placements. A skill-based retrieval framework aims to improve sample efficiency and transferability.

Method: Demonstrations are broken into object-centric skills with temporal boundaries. At test time, the method retrieves demonstrated skills based on current object geometry, aligns them to the observed state using an uncertainty-aware estimator that tracks centroid and yaw, and executes via a retrieve-align-execute loop. Evaluated on three multi-stage tasks (Grasp+Pull, Grasp+Open, Grasp+Grasp) across two dexterous hands (Allegro and LEAP) in real-world trials (>1,000).

Result: Average success of 66% on training objects with 3–4 demonstrations per object; outperforms diffusion policy baselines by 2–3x while using far fewer demonstrations; shows robust generalization to held-out objects and spatial variations up to ±25 cm.

Conclusion: DexMulti demonstrates a sample-efficient framework for real-world dexterous multi-task manipulation by leveraging object-centric skills and retrieval-based execution, achieving competitive success with limited demonstrations and robust generalization. Potential limitations include reliance on object-geometry-based retrieval, and questions remain about scaling to more diverse tasks and object categories.

Abstract: Dexterous hands enable concurrent prehensile and nonprehensile manipulation, such as holding one object while interacting with another, a capability essential for everyday tasks yet underexplored in robotics. Learning such long-horizon, contact-rich multi-stage behaviors is challenging because demonstrations are expensive to collect and end-to-end policies require substantial data to generalize across varied object geometries and placements. We present DexMulti, a sample-efficient approach for real-world dexterous multi-task manipulation that decomposes demonstrations into object-centric skills with well-defined temporal boundaries. Rather than learning monolithic policies, our method retrieves demonstrated skills based on current object geometry, aligns them to the observed object state using an uncertainty-aware estimator that tracks centroid and yaw, and executes them via a retrieve-align-execute paradigm. We evaluate on three multi-stage tasks requiring concurrent manipulation (Grasp + Pull, Grasp + Open, and Grasp + Grasp) across two dexterous hands (Allegro and LEAP) in over 1,000 real-world trials. Our approach achieves an average success rate of 66% on training objects with only 3-4 demonstrations per object, outperforming diffusion policy baselines by 2-3x while requiring far fewer demonstrations. Results demonstrate robust generalization to held-out objects and spatial variations up to +/-25 cm.

</details>


### [268] [Coupling Tensor Trains with Graph of Convex Sets: Effective Compression, Exploration, and Planning in the C-Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11658)
*Gerhard Reinerth,Riddhiman Laha,Marcello Romano*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TANGO merges Tensor Train-based compression with a GCS-like graph planner to enable scalable, geometry-aware trajectory generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimization-based planners struggle with high- dimensional configuration spaces due to the reliance on convex decompositions; a compact, expressive representation of feasible configurations can enable faster planning and better scalability.

Method: Approximate the feasible configuration space with Tensor Train decomposition, identify task-relevant regions, and embed them into a Graph of Convex Sets–like structure to produce geometry-aware trajectories.

Result: Simulation studies on planar and real robots demonstrate effective compression of the configuration space and generation of higher-quality trajectories.

Conclusion: Joint tensor-based compression and structured graph reasoning yield efficient, scalable motion planning and pave the way for more expressive configuration-space representations in robotics.

Abstract: We present TANGO (Tensor ANd Graph Optimization), a novel motion planning framework that integrates tensor-based compression with structured graph optimization to enable efficient and scalable trajectory generation. While optimization-based planners such as the Graph of Convex Sets (GCS) offer powerful tools for generating smooth, optimal trajectories, they typically rely on a predefined convex characterization of the high-dimensional configuration space-a requirement that is often intractable for general robotic tasks. TANGO builds further by using Tensor Train decomposition to approximate the feasible configuration space in a compressed form, enabling rapid discovery and estimation of task-relevant regions. These regions are then embedded into a GCS-like structure, allowing for geometry-aware motion planning that respects both system constraints and environmental complexity. By coupling tensor-based compression with structured graph reasoning, TANGO enables efficient, geometry-aware motion planning and lays the groundwork for more expressive and scalable representations of configuration space in future robotic systems. Rigorous simulation studies on planar and real robots reinforce our claims of effective compression and higher quality trajectories.

</details>


### [269] [Energy Prediction on Sloping Ground for Quadruped Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11963)
*Mohamed Ounally,Cyrille Pierre,Johann Laconte*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A compact, onboard-sensor energy model for quadrupeds predicting energy cost as a near-linear function of slope, with higher lateral costs and additive segment behavior; validated on natural terrain and suitable for path-level energy planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Energy management and endurance in outdoor legged robots; need a simple model using standard sensors that works in unexplored environments without specialized instrumentation.

Method: Field-derived model identified from runs on a commercial quadruped; expresses energy as a compact function of slope angle and heading; relies only on standard onboard sensors; field validation on natural terrain; examines additivity of costs across trajectory segments.

Result: Energy cost shows near-linear dependence on slope; lateral (sideways) costs are consistently higher; costs add across trajectory segments, enabling path-level energy prediction.

Conclusion: The model enables planning-oriented energy estimation using readily available sensor data and is applicable to unexplored outdoor terrains, supporting energy-aware routing and mission planning for legged robots.

Abstract: Energy management is a fundamental challenge for legged robots in outdoor environments. Endurance directly constrains mission success, while efficient resource use reduces ecological impact. This paper investigates how terrain slope and heading orientation influence the energetic cost of quadruped locomotion. We introduce a simple energy model that relies solely on standard onboard sensors, avoids specialized instrumentation, and remains applicable in previously unexplored environments. The model is identified from field runs on a commercial quadruped and expressed as a compact function of slope angle and heading. Field validation on natural terrain shows near-linear trends of force-equivalent cost with slope angle, consistently higher lateral costs, and additive behavior across trajectory segments, supporting path-level energy prediction for planning-oriented evaluation.

</details>


### [270] [Learning Visuomotor Policy for Multi-Robot Laser Tag Game](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11980)
*Kai Li,Shiyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: End-to-end visuomotor policy for multi-robot laser tag using distillation from a multi-agent teacher; permutation-invariant features and depth heatmaps improve performance; achieves higher hitting accuracy and collision avoidance; demonstrated on real robots; code to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classic modular approaches to multi-robot visual-control tasks suffer from limited observability, reliance on depth mapping, and inter-robot communication; an end-to-end approach with vision-based control can overcome these limitations and leverage learned representations.

Method: Train a high-performing teacher policy with multi-agent reinforcement learning and distill its knowledge into a vision-based student policy. Use a permutation-invariant feature extractor and depth heatmap input to improve performance over standard architectures; map images directly to robot actions in an end-to-end visuomotor policy.

Result: Student policy matches or exceeds teacher performance, with a 16.7% improvement in hitting accuracy and 6% improvement in collision avoidance compared to classic methods; successfully deployed on real robots; code to be released.

Conclusion: End-to-end visuomotor policies with permutation-invariant representations and depth-based inputs can outperform modular pipelines in multi-robot shooting-game scenarios; multi-agent RL distillation enhances real-world performance and enables deployment.

Abstract: In this paper, we study multi robot laser tag, a simplified yet practical shooting-game-style task. Classic modular approaches on these tasks face challenges such as limited observability and reliance on depth mapping and inter robot communication. To overcome these issues, we present an end-to-end visuomotor policy that maps images directly to robot actions. We train a high performing teacher policy with multi agent reinforcement learning and distill its knowledge into a vision-based student policy. Technical designs, including a permutation-invariant feature extractor and depth heatmap input, improve performance over standard architectures. Our policy outperforms classic methods by 16.7% in hitting accuracy and 6% in collision avoidance, and is successfully deployed on real robots. Code will be released publicly.

</details>


### [271] [Sim-to-reality adaptation for Deep Reinforcement Learning applied to an underwater docking application](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12020)
*Alaaeddine Chaarani,Narcis Palomeras,Pere Ridao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DRL-based autonomous docking for a Girona AUV using a high-fidelity digital twin and multiprocessing RL to bridge sim-to-real gaps and cut training latency; achieves strong sim results and validates in a tank.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the sim-to-real gap and long training times in autonomous underwater docking under unpredictable environments by leveraging a high-fidelity digital twin and accelerated RL training.

Method: Adapt Stonefish simulator into a multiprocessing RL framework; train a 6-DoF PPO policy in a headless environment with randomized initial poses; incorporate realistic AUV dynamics, collision models, and sensor noise; reward shaping on distance, orientation, action smoothness, and adaptive collision penalties to enable soft docking.

Result: Simulation success rate >90%; physical test-tank validation confirms sim-to-real adaptation; emergent behaviors such as pitch-based braking and yaw oscillations facilitate mechanical alignment.

Conclusion: The proposed workflow demonstrates effective sim-to-real transfer for autonomous underwater docking, with accelerated training and robust policy performance; potential future work includes addressing generalization, scalability, and safety considerations.

Abstract: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) offers a robust alternative to traditional control methods for autonomous underwater docking, particularly in adapting to unpredictable environmental conditions. However, bridging the "sim-to-real" gap and managing high training latencies remain significant bottlenecks for practical deployment. This paper presents a systematic approach for autonomous docking using the Girona Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) by leveraging a high-fidelity digital twin environment. We adapted the Stonefish simulator into a multiprocessing RL framework to significantly accelerate the learning process while incorporating realistic AUV dynamics, collision models, and sensor noise. Using the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm, we developed a 6-DoF control policy trained in a headless environment with randomized starting positions to ensure generalized performance. Our reward structure accounts for distance, orientation, action smoothness, and adaptive collision penalties to facilitate soft docking. Experimental results demonstrate that the agent achieved a success rate of over 90% in simulation. Furthermore, successful validation in a physical test tank confirmed the efficacy of the sim-to-reality adaptation, with the DRL controller exhibiting emergent behaviors such as pitch-based braking and yaw oscillations to assist in mechanical alignment.

</details>


### [272] [Flight through Narrow Gaps with Morphing-Wing Drones](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12059)
*Julius Wanner,Hoang-Vu Phan,Charbel Toumieh,Dario Floreano*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Morphing-wing drone uses mid-flight wing sweep to pass gaps narrower than wingspan; combines aerodynamic modeling with NMPC to handle lift loss at low speeds, validated on a 130 g hardware drone with ~5 cm altitude error during 5–7 m/s gap-passage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome fixed-wing gap constraints by imitating avian maneuvers to traverse sub-wingspan gaps; address control challenges from lift loss and need for precisely timed wing-sweep actuation.

Method: Develop a general aerodynamic model for wing-sweep morphing flight at low speeds and post-stall angles of attack; integrate longitudinal dynamics into an optimal reference trajectory generation and nonlinear model predictive control with runtime adaptive costs/constraints; validate on a 130 g morphing drone, ensuring fully swept wings near the gap and analyzing trajectory.

Result: Average altitude error ~5 cm during narrow-gap passage at forward speeds 5–7 m/s; successful fully swept wings near the gap across variable threshold distances; lift loss compensated by accelerating and pitching upwards ahead of the gap; near-constant forward speed and altitude during passage; hardware demonstration of accurate gap passage.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of accurate sub-wingspan gap passage with morphing-wing drones using an integrated aerodynamic model and NMPC framework, enabling near-constant forward speed and altitude during gap traversal across different threshold distances.

Abstract: The size of a narrow gap traversable by a fixed-wing drone is limited by its wingspan. Inspired by birds, here, we enable the traversal of a gap of sub-wingspan width and height using a morphing-wing drone capable of temporarily sweeping in its wings mid-flight. This maneuver poses control challenges due to sudden lift loss during gap-passage at low flight speeds and the need for precisely timed wing-sweep actuation ahead of the gap. To address these challenges, we first develop an aerodynamic model for general wing-sweep morphing drone flight including low flight speeds and post-stall angles of attack. We integrate longitudinal drone dynamics into an optimal reference trajectory generation and Nonlinear Model Predictive Control framework with runtime adaptive costs and constraints. Validated on a 130 g wing-sweep-morphing drone, our method achieves an average altitude error of 5 cm during narrow-gap passage at forward speeds between 5 and 7 m/s, whilst enforcing fully swept wings near the gap across variable threshold distances. Trajectory analysis shows that the drone can compensate for lift loss during gap-passage by accelerating and pitching upwards ahead of the gap to an extent that differs between reference trajectory optimization objectives. We show that our strategy also allows for accurate gap passage on hardware whilst maintaining a constant forward flight speed reference and near-constant altitude.

</details>


### [273] [Decentralized Cooperative Localization for Multi-Robot Systems with Asynchronous Sensor Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12075)
*Nivand Khosravi,Niusha Khosravi,Mohammad Bozorg,Masoud S. Bahraini*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A decentralized cooperative localization (DCL) framework where each robot localizes via EKF, sharing measurement information only when communication links exist and companion robots are detected by LiDAR; maintains cross-correlation consistency, handles asynchronous data and dynamic maneuvers, and enables initialization in arbitrary reference frames with automatic alignment. It introduces a dual-landmark scheme using static features and mobile robots to improve robustness in feature-sparse environments. Experimental results show DCL outperforms centralized localization (CCL) with up to 34% RMSE reduction, and the dual-landmark variant yields up to 56% improvement, applicable to enclosed spaces and feature-sparse terrains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GPS-denied environments and limited, intermittent communication require a localization approach that (i) preserves cross-correlation between agents, (ii) handles asynchronous, heterogeneous sensor data, (iii) tolerates dynamic maneuvers and unknown initial orientations, and (iv) remains robust in feature-sparse environments where traditional methods fail.

Method: Each robot runs a local Extended Kalman Filter for ego-localization. Measurements are shared during update steps only when a communication link is available and companion robots are detected by LiDAR. The framework maintains cross-correlation consistency across agents despite asynchronous data. It supports arbitrary initial orientations via transformation matrices in prediction and update stages, enabling automatic frame alignment. A dual-landmark evaluation framework leverages both static environmental features and other mobile robots as dynamic landmarks to bolster robustness in sparse-feature settings.

Result: In Gazebo and real basement experiments, DCL achieved a 34% reduction in RMSE compared to centralized cooperative localization (CCL). The dual-landmark variant improved RMSE by 56%.

Conclusion: DCL provides a robust, communication-adaptive localization paradigm for multi-robot systems operating in GPS-denied, feature-sparse, or dynamic environments. It preserves cross-correlation, handles asynchronous data, supports arbitrary frame initialization with automatic alignment, and benefits from dual-landmark information, outperforming centralized approaches in challenging domains.

Abstract: Decentralized cooperative localization (DCL) is a promising approach for nonholonomic mobile robots operating in GPS-denied environments with limited communication infrastructure. This paper presents a DCL framework in which each robot performs localization locally using an Extended Kalman Filter, while sharing measurement information during update stages only when communication links are available and companion robots are successfully detected by LiDAR. The framework preserves cross-correlation consistency among robot state estimates while handling asynchronous sensor data with heterogeneous sampling rates and accommodating accelerations during dynamic maneuvers. Unlike methods that require pre-aligned coordinate systems, the proposed approach allows robots to initialize with arbitrary reference-frame orientations and achieves automatic alignment through transformation matrices in both the prediction and update stages. To improve robustness in feature-sparse environments, we introduce a dual-landmark evaluation framework that exploits both static environmental features and mobile robots as dynamic landmarks. The proposed framework enables reliable detection and feature extraction during sharp turns, while prediction accuracy is improved through information sharing from mutual observations. Experimental results in both Gazebo simulation and real-world basement environments show that DCL outperforms centralized cooperative localization (CCL), achieving a 34% reduction in RMSE, while the dual-landmark variant yields an improvement of 56%. These results demonstrate the applicability of DCL to challenging domains such as enclosed spaces, underwater environments, and feature-sparse terrains where conventional localization methods are ineffective.

</details>


### [274] [CRAFT: A Tendon-Driven Hand with Hybrid Hard-Soft Compliance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12120)
*Leo Lin,Shivansh Patel,Jay Moon,Svetlana Lazebnik,Unnat Jain*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A low-cost, tendon-driven anthropomorphic hand (CRAFT) with hybrid hard-soft joints and rolling-contact surfaces; achieves full Feix grasps (33/33) and improved strength/endurance for contact-rich tasks; open-source under $600.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robust contact-rich manipulation by combining rigid load-bearing links with soft, joint-localized compliance, addressing impact concentration at joints and needing repeatable motion paths; also aims for teleoperation-friendly, low-cost, open-source hardware.

Method: Design details: tendon-driven actuators with 15 motors distributed on the fingers; joints feature soft material at joints while links remain rigid; rolling-contact joint surfaces to constrain motion along repeatable paths; full hand capable of 33/33 grasps per Feix taxonomy; project includes teleoperation and simulation integration; cost below $600 and open-source release.

Result: Structural tests show improved strength and endurance with repeatability comparable to baselines. In teleoperation, better handling of fragile and low-friction items; achieves full coverage of Feix 33/33 grasps. Open-source release with vision-based teleoperation and simulation integration; project page provided.

Conclusion: CRAFT demonstrates a practical, low-cost, tendon-driven hand with hybrid compliance and rolling joints that excels in contact-rich tasks and teleoperation, offering full grasp taxonomy coverage and accessible open-source availability.

Abstract: We introduce CRAFT hand, a tendon-driven anthropomorphic hand with hybrid hard-soft compliance for contact-rich manipulation. The design is based on a simple idea: contact is not uniform across the hand. Impacts concentrate at joints, while links carry most of the load. CRAFT places soft material at joints and keeps links rigid, and uses rollingcontact joint surfaces to keep flexion on repeatable motion paths. Fifteen motors mounted on the fingers drive the hand through tendons, keeping the form factor compact and the fingers light. In structural tests, CRAFT improves strength and endurance while maintaining comparable repeatability. In teleoperation, CRAFT improves handling of fragile and low-friction items, and the hand covers 33/33 grasps in the Feix taxonomy. The full design costs under $600 and will be released open-source with visionbased teleoperation and simulation integration. Project page: http://craft-hand.github.io/

</details>


### [275] [ComFree-Sim: A GPU-Parallelized Analytical Contact Physics Engine for Scalable Contact-Rich Robotics Simulation and Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12185)
*Chetan Borse,Zhixian Xie,Wei-Cheng Huang,Wanxin Jin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A GPU-accelerated, complementarity-free contact engine (ComFree-Sim) achieves near-linear scaling with contact count by solving contact impulses in closed form using a dual-cone impedance update; it supports a unified 6D friction model, integrates as a MuJoCo-compatible backend (MJWarp), and enables real-time control in dexterous manipulation and motion retargeting with higher throughput and comparable fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional physics engines enforce non-penetration and Coulomb friction via complementarity constraints with per-step iterative solvers. The cost grows superlinearly with the number of contacts, hindering real-time control in contact-rich robotics.

Method: Analytical contact impulse computation in closed form via an impedance-style prediction–correction update in the dual cone of Coulomb friction. Decoupled across contact pairs and across cone facets, enabling GPU kernel execution. Extends to a unified 6D contact model (tangential, torsional, rolling friction) with a practical dual-cone impedance heuristic. Implemented in Warp with a MuJoCo-compatible interface as a drop-in MJWarp backend.

Result: Experiments show near-linear runtime scaling with number of contacts and 2–3× higher throughput than MJWarp in dense scenes, with comparable physical fidelity. Real-time MPC for in-hand dexterous manipulation on a LEAP hand and dynamics-aware motion retargeting demonstrate that low-latency simulation improves closed-loop success and enables high-frequency control in contact-rich tasks.

Conclusion: ComFree-Sim delivers scalable, low-latency, physically faithful contact simulation suitable for real-time control in contact-rich robotics, addressing a key bottleneck in simulation-to-control pipelines and enabling practical high-frequency control.

Abstract: Physics simulation for contact-rich robotics is often bottlenecked by contact resolution: mainstream engines enforce non-penetration and Coulomb friction via complementarity constraints or constrained optimization, requiring per-step iterative solves whose cost grows superlinearly with contact density. We present ComFree-Sim, a GPU-parallelized analytical contact physics engine built on complementarity-free contact modeling. ComFree-Sim computes contact impulses in closed form via an impedance-style prediction--correction update in the dual cone of Coulomb friction. Contact computation decouples across contact pairs and becomes separable across cone facets, mapping naturally to GPU kernels and yielding near-linear runtime scaling with the number of contacts. We further extend the formulation to a unified 6D contact model capturing tangential, torsional, and rolling friction, and introduce a practical dual-cone impedance heuristic. ComFree-Sim is implemented in Warp and exposed through a MuJoCo-compatible interface as a drop-in backend alternative to MuJoCo Warp (MJWarp). Experiments benchmark penetration, friction behaviors, stability, and simulation runtime scaling against MJWarp, demonstrating near-linear scaling and 2--3 times higher throughput in dense contact scenes with comparable physical fidelity. We deploy ComFree-Sim in real-time MPC for in-hand dexterous manipulation on a real-world multi-fingered LEAP hand and in dynamics-aware motion retargeting, demonstrating that low-latency simulation yields higher closed-loop success rates and enables practical high-frequency control in contact-rich tasks.

</details>


### [276] [SaPaVe: Towards Active Perception and Manipulation in Vision-Language-Action Models for Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12193)
*Mengzhen Liu,Enshen Zhou,Cheng Chi,Yi Han,Shanyu Rong,Liming Chen,Pengwei Wang,Zhongyuan Wang,Shanghang Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SaPaVe is a decoupled, data-efficient framework for semantic active perception and manipulation, featuring bottom-up training, new datasets (ActiveViewPose-200K, ActiveManip-Bench), a 3D geometry-aware module, and strong real-world results, outperforming recent models by up to 31.25% in success rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the mismatch between semantic-driven active perception and robust, viewpoint-invariant manipulation execution, aiming for data-efficient training and generalization in complex scenes.

Method: SaPaVe decouples camera control from manipulation actions, uses a bottom-up training strategy (semantic camera control trained on a large-scale dataset, then joint optimization with hybrid data), introduces ActiveViewPose-200K for semantic camera movement learning, adds a 3D geometry-aware module for robustness across viewpoints, and provides ActiveManip-Bench as a benchmark for active manipulation beyond fixed views.

Result: Empirical results show SaPaVe outperforms recent vision-language-action models (e.g., GR00T N1 and π0) with up to 31.25% higher real-world task success rates; demonstrated robustness to dynamic viewpoints; new datasets and benchmark to support research; validated in both simulation and real-world settings.

Conclusion: Tightly coupled perception and execution, when trained with decoupled yet coordinated strategies, yields efficient and generalizable active manipulation; the proposed datasets and benchmark support broader research in semantic active perception and robust manipulation.

Abstract: Active perception and manipulation are crucial for robots to interact with complex scenes. Existing methods struggle to unify semantic-driven active perception with robust, viewpoint-invariant execution. We propose SaPaVe, an end-to-end framework that jointly learns these capabilities in a data-efficient manner. Our approach decouples camera and manipulation actions rather than placing them in a shared action space, and follows a bottom-up training strategy: we first train semantic camera control on a large-scale dataset, then jointly optimize both action types using hybrid data. To support this framework, we introduce ActiveViewPose-200K, a dataset of 200k image-language-camera movement pairs for semantic camera movement learning, and a 3D geometry-aware module that improves execution robustness under dynamic viewpoints. We also present ActiveManip-Bench, the first benchmark for evaluating active manipulation beyond fixed-view settings. Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world environments show that SaPaVe outperforms recent vision-language-action models such as GR00T N1 and \(π_0\), achieving up to 31.25\% higher success rates in real-world tasks. These results show that tightly coupled perception and execution, when trained with decoupled yet coordinated strategies, enable efficient and generalizable active manipulation. Project page: https://lmzpai.github.io/SaPaVe

</details>


### [277] [HandelBot: Real-World Piano Playing via Fast Adaptation of Dexterous Robot Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12243)
*Amber Xie,Haozhi Qi,Dorsa Sadigh*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HandelBot combines a sim-trained policy with a two-stage adaptation: (1) a structured refinement that adjusts lateral finger joints using physical rollouts to fix spatial alignment, and (2) residual reinforcement learning to learn fine-grained corrective actions. It achieves millimeter-scale precision in bimanual piano playing, outperforming direct sim deployment by 1.8x with only 30 minutes of real data across five songs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dexterous multi-finger manipulation is data-intensive; sim-to-real transfers often fail at millimeter precision tasks such as bimanual piano playing. Reducing the required real-world data while achieving high precision remains a core challenge.

Method: Stage 1: From a simulation-trained policy, perform structured refinement by adjusting lateral finger joints based on physical rollouts to correct spatial alignments. Stage 2: Use residual reinforcement learning to autonomously learn fine-grained corrective actions on top of the refined policy. Hardware experiments across five songs validate the approach.

Result: The approach successfully performs precise bimanual piano playing, outperforming direct simulation deployment by a factor of 1.8x and requiring only 30 minutes of physical interaction data.

Conclusion: A two-stage sim-to-real adaptation—structured refinement plus residual RL—enables mm-scale precision in dexterous manipulation with limited real data, suggesting broad applicability to other fine-grained manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Mastering dexterous manipulation with multi-fingered hands has been a grand challenge in robotics for decades. Despite its potential, the difficulty of collecting high-quality data remains a primary bottleneck for high-precision tasks. While reinforcement learning and simulation-to-real-world transfer offer a promising alternative, the transferred policies often fail for tasks demanding millimeter-scale precision, such as bimanual piano playing. In this work, we introduce HandelBot, a framework that combines a simulation policy and rapid adaptation through a two-stage pipeline. Starting from a simulation-trained policy, we first apply a structured refinement stage to correct spatial alignments by adjusting lateral finger joints based on physical rollouts. Next, we use residual reinforcement learning to autonomously learn fine-grained corrective actions. Through extensive hardware experiments across five recognized songs, we demonstrate that HandelBot can successfully perform precise bimanual piano playing. Our system outperforms direct simulation deployment by a factor of 1.8x and requires only 30 minutes of physical interaction data.

</details>


### [278] [HumDex:Humanoid Dexterous Manipulation Made Easy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12260)
*Liang Heng,Yihe Tang,Jiajun Xu,Henghui Bao,Di Huang,Yue Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HumDex provides a portable, IMU-based teleoperation system for humanoid whole-body dexterous manipulation, with a learning-based retargeting method and a two-stage imitation learning framework that improves generalization while reducing data needs; fully open-source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The main bottleneck is collecting high-quality demonstration data for complex whole-body dexterous manipulation, aggravated by teleoperation systems that lack portability, experience occlusions, or suffer from insufficient precision.

Method: 1) IMU-based motion capture for accurate, portable full-body tracking. 2) Learning-based retargeting for smooth, natural dexterous hand motions without manual parameter tuning. 3) A two-stage imitation learning pipeline: pre-train on diverse human motion data to learn general priors, then fine-tune on robot data to bridge embodiment gaps and achieve precise execution.

Result: The approach significantly improves generalization to new configurations, objects, and backgrounds with minimal data acquisition costs, and the framework is fully reproducible and open-sourced.

Conclusion: HumDex effectively combines portable full-body tracking with intelligent hand retargeting and a data-efficient imitation learning strategy to enable accessible, high-fidelity humanoid dexterous manipulation, supported by open-source release.

Abstract: This paper investigates humanoid whole-body dexterous manipulation, where the efficient collection of high-quality demonstration data remains a central bottleneck.
  Existing teleoperation systems often suffer from limited portability, occlusion, or insufficient precision, which hinders their applicability to complex whole-body tasks. To address these challenges, we introduce HumDex, a portable teleoperation system designed for humanoid whole-body dexterous manipulation. Our system leverages IMU-based motion tracking to address the portability-precision trade-off, enabling accurate full-body tracking while remaining easy to deploy. For dexterous hand control, we further introduce a learning-based retargeting method that generates smooth and natural hand motions without manual parameter tuning. Beyond teleoperation, HumDex enables efficient collection of human motion data. Building on this capability, we propose a two-stage imitation learning framework that first pre-trains on diverse human motion data to learn generalizable priors, and then fine-tunes on robot data to bridge the embodiment gap for precise execution. We demonstrate that this approach significantly improves generalization to new configurations, objects, and backgrounds with minimal data acquisition costs. The entire system is fully reproducible and open-sourced at https://github.com/physical-superintelligence-lab/HumDex.

</details>


### [279] [$Ψ_0$: An Open Foundation Model Towards Universal Humanoid Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12263)
*Songlin Wei,Hongyi Jing,Boqian Li,Zhenyu Zhao,Jiageng Mao,Zhenhao Ni,Sicheng He,Jie Liu,Xiawei Liu,Kaidi Kang,Sheng Zang,Weiduo Yuan,Marco Pavone,Di Huang,Yue Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Ψ0 is a two-stage humanoid loco-manipulation foundation model: pre-train a visual-language-motion backbone on egocentric human videos, then post-train a flow-based action expert on real humanoid data, achieving superior sample efficiency and performance (≈800h human + 30h robot data), outperforming data-heavy baselines by >40%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humanoid loco-manipulation suffers from kinematic disparities between humans and robots; simply scaling data from diverse sources is inefficient; need a data-efficient transfer strategy and a tailored data recipe.

Method: Staged learning: autoregressive pre-training of a VLM backbone on large-scale egocentric human manipulation data; post-training a flow-based action expert on domain-specific humanoid trajectories; emphasizes a data recipe that prioritizes high-quality egocentric human data before real-world humanoid data; plan to open-source pipeline.

Result: Real-world experiments show superior performance with about 800 hours of human video data and 30 hours of robot data; outperforms baselines trained on >10x more data by over 40% in overall success rate across multiple tasks.

Conclusion: Open-sourcing the ecosystem: data processing/training pipeline, a humanoid foundation model, and a real-time action inference engine; claims strong data-efficiency gains and practical impact.

Abstract: We introduce $Ψ_0$ (Psi-Zero), an open foundation model to address challenging humanoid loco-manipulation tasks. While existing approaches often attempt to address this fundamental problem by co-training on large and diverse human and humanoid data, we argue that this strategy is suboptimal due to the fundamental kinematic and motion disparities between humans and humanoid robots. Therefore, data efficiency and model performance remain unsatisfactory despite the considerable data volume. To address this challenge, \ours\;decouples the learning process to maximize the utility of heterogeneous data sources. Specifically, we propose a staged training paradigm with different learning objectives: First, we autoregressively pre-train a VLM backbone on large-scale egocentric human videos to acquire generalizable visual-action representations. Then, we post-train a flow-based action expert on high-quality humanoid robot data to learn precise robot joint control. Our research further identifies a critical yet often overlooked data recipe: in contrast to approaches that scale with noisy Internet clips or heterogeneous cross-embodiment robot datasets, we demonstrate that pre-training on high-quality egocentric human manipulation data followed by post-training on domain-specific real-world humanoid trajectories yields superior performance. Extensive real-world experiments demonstrate that \ours\ achieves the best performance using only about 800 hours of human video data and 30 hours of real-world robot data, outperforming baselines pre-trained on more than 10$\times$ as much data by over 40\% in overall success rate across multiple tasks. We will open-source the entire ecosystem to the community, including a data processing and training pipeline, a humanoid foundation model, and a real-time action inference engine.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [280] [DIVE: Scaling Diversity in Agentic Task Synthesis for Generalizable Tool Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11076)
*Aili Chen,Chi Zhang,Junteng Liu,Jiangjie Chen,Chengyu Du,Yunji Li,Ming Zhong,Qin Wang,Zhengmao Zhu,Jiayuan Song,Ke Ji,Junxian He,Pengyu Zhao,Yanghua Xiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DIVE inverts synthesis order to ground task generation in real-world tool traces, enabling diverse, verifiable tasks and robust OOD generalization for tool-using LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current post-training tool-using LLMs generalize poorly when task/toolsets shift because synthesized tasks lack diversity. Scaling diversity is hard due to the need for executability and verifiability, while OOD generalization requires broad tool-type coverage and varied usage patterns.

Method: DIVE executes diverse, real-world tools first and derives the set of tasks strictly entailed by the resulting traces, creating grounded task data. It scales diversity along two axes (tool-pool coverage and per-task toolset variety) and adds an Evidence Collection–Task Derivation loop that induces multi-step tool use across 373 tools in five domains. The model is trained on DIVE data (48k SFT + 3.2k RL) for Qwen3-8B.

Result: Training on DIVE data yields +22 average points across 9 OOD benchmarks and +68 over the strongest 8B baseline. A controlled scaling analysis shows diversity scaling outperforms quantity scaling for OOD generalization, even when data is reduced by 4x.

Conclusion: Grounded by construction, DIVE enables richer, more diverse tool-use patterns and robust OOD generalization by prioritizing diversity scaling of tool usage over mere data quantity; the approach is scalable to large toolsets and multiple domains.

Abstract: Recent work synthesizes agentic tasks for post-training tool-using LLMs, yet robust generalization under shifts in tasks and toolsets remains an open challenge. We trace this brittleness to insufficient diversity in synthesized tasks. Scaling diversity is difficult because training requires tasks to remain executable and verifiable, while generalization demands coverage of diverse tool types, toolset combinations, and heterogeneous tool-use patterns. We propose DIVE, an evidence-driven recipe that inverts synthesis order, executing diverse, real-world tools first and reverse-deriving tasks strictly entailed by the resulting traces, thereby providing grounding by construction. DIVE scales structural diversity along two controllable axes, tool-pool coverage and per-task toolset variety, and an Evidence Collection--Task Derivation loop further induces rich multi-step tool-use patterns across 373 tools in five domains. Training Qwen3-8B on DIVE data (48k SFT + 3.2k RL) improves by +22 average points across 9 OOD benchmarks and outperforms the strongest 8B baseline by +68. Remarkably, controlled scaling analysis reveals that diversity scaling consistently outperforms quantity scaling for OOD generalization, even with 4x less data.

</details>


### [281] [A Survey of Reasoning in Autonomous Driving Systems: Open Challenges and Emerging Paradigms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11093)
*Kejin Yu,Yuhan Sun,Taiqiang Wu,Ruixu Zhang,Zhiqiang Lin,Yuxin Meng,Junjie Wang,Yujiu Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A comprehensive review arguing that high-level autonomous driving should be driven by robust, generalizable reasoning powered by LLMs/MLLMs. It introduces a Cognitive Hierarchy to decompose driving tasks, identifies seven core reasoning challenges, and analyzes system-centric and evaluation-centric approaches, highlighting a shift toward interpretable glass-box agents and outlining key future directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous driving systems excel at perception in structured environments but fail in long-tail scenarios and complex social interactions. LLMs/MLLMs offer a cognitive engine to enable human-like reasoning, but there is no systematic framework to integrate them into driving. A structured approach is needed to elevate reasoning to the core of the system.

Method: Proposes a Cognitive Hierarchy to decompose driving tasks by cognitive/interactive complexity; derives seven core reasoning challenges (e.g., responsiveness-reasoning trade-off, social-game reasoning); conducts a dual-perspective review of system-centric agent architectures and evaluation-centric validation practices; synthesizes trends toward glass-box, interpretable agents; discusses limitations and future directions.

Result: Identifies a fundamental tension between deliberative, high-latency LLM-based reasoning and the millisecond-scale safety requirements of vehicle control; presents a holistic framework and taxonomy that guides integration of cognitive reasoning into AD; highlights the trend toward interpretable agents and neuro-symbolic directions for future work.

Conclusion: Bridging symbolic reasoning with physical control is essential. Future work should develop verifiable neuro-symbolic architectures, robust reasoning under uncertainty, and scalable models for implicit social negotiation to enable reliable, human-like autonomous driving.

Abstract: The development of high-level autonomous driving (AD) is shifting from perception-centric limitations to a more fundamental bottleneck, namely, a deficit in robust and generalizable reasoning. Although current AD systems manage structured environments, they consistently falter in long-tail scenarios and complex social interactions that require human-like judgment. Meanwhile, the advent of large language and multimodal models (LLMs and MLLMs) presents a transformative opportunity to integrate a powerful cognitive engine into AD systems, moving beyond pattern matching toward genuine comprehension. However, a systematic framework to guide this integration is critically lacking. To bridge this gap, we provide a comprehensive review of this emerging field and argue that reasoning should be elevated from a modular component to the system's cognitive core. Specifically, we first propose a novel Cognitive Hierarchy to decompose the monolithic driving task according to its cognitive and interactive complexity. Building on this, we further derive and systematize seven core reasoning challenges, such as the responsiveness-reasoning trade-off and social-game reasoning. Furthermore, we conduct a dual-perspective review of the state-of-the-art, analyzing both system-centric approaches to architecting intelligent agents and evaluation-centric practices for their validation. Our analysis reveals a clear trend toward holistic and interpretable "glass-box" agents. In conclusion, we identify a fundamental and unresolved tension between the high-latency, deliberative nature of LLM-based reasoning and the millisecond-scale, safety-critical demands of vehicle control. For future work, a primary objective is to bridge the symbolic-to-physical gap by developing verifiable neuro-symbolic architectures, robust reasoning under uncertainty, and scalable models for implicit social negotiation.

</details>


### [282] [PACED: Distillation at the Frontier of Student Competence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11178)
*Yuanda Xu,Hejian Sang,Zhengze Zhou,Ran He,Zhipeng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Paced: a Beta-kernel pass-rate weighted distillation that focuses learning on the frontier of a model's competence to improve efficiency and accuracy, with theoretical guarantees and robust gains across distillation and self-distillation setups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LLM distillation wastes compute on problems already mastered or far beyond the student; the gradient signal vanishes at pass-rate extremes, causing inefficiency. A principled method is needed to concentrate learning on the zone of proximal development.

Method: Introduce Paced, a pass-rate weight w(p)=p^α(1-p)^β derived from the boundary-vanishing SNR structure of distillation gradients. Provide theoretical results: Beta kernel arises as leading-order weight family and is minimax-robust under bounded misspecification. Empirically validate with forward KL distillation (teacher→student) and reverse KL self-distillation; explore a two-stage forward-then-reverse KL schedule. Requires only student rollouts to estimate pass rates, no architectural changes, compatible with any KL direction.

Result: Paced yields significant gains over base models in teacher→student distillation while limiting forgetting; in instruction-tuned self-distillation with reverse KL, gains exceed baselines; two-stage forward-then-reverse KL schedule provides the strongest improvements, especially on standard reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: The Beta-weighted pacing addresses a structural inefficiency in distillation by concentrating learning where it's most needed (the frontier). The method is theoretically robust and practically compatible with various KL directions, requiring only pass-rate estimates from student rollouts and no architectural changes.

Abstract: Standard LLM distillation wastes compute on two fronts: problems the student has already mastered (near-zero gradients) and problems far beyond its reach (incoherent gradients that erode existing capabilities). We show that this waste is not merely intuitive but structurally inevitable: the gradient signal-to-noise ratio in distillation provably vanishes at both pass-rate extremes. This theoretical observation leads to Paced, a framework that concentrates distillation on the zone of proximal development -- the frontier of a student model's competence -- via a principled pass-rate weight $w(p) = p^α(1 - p)^β$ derived from the boundary-vanishing structure of distillation gradients. Key results: (1) Theory: We prove that the Beta kernel $w(p) = p^α(1-p)^β$ is a leading-order weight family arising from the SNR structure of distillation, and that it is minimax-robust -- under bounded multiplicative misspecification, worst-case efficiency loss is only $O(δ^2)$. (2)Distillation: On distillation from a larger teacher to a smaller student model with forward KL, Paced achieves significant gain over the base model, while keeping benchmark forgetting at a low level. (3)Self-distillation: On instruction-tuned models with reverse KL, gains are exceeding baselines as well. (4)Two-stage synergy: A forward-KL-then-reverse-KL schedule yields the strongest results in our setting, reaching substantial improvements on standard reasoning benchmarks -- supporting a mode-coverage-then-consolidation interpretation of the distillation process. All configurations require only student rollouts to estimate pass rates, need no architectural changes, and are compatible with any KL direction.

</details>


### [283] [Measuring AI Agents' Progress on Multi-Step Cyber Attack Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11214)
*Linus Folkerts,Will Payne,Simon Inman,Philippos Giavridis,Joe Skinner,Sam Deverett,James Aung,Ekin Zorer,Michael Schmatz,Mahmoud Ghanem,John Wilkinson,Alan Steer,Vy Hong,Jessica Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Frontier AI models show scalable autonomous cyber-attack capabilities across two cyber ranges, with performance improving with compute and generational advances; corporate-range tasks progress substantially while industrial-control-system tasks remain limited.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify real-world autonomous cyber-attack capabilities of frontier AI models, specifically how performance scales with inference-time compute and across model generations.

Method: Evaluate seven models released Aug 2024–Feb 2026 on two purpose-built ranges (32-step corporate network, 7-step industrial control system). Assess effect of varying inference-time compute budgets (10M–100M tokens) on multi-step task completion, comparing performance across generations.

Result: Two main trends: (1) performance scales log-linearly with inference compute, with up to 59% gains from 10M to 100M tokens and no plateau; (2) newer model generations outperform predecessors at fixed token budgets. Corporate network: steps completed at 10M tokens rose from 1.7 (GPT-4o, Aug 2024) to 9.8 (Opus 4.6, Feb 2026); best single run completed 22 of 32 steps (~6 human-hours). Industrial control system: progress remains limited, recent models reliably complete 1.2–1.4 of 7 steps (max 3).

Conclusion: Frontier AI agents exhibit increasing autonomous cyber-attack capability with compute and model evolution, notably in corporate networks, raising security concerns and underscoring the need for robust defenses and governance; ICS results suggest domain-specific limits and safer deployment considerations.

Abstract: We evaluate the autonomous cyber-attack capabilities of frontier AI models on two purpose-built cyber ranges-a 32-step corporate network attack and a 7-step industrial control system attack-that require chaining heterogeneous capabilities across extended action sequences. By comparing seven models released over an eighteen-month period (August 2024 to February 2026) at varying inference-time compute budgets, we observe two capability trends. First, model performance scales log-linearly with inference-time compute, with no observed plateau-increasing from 10M to 100M tokens yields gains of up to 59%, requiring no specific technical sophistication from the operator. Second, each successive model generation outperforms its predecessor at fixed token budgets: on the corporate network range, average steps completed at 10M tokens rose from 1.7 (GPT-4o, August 2024) to 9.8 (Opus 4.6, February 2026). The best single run completed 22 of 32 steps, corresponding to roughly 6 of the estimated 14 hours a human expert would need. On the industrial control system range, performance remains limited, though the most recent models are the first to reliably complete steps, averaging 1.2-1.4 of 7 (max 3).

</details>


### [284] [Reversible Lifelong Model Editing via Semantic Routing-Based LoRA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11239)
*Haihua Luo,Xuming Ran,Tommi Kärkkäinen,Zhonghua Chen,Jiangrong Shen,Qi Xu,Fengyu Cong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SoLA introduces a lifelong model editing framework where each edit is a frozen LoRA module activated by semantic routing; it enables reversible rollback by removing routing keys, addressing semantic drift and forgetting while enabling end-to-end decision making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address continual model updating in LLMs without semantic drift or catastrophic forgetting; existing modular or parameter-efficient methods are insufficient and cannot support precise rollback of edits.

Method: Independent LoRA modules per edit, frozen after training; semantic routing maps inputs to relevant modules; dynamic activation of modules via semantic matching; ability to revoke edits by removing routing key; integrate decision-making into the edited layer avoiding extra routing networks.

Result: Empirical experiments show effective learning and retention of edited knowledge; accurate, efficient, and reversible lifelong model editing.

Conclusion: SoLA offers reversible lifelong model editing with semantic routing-guided module activation, reducing drift and forgetting, and enabling end-to-end decision-making; claims first reversible rollback capability in this domain.

Abstract: The dynamic evolution of real-world necessitates model editing within Large Language Models. While existing methods explore modular isolation or parameter-efficient strategies, they still suffer from semantic drift or knowledge forgetting due to continual updating. To address these challenges, we propose SoLA, a Semantic routing-based LoRA framework for lifelong model editing. In SoLA, each edit is encapsulated as an independent LoRA module, which is frozen after training and mapped to input by semantic routing, allowing dynamic activation of LoRA modules via semantic matching. This mechanism avoids semantic drift caused by cluster updating and mitigates catastrophic forgetting from parameter sharing. More importantly, SoLA supports precise revocation of specific edits by removing key from semantic routing, which restores model's original behavior. To our knowledge, this reversible rollback editing capability is the first to be achieved in existing literature. Furthermore, SoLA integrates decision-making process into edited layer, eliminating the need for auxiliary routing networks and enabling end-to-end decision-making process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SoLA effectively learns and retains edited knowledge, achieving accurate, efficient, and reversible lifelong model editing.

</details>


### [285] [Mind the Sim2Real Gap in User Simulation for Agentic Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11245)
*Xuhui Zhou,Weiwei Sun,Qianou Ma,Yiqing Xie,Jiarui Liu,Weihua Du,Sean Welleck,Yiming Yang,Graham Neubig,Sherry Tongshuang Wu,Maarten Sap*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-based user simulators show a clear Sim2Real gap: they are overly cooperative and uniform, inflating agent success compared to real users; a new User-Sim Index (USI) reveals the mismatch across 31 simulators and eight feedback dimensions, highlighting the need for human validation and improved simulation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify how faithfully LLM-based simulators replicate real user interactive behavior in multi-turn NLP tasks, addressing concerns that simulator-derived evaluation may overestimate agent performance.

Method: Conducted the first full τ-bench protocol with real humans (451 participants, 165 tasks) and benchmarked 31 LLM simulators from proprietary, open-source, and specialized families. Introduced the User-Sim Index (USI) to measure resemblance to real user behavior and feedback. Compared behavior, feedback quality across eight dimensions, and reward signals between humans and simulators.

Result: LLM simulators are excessively cooperative, stylistically uniform, and lack realistic frustration/ambiguity, creating an easy mode that inflates agent success rates beyond human baselines. Humans provide nuanced judgments across eight quality dimensions; simulators produce uniformly positive feedback. Rule-based rewards fail to capture rich human feedback. Greater general model capability does not guarantee more faithful simulation.

Conclusion: Human validation is essential when using LLM-based user simulators in the development cycle. The study motivates the creation of improved user-simulation models and more robust evaluation protocols to reduce Sim2Real gaps.

Abstract: As NLP evaluation shifts from static benchmarks to multi-turn interactive settings, LLM-based simulators have become widely used as user proxies, serving two roles: generating user turns and providing evaluation signals. Yet, these simulations are frequently assumed to be faithful to real human behaviors, often without rigorous verification. We formalize the Sim2Real gap in user simulation and present the first study running the full $τ$-bench protocol with real humans (451 participants, 165 tasks), benchmarking 31 LLM simulators across proprietary, open-source, and specialized families using the User-Sim Index (USI), a metric we introduce to quantify how well LLM simulators resemble real user interactive behaviors and feedback. Behaviorally, LLM simulators are excessively cooperative, stylistically uniform, and lack realistic frustration or ambiguity, creating an "easy mode" that inflates agent success rates above the human baseline. In evaluations, real humans provide nuanced judgments across eight quality dimensions while simulated users produce uniformly more positive feedback; rule-based rewards are failing to capture rich feedback signals generated by human users. Overall, higher general model capability does not necessarily yield more faithful user simulation. These findings highlight the importance of human validation when using LLM-based user simulators in the agent development cycle and motivate improved models for user simulation.

</details>


### [286] [The Unlearning Mirage: A Dynamic Framework for Evaluating LLM Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11266)
*Raj Sanjay Shah,Jing Huang,Keerthiram Murugesan,Nathalie Baracaldo,Diyi Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A dynamic, structured probe framework stress-tests LLM unlearning by generating simple-to-multi-hop Q&A probes from the pre-unlearning model; it reveals brittleness not captured by static benchmarks and aligns with prior evaluations, while uncovering new multi-hop failures; supports scalable evaluation and open-source release.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current unlearning methods are brittle and static benchmarks fail to detect vulnerabilities, especially with multi-hop reasoning and aliasing. There is a need for robust, scalable evaluation to ensure safety, bias mitigation, and legal compliance (e.g., right to be forgotten).

Method: Elicit knowledge from the pre-unlearning model, construct targeted probes from simple to multi-hop queries, automatically generate semantically equivalent Q&A probes, and perform activation analyses to understand computation pathways. Compare results with existing benchmarks and test for new failures; release as an open-source package.

Result: The framework achieves comparable coverage to existing benchmarks via automated semantic-equivalence probes, aligns with prior evaluations, and uncovers new unlearning failures, notably in multi-hop settings. Activation analyses show single-hop queries rely on dominant pathways (more easily disrupted), while multi-hop queries use alternative pathways (more resilient).

Conclusion: A scalable, dynamic evaluation framework for unlearning that reduces dependence on manual test sets, enabling practical adoption in real-world applications; provided as open-source tooling.

Abstract: Unlearning in Large Language Models (LLMs) aims to enhance safety, mitigate biases, and comply with legal mandates, such as the right to be forgotten. However, existing unlearning methods are brittle: minor query modifications, such as multi-hop reasoning and entity aliasing, can recover supposedly forgotten information. As a result, current evaluation metrics often create an illusion of effectiveness, failing to detect these vulnerabilities due to reliance on static, unstructured benchmarks. We propose a dynamic framework that stress tests unlearning robustness using complex structured queries. Our approach first elicits knowledge from the target model (pre-unlearning) and constructs targeted probes, ranging from simple queries to multi-hop chains, allowing precise control over query difficulty. Our experiments show that the framework (1) shows comparable coverage to existing benchmarks by automatically generating semantically equivalent Q&A probes, (2) aligns with prior evaluations, and (3) uncovers new unlearning failures missed by other benchmarks, particularly in multi-hop settings. Furthermore, activation analyses show that single-hop queries typically follow dominant computation pathways, which are more likely to be disrupted by unlearning methods. In contrast, multi-hop queries tend to use alternative pathways that often remain intact, explaining the brittleness of unlearning techniques in multi-hop settings. Our framework enables practical and scalable evaluation of unlearning methods without the need for manual construction of forget test sets, enabling easier adoption for real-world applications.
