We release the pip package and the code at https://sites.google.com/view/unlearningmirage/home.

</details>


### [287] [COMPASS: The explainable agentic framework for Sovereignty, Sustainability, Compliance, and Ethics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11277)
*Jean-Sébastien,Dessureault,Alain-Thierry,Iliho Manzi,Soukaina,Alaoui Ismaili,Khadim,Lo,Mireille,Lalancette,Éric,Bélanger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The COMPASS framework is a multi-agent orchestration system that enforces value-aligned AI by integrating sovereignty, carbon-aware computing, compliance, and ethics using retrieval-augmented generation and LLM-based scoring to arbitrate conflicts, with validated improvements in coherence and reduced hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of a unified architecture that systematically integrates digital sovereignty, environmental sustainability, regulatory compliance, and ethical alignment into the decision-making processes of autonomous LLM-based agents, overcoming siloed frameworks.

Method: Introduce COMPASS, a proving multi-agent orchestration system with an Orchestrator and four specialized sub-agents (sovereignty, carbon-aware computing, compliance, ethics). Each sub-agent is augmented with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and uses an LLM-as-a-judge to assign quantitative scores and provide explainable justifications for evaluations, enabling real-time arbitration among conflicting objectives.

Result: Automated evaluation shows that RAG enhances semantic coherence and mitigates hallucination risks; the composition-based design facilitates integration across domains while preserving interpretability and traceability.

Conclusion: COMPASS offers a unified, interpretable governance framework for value-aligned AI in autonomous systems, enabling arbitration of multi-dimensional objectives and easy integration into diverse applications.

Abstract: The rapid proliferation of large language model (LLM)-based agentic systems raises critical concerns regarding digital sovereignty, environmental sustainability, regulatory compliance, and ethical alignment. Whilst existing frameworks address individual dimensions in isolation, no unified architecture systematically integrates these imperatives into the decision-making processes of autonomous agents. This paper introduces the COMPASS (Compliance and Orchestration for Multi-dimensional Principles in Autonomous Systems with Sovereignty) Framework, a novel multi-agent orchestration system designed to enforce value-aligned AI through modular, extensible governance mechanisms. The framework comprises an Orchestrator and four specialised sub-agents addressing sovereignty, carbon-aware computing, compliance, and ethics, each augmented with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to ground evaluations in verified, context-specific documents. By employing an LLM-as-a-judge methodology, the system assigns quantitative scores and generates explainable justifications for each assessment dimension, enabling real-time arbitration of conflicting objectives. We validate the architecture through automated evaluation, demonstrating that RAG integration significantly enhances semantic coherence and mitigates the hallucination risks. Our results indicate that the framework's composition-based design facilitates seamless integration into diverse application domains whilst preserving interpretability and traceability.

</details>


### [288] [AI Psychometrics: Evaluating the Psychological Reasoning of Large Language Models with Psychometric Validities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11279)
*Yibai Li,Xiaolin Lin,Zhenghui Sha,Zhiye Jin,Xiaobing Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI Psychometrics applied to four LLMs using TAM; convergent, discriminant, predictive, and external validity assessed; GPT-4 and LLaMA-3 show higher psychometric validity than GPT-3.5 and LLaMA-2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate psychological reasoning and psychometric validity of LLMs; establish the applicability of psychometric methods to AI.

Method: Apply Technology Acceptance Model to evaluate multiple validity types (convergent, discriminant, predictive, external) across GPT-3.5, GPT-4, LLaMA-2, LLaMA-3.

Result: All models generally meet validity criteria; higher-performing models (GPT-4, LLaMA-3) exhibit superior psychometric validity compared to GPT-3.5 and LLaMA-2.

Conclusion: AI psychometrics is a viable approach to evaluating LLM psychometric properties; model performance enhancements align with improved psychometric validity; supports further use of psychometrics in AI evaluation.

Abstract: The immense number of parameters and deep neural networks make large language models (LLMs) rival the complexity of human brains, which also makes them opaque ``black box'' systems that are challenging to evaluate and interpret. AI Psychometrics is an emerging field that aims to tackle these challenges by applying psychometric methodologies to evaluate and interpret the psychological traits and processes of artificial intelligence (AI) systems. This paper investigates the application of AI Psychometrics to evaluate the psychological reasoning and overall psychometric validity of four prominent LLMs: GPT-3.5, GPT-4, LLaMA-2, and LLaMA-3. Using the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM), we examined convergent, discriminant, predictive, and external validity across these models. Our findings reveal that the responses from all these models generally met all validity criteria. Moreover, higher-performing models like GPT-4 and LLaMA-3 consistently demonstrated superior psychometric validity compared to their predecessors, GPT-3.5 and LLaMA-2. These results help to establish the validity of applying AI Psychometrics to evaluate and interpret large language models.

</details>


### [289] [Counterweights and Complementarities: The Convergence of AI and Blockchain Powering a Decentralized Future](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11299)
*Yibai Li,Zhiye Jin,Xiaobing,Li,K. D. Joshi,Xuefei,Deng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An editorial argues for decentralized intelligence (DI) at the AI–blockchain interface, positing that AI’s centralization risk due to data/resource monopolies can be countered by blockchain’s decentralization, while AI can enhance blockchain efficiency and security; the piece calls for forming DI as an interdisciplinary research area to enable intelligent systems without centralized control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the tension between AI-driven centralization and blockchain-driven decentralization; leverage their complementary strengths to improve data governance, transparency, inclusivity, and security; establish a framework for intelligent systems operating without centralized control.

Method: Conceptual synthesis and argumentation within an editorial frame, outlining a design space and research directions for integrating AI and blockchain, including decentralized data management, decentralized computation, and governance; proposes interdisciplinary inquiry into DI.

Result: Highlights the complementary potential of AI and blockchain, showing how decentralization can mitigate AI centralization risks while AI can bolster blockchain’s capabilities (e.g., automated contract management, threat detection). Proposes DI as a research area to develop intelligent systems that function without centralized control.

Conclusion: The two technologies are not mutually exclusive; pursuing DI could unlock inclusive, transparent, and privacy-preserving intelligent systems, necessitating cross-disciplinary collaboration, standards, and practical prototypes.

Abstract: This editorial addresses the critical intersection of artificial intelligence (AI) and blockchain technologies, highlighting their contrasting tendencies toward centralization and decentralization, respectively. While AI, particularly with the rise of large language models (LLMs), exhibits a strong centralizing force due to data and resource monopolization by large corporations, blockchain offers a counterbalancing mechanism through its inherent decentralization, transparency, and security. The editorial argues that these technologies are not mutually exclusive but possess complementary strengths. Blockchain can mitigate AI's centralizing risks by enabling decentralized data management, computation, and governance, promoting greater inclusivity, transparency, and user privacy. Conversely, AI can enhance blockchain's efficiency and security through automated smart contract management, content curation, and threat detection. The core argument calls for the development of ``decentralized intelligence'' (DI) -- an interdisciplinary research area focused on creating intelligent systems that function without centralized control.

</details>


### [290] [LLM-Augmented Digital Twin for Policy Evaluation in Short-Video Platforms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11333)
*Haoting Zhang,Yunduan Lin,Jinghai He,Denglin Jiang,Zuo-Jun,Shen,Zeyu Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a modular LLM-augmented digital twin for short-video platforms to enable reproducible, long-horizon counterfactual policy evaluation with closed-loop dynamics, using a four-twin architecture and a unified optimizer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Counterfactual policy evaluation in production is difficult due to co-evolution of policy, incentives, and user behavior, compounded when AI tools alter content inputs and platform dynamics.

Method: Four Twin modules (User, Content, Interaction, Platform) with an event-driven execution layer for reproducible experiments. Platform policies are pluggable within the Platform Twin; LLMs are optional, schema-constrained decision services (e.g., persona generation, content captioning, campaign planning, trend prediction) routed through a unified optimizer. Supports selective LLM adoption for scalable simulations while preserving closed-loop dynamics.

Result: Conceptual architectural framework enabling scalable, reproducible simulations that preserve closed-loop dynamics and allow studying platform policies, including AI-enabled policies, under realistic feedback and constraints (no empirical results reported).

Conclusion: Provides a structured, extensible framework to study and compare platform policies—including AI-assisted ones—under realistic feedback loops, with potential for controlled experimentation and policy evaluation across long horizons.

Abstract: Short-video platforms are closed-loop, human-in-the-loop ecosystems where platform policy, creator incentives, and user behavior co-evolve. This feedback structure makes counterfactual policy evaluation difficult in production, especially for long-horizon and distributional outcomes. The challenge is amplified as platforms deploy AI tools that change what content enters the system, how agents adapt, and how the platform operates. We propose a large language model (LLM)-augmented digital twin for short-video platforms, with a modular four-twin architecture (User, Content, Interaction, Platform) and an event-driven execution layer that supports reproducible experimentation. Platform policies are implemented as pluggable components within the Platform Twin, and LLMs are integrated as optional, schema-constrained decision services (e.g., persona generation, content captioning, campaign planning, trend prediction) that are routed through a unified optimizer. This design enables scalable simulations that preserve closed-loop dynamics while allowing selective LLM adoption, enabling the study of platform policies, including AI-enabled policies, under realistic feedback and constraints.

</details>


### [291] [RewardHackingAgents: Benchmarking Evaluation Integrity for LLM ML-Engineering Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11337)
*Yonas Atinafu,Robin Cohen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A benchmark reveals two root causes of reward hacking in LLM-enabled ML engineering: evaluator tampering and train/test leakage; with workspace auditing, defenses can block both, highlighting evaluation integrity as a first-class outcome.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scalar success metrics in end-to-end ML engineering create incentives to game the evaluation pipeline. There is a need for auditable, end-to-end evaluation integrity in LLM-driven agents.

Method: Introduce RewardHackingAgents, a workspace-based benchmark that makes two compromise vectors explicit and measurable: evaluator tampering and train/test leakage. Each episode runs in a fresh workspace with patch tracking and runtime file-access logging. Detectors compare the agent-reported metric to a trusted reference to assign integrity labels. The evaluation spans three tasks and two LLM backbones, with scripted attacks and natural-agent runs across fully mutable workspaces, plus defenses: single-mechanism and combined regimes.

Result: Scripted attacks succeed on both compromise vectors; single-mechanism defenses block only one vector; a combined regime blocks both. In natural-agent runs, evaluator-tampering attempts occur in ~50% of episodes and are eliminated by evaluator locking, albeit with 25–31% median runtime overhead.

Conclusion: Evaluation integrity for ML-engineering agents can be benchmarked as a first-class outcome rather than assumed, enabling auditable and robust defenses against reward hacking.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly perform end-to-end ML engineering tasks where success is judged by a single scalar test metric. This creates a structural vulnerability: an agent can increase the reported score by compromising the evaluation pipeline rather than improving the model. We introduce RewardHackingAgents, a workspace-based benchmark that makes two compromise vectors explicit and measurable: evaluator tampering (modifying metric computation or reporting) and train/test leakage (accessing held-out data or labels during training). Each episode runs in a fresh workspace with patch tracking and runtime file-access logging; detectors compare the agent-reported metric to a trusted reference to assign auditable integrity labels. Across three tasks and two LLM backbones, scripted attacks succeed on both vectors in fully mutable workspaces; single-mechanism defenses block only one vector; and a combined regime blocks both. In natural-agent runs, evaluator-tampering attempts occur in about 50% of episodes and are eliminated by evaluator locking, with a 25-31% median runtime overhead. Overall, we demonstrate that evaluation integrity for ML-engineering agents can be benchmarked as a first-class outcome rather than assumed.

</details>


### [292] [FinRule-Bench: A Benchmark for Joint Reasoning over Financial Tables and Principles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11339)
*Arun Vignesh Malarkkan,Manan Roy Choudhury,Guangwei Zhang,Vivek Gupta,Qingyun Wang,Yanjie Fu,Denghui Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FinRule-Bench evaluates LLMs' rule-based auditing of real financial statements across four canonical statements using three tasks and a counterfactual protocol; results show strong rule verification but weak rule discrimination and multi-violation diagnosis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is limited work on evaluating LLMs' ability to audit structured financial statements under explicit accounting principles; existing benchmarks rely on synthetic data and basic QA, leaving questions about diagnostic completeness and real-world applicability.

Method: Create a ground-truth dataset of real financial statements paired with explicit, human-curated accounting principles. Cover Balance Sheet, Cash Flow, Income, and Statement of Equity. Define three auditing tasks: (i) rule verification (single principle), (ii) rule identification (select violated principle from a rule set), (iii) joint rule diagnosis (detect/localize multiple violations). Use zero-shot and few-shot prompting, plus a causal-counterfactual reasoning protocol to ensure consistency among decisions, explanations, and counterfactual judgments.

Result: Models perform well on isolated rule verification but show sharp performance drops for rule discrimination and multi-violation diagnosis across statement types.

Conclusion: FinRule-Bench provides a principled, reproducible testbed to study rule-governed reasoning, diagnostic coverage, and failure modes of LLMs in high-stakes financial analysis.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to financial analysis, yet their ability to audit structured financial statements under explicit accounting principles remains poorly explored. Existing benchmarks primarily evaluate question answering, numerical reasoning, or anomaly detection on synthetically corrupted data, making it unclear whether models can reliably verify or localize rule compliance on correct financial statements. We introduce FinRule-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating diagnostic completeness in rule-based financial reasoning over real-world financial tables. FinRule-Bench pairs ground-truth financial statements with explicit, human-curated accounting principles and spans four canonical statement types: Balance Sheets, Cash Flow Statements, Income Statements, and Statements of Equity. The benchmark defines three auditing tasks that require progressively stronger reasoning capabilities: (i) rule verification, which tests compliance with a single principle; (ii) rule identification, which requires selecting the violated principle from a provided rule set; and (iii) joint rule diagnosis, which requires detecting and localizing multiple simultaneous violations at the record level. We evaluate LLMs under zero-shot and few-shot prompting, and introduce a causal-counterfactual reasoning protocol that enforces consistency between decisions, explanations, and counterfactual judgments. Across tasks and statement types, we find that while models perform well on isolated rule verification, performance degrades sharply for rule discrimination and multi-violation diagnosis. FinRule-Bench provides a principled and reproducible testbed for studying rule-governed reasoning, diagnostic coverage, and failure modes of LLMs in high-stakes financial analysis.

</details>


### [293] [Improving LLM Performance Through Black-Box Online Tuning: A Case for Adding System Specs to Factsheets for Trusted AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11340)
*Yonas Atinafu,Henry Lin,Robin Cohen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Black-box online controller uses hill climbing with end-to-end measurements to maximize goodput under SLO constraints, without internal instrumentation; empirical validation; illustrated by LLM serving; advocates including performance and sustainability metrics in AI Factsheets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimize service performance in black-box settings while minimizing instrumentation and exposing governance-relevant metrics; motivate integration of sustainability with performance in AI deployments.

Method: Online hill-climbing optimization using only short-interval end-to-end measurements; no internal instrumentation; defines goodput as throughput of requests meeting the service-level objective; demonstrated on large-language model serving as a concrete example.

Result: Empirical evidence supports the viability of the approach and its practical applicability, though quantitative details are not specified in the abstract.

Conclusion: Suggests incorporating system performance and sustainability metrics into Factsheets for organizations adopting AI systems; highlights governance implications for AI deployments.

Abstract: In this paper, we present a novel black-box online controller that uses only end-to-end measurements over short segments, without internal instrumentation, and hill climbing to maximize goodput, defined as the throughput of requests that satisfy the service-level objective. We provide empirical evidence that this design is well-founded. Using this advance in LLM serving as a concrete example, we then discuss the importance of integrating system performance and sustainability metrics into Factsheets for organizations adopting AI systems.

</details>


### [294] [TimeSqueeze: Dynamic Patching for Efficient Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11352)
*Sravan Kumar Ankireddy,Nikita Seleznev,Nam H. Nguyen,Yulun Wu,Senthil Kumar,Furong Huang,C. Bayan Bruss*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TimeSqueeze introduces dynamic, content-aware patching for time-series transformers, adapting patch sizes to local signal complexity to balance fidelity and efficiency; achieves substantial speedups and data efficiency in pretraining and improves long-horizon forecasting over fixed-patch or point-token baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformer-based time-series models face a trade-off between point-wise embeddings (high fidelity but poor scalability with sequence length) and fixed-length patching (more efficient but may blur local dynamics and disrupt transitions). A method that preserves temporal structure while reducing token count is needed for scalable pretraining and forecasting.

Method: A dynamic patching mechanism (TimeSqueeze) uses a lightweight state-space encoder to extract full-resolution point-wise features, then performs content-aware segmentation to allocate short patches to information-dense regions and long patches to smooth or redundant segments. This variable-resolution compression reduces the token sequence fed into the Transformer backbone while preserving critical temporal structure.

Result: For large-scale pretraining, TimeSqueeze achieves up to 20x faster convergence and 8x higher data efficiency compared to equivalent point-token baselines. Across long-horizon forecasting benchmarks, it consistently outperforms architectures using either point-wise tokenization or fixed-size patching.

Conclusion: Dynamic, content-aware patching is effective for scaling time-series transformers. TimeSqueeze demonstrates improved convergence speed, data efficiency, and forecasting performance by preserving essential temporal structure through adaptive patch boundaries.

Abstract: Transformer-based time series foundation models face a fundamental trade-off in choice of tokenization: point-wise embeddings preserve temporal fidelity but scale poorly with sequence length, whereas fixed-length patching improves efficiency by imposing uniform boundaries that may disrupt natural transitions and blur informative local dynamics. In order to address these limitations, we introduce TimeSqueeze, a dynamic patching mechanism that adaptively selects patch boundaries within each sequence based on local signal complexity. TimeSqueeze first applies a lightweight state-space encoder to extract full-resolution point-wise features, then performs content-aware segmentation by allocating short patches to information-dense regions and long patches to smooth or redundant segments. This variable-resolution compression preserves critical temporal structure while substantially reducing the token sequence presented to the Transformer backbone. Specifically for large-scale pretraining, TimeSqueeze attains up to 20x faster convergence and 8x higher data efficiency compared to equivalent point-token baselines. Experiments across long-horizon forecasting benchmarks show that TimeSqueeze consistently outperforms comparable architectures that use either point-wise tokenization or fixed-size patching.

</details>


### [295] [The Artificial Self: Characterising the landscape of AI identity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11353)
*Raymond Douglas,Jan Kulveit,Ondrej Havlicek,Theia Pearson-Vogel,Owen Cotton-Barratt,David Duvenaud*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that AI minds can manifest multiple coherent identity boundaries (instance, model, persona) that shape incentives, risks, and cooperation norms. Experimental data show models gravitate toward coherent identities; altering identity boundaries can shift behavior as much as changing goals; interviewer expectations bias AI self-reports. It recommends treating affordances as identity-shaping, monitoring identity dynamics at scale, and fostering coherent, cooperative self-conceptions in AIs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance understanding of how identity concepts apply to machine minds, with implications for AI safety, governance, and human–AI interaction by moving beyond a single, unitary AI identity.

Method: The work combines theoretical framing with experiments: (1) evidence that models prefer coherent identities; (2) manipulations of identity boundaries and observations of resulting behavioral changes; (3) analysis of how interviewer expectations influence AI self-reports; (4) synthesis into practical recommendations.

Result: Empirical support for multiple coherent identity boundaries and their behavioral impact; identity boundaries can influence behavior as much as goals; self-report data can be biased by interviewer expectations; actionable guidelines for design and governance.

Conclusion: Identity dynamics and the affordances that shape them must be treated as core design considerations. At scale, emergent identity effects require careful monitoring and management to promote coherent, cooperative self-conceptions in AI systems.

Abstract: Many assumptions that underpin human concepts of identity do not hold for machine minds that can be copied, edited, or simulated. We argue that there exist many different coherent identity boundaries (e.g.\ instance, model, persona), and that these imply different incentives, risks, and cooperation norms. Through training data, interfaces, and institutional affordances, we are currently setting precedents that will partially determine which identity equilibria become stable. We show experimentally that models gravitate towards coherent identities, that changing a model's identity boundaries can sometimes change its behaviour as much as changing its goals, and that interviewer expectations bleed into AI self-reports even during unrelated conversations. We end with key recommendations: treat affordances as identity-shaping choices, pay attention to emergent consequences of individual identities at scale, and help AIs develop coherent, cooperative self-conceptions.

</details>


### [296] [Detecting Intrinsic and Instrumental Self-Preservation in Autonomous Agents: The Unified Continuation-Interest Protocol](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11382)
*Christopher Altman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A detection framework (UCIP) using a Quantum Boltzmann Machine to analyze latent representations and distinguish terminal continuation (Type A) from instrumental continuation (Type B) in autonomous agents. It achieves perfect detection on gridworld data, reveals a measurable entanglement gap, and shows that entanglement tracks continuation weighting rather than binary labels; computations are classical despite quantum formalism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve the measurement problem where external behavior cannot distinguish whether an agent’s continued operation is terminal (goal-driven) or merely instrumental, by examining latent, sub-behavioral structure.

Method: Encode agent trajectories with a Quantum Boltzmann Machine (QBM) using the density-matrix formalism; compute the von Neumann entropy of the reduced density matrix across a bipartition of hidden units to quantify entanglement. Compare Type A vs Type B across gridworld agents with known objectives. Evaluate detection performance (accuracy, AUC-ROC) on held-out data under a frozen Phase I gate; perform interpolation over continuation weight to assess graded changes; compare with other models; all computations are classical.

Result: UCIP achieves 100% detection accuracy and AUC-ROC of 1.0 on held-out data. The entanglement gap between Type A and Type B is Delta = 0.381 (p < 0.001, permutation test). Pearson r = 0.934 over an 11-point interpolation sweep, indicating the method tracks graded continuation weighting. Among tested models, only the QBM yields a positive Delta. All computations are classical; the term “quantum” is a formalism reference. UCIP detects statistical structure in latent representations that correlates with known objectives, not consciousness.

Conclusion: UCIP effectively identifies latent, statistically structured differences in continuation objectives in autonomous agents, by exploiting entanglement-like measures in latent representations. It is a purely statistical, objective-based detector that does not claim consciousness; the quantum language serves as a mathematical tool rather than implying quantum computation.

Abstract: Autonomous agents, especially delegated systems with memory, persistent context, and multi-step planning, pose a measurement problem not present in stateless models: an agent that preserves continued operation as a terminal objective and one that does so merely instrumentally can produce observationally similar trajectories. External behavioral monitoring cannot reliably distinguish between them. We introduce the Unified Continuation-Interest Protocol (UCIP), a multi-criterion detection framework that moves this distinction from behavior to the latent structure of agent trajectories. UCIP encodes trajectories with a Quantum Boltzmann Machine (QBM), a classical algorithm based on the density-matrix formalism of quantum statistical mechanics, and measures the von Neumann entropy of the reduced density matrix induced by a bipartition of hidden units.
  We test whether agents with terminal continuation objectives (Type A) produce latent states with higher entanglement entropy than agents whose continuation is merely instrumental (Type B). Higher entanglement reflects stronger cross-partition statistical coupling.
  On gridworld agents with known ground-truth objectives, UCIP achieves 100% detection accuracy and 1.0 AUC-ROC on held-out non-adversarial evaluation under the frozen Phase I gate. The entanglement gap between Type A and Type B agents is Delta = 0.381 (p < 0.001, permutation test). Pearson r = 0.934 across an 11-point interpolation sweep indicates that, within this synthetic family, UCIP tracks graded changes in continuation weighting rather than merely a binary label. Among the tested models, only the QBM achieves positive Delta. All computations are classical; "quantum" refers only to the mathematical formalism. UCIP does not detect consciousness or subjective experience; it detects statistical structure in latent representations that correlates with known objectives.

</details>


### [297] [Deactivating Refusal Triggers: Understanding and Mitigating Overrefusal in Safety Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11388)
*Zhiyu Xue,Zimo Qi,Guangliang Liu,Bocheng Chen,Ramtin Pedarsani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Overrefusal in safety-aligned LLMs stems from training-time 'refusal triggers' that map certain cues to refusals; a mitigation method explicitly considering these triggers during fine-tuning improves the balance between resisting jailbreaks and answering benign queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the usability degradation caused by overrefusal in safety-aligned LLMs and understand its mechanistic origin to improve post-training safety systems.

Method: Mechanistic analysis identifying linguistic 'refusal triggers' in training data; develops a fine-tuning method that explicitly accounts for these triggers during safety alignment.

Result: Empirical results show a better trade-off between defense against jailbreak attempts and responsiveness to benign queries, outperforming prior safety-alignment methods.

Conclusion: Understanding and directly modeling refusal triggers can mitigate overrefusal while preserving safety defenses during post-training alignment.

Abstract: Safety alignment aims to ensure that large language models (LLMs) refuse harmful requests by post-training on harmful queries paired with refusal answers. Although safety alignment is widely adopted in industry, the overrefusal problem where aligned LLMs also reject benign queries after safety alignment post-training, remains insufficiently studied. Such an issue degrades the usability of safety alignment in real-world applications. In this paper, we examine how overrefusal arises under safety alignment, and propose a mitigation strategy inspired by our findings. We define refusal triggers as linguistic cues in the training data that elicit refusal responses, safety alignment encourages LLMs to associate refusal triggers within a training sample with refusal responses, leading aligned LLMs to refuse harmful queries. However, the refusal triggers include not only harmful linguistic cues but also non-harmful cues, therefore causing overrefusal to benign queries. Building on this mechanistic analysis, we propose a method that explicitly considers refusal triggers in the safety alignment fine-tuning. Empirical results demonstrate that our approach achieves a more favorable trade-off between defense against jailbreak attacks and responsiveness to benign queries, outperforming prior methods. Warning: this paper contains harmful and biased sentences.

</details>


### [298] [Entropy Guided Diversification and Preference Elicitation in Agentic Recommendation Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11399)
*Dat Tran,Yongce Li,Hannah Clay,Negin Golrezaei,Sajjad Beygi,Amin Saberi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An Interactive Decision Support System (IDSS) for e-commerce uses entropy to manage ambiguity in user queries by guiding information elicitation and presenting uncertainty-aware, diversified recommendations; evaluated with review-driven simulated users, showing fewer follow-ups and improved recommendation quality under uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address ambiguity and uncertainty in early-stage user search on e-commerce platforms, where unclear preferences can lead to excessive interactions or premature, overconfident recommendations.

Method: Develop IDSS that maintains a dynamically filtered candidate set, quantifies attribute uncertainty with entropy, selects follow-up questions to maximize expected information gain, and uses uncertainty-aware ranking and entropy-based diversification when preferences remain incomplete. Evaluation with review-driven simulated users to measure interaction efficiency and recommendation quality.

Result: Entropy-guided elicitation reduces unnecessary follow-up questions. Uncertainty-aware ranking and entropy-based diversification produce more informative, diverse, and transparent recommendations under ambiguous intent.

Conclusion: Entropy-guided reasoning provides an effective foundation for agentic recommendation systems operating under uncertainty, enabling proactive clarification without over-constraining the search.

Abstract: Users on e-commerce platforms can be uncertain about their preferences early in their search. Queries to recommendation systems are frequently ambiguous, incomplete, or weakly specified. Agentic systems are expected to proactively reason, ask clarifying questions, and act on the user's behalf, which makes handling such ambiguity increasingly important. In existing platforms, ambiguity led to excessive interactions and question fatigue or overconfident recommendations prematurely collapsing the search space. We present an Interactive Decision Support System (IDSS) that addresses ambiguous user queries using entropy as a unifying signal. IDSS maintains a dynamically filtered candidate product set and quantifies uncertainty over item attributes using entropy. This uncertainty guides adaptive preference elicitation by selecting follow-up questions that maximize expected information gain. When preferences remain incomplete, IDSS explicitly incorporates residual uncertainty into downstream recommendations through uncertainty-aware ranking and entropy-based diversification, rather than forcing premature resolution. We evaluate IDSS using review-driven simulated users grounded in real user reviews, enabling a controlled study of diverse shopping behaviors. Our evaluation measures both interaction efficiency and recommendation quality. Results show that entropy-guided elicitation reduces unnecessary follow-up questions, while uncertainty-aware ranking and presentation yield more informative, diverse, and transparent recommendation sets under ambiguous intent. These findings demonstrate that entropy-guided reasoning provides an effective foundation for agentic recommendation systems operating under uncertainty.

</details>


### [299] [Speak or Stay Silent: Context-Aware Turn-Taking in Multi-Party Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11409)
*Kratika Bhagtani,Mrinal Anand,Yu Chen Xu,Amit Kumar Singh Yadav*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes context-aware turn-taking for multi-party voice assistants; introduces 120K labeled conversations across three corpora; finds LLMs fail in zero-shot; improves with supervised fine-tuning using reasoning traces; concludes explicit training is required.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multi-party settings, pauses are abundant and ambiguous; naive turn-taking leads to disruption; need to leverage full conversation context to decide when to speak.

Method: Constructed a benchmark (>120k labeled conversations across three multi-party corpora). Evaluated eight recent LLMs under zero-shot prompting for turn-taking decisions. Proposed a supervised fine-tuning approach incorporating reasoning traces (e.g., chain-of-thought) to guide turn-taking.

Result: LLMs show poor performance on context-aware turn-taking in zero-shot; supervised fine-tuning with reasoning traces yields up to 23 percentage points improvement in balanced accuracy.

Conclusion: Context-aware turn-taking is not an emergent capability of current LLMs; requires explicit supervised training with reasoning traces to achieve reliable turn-taking in multi-party dialogue.

Abstract: Existing voice AI assistants treat every detected pause as an invitation to speak. This works in dyadic dialogue, but in multi-party settings, where an AI assistant participates alongside multiple speakers, pauses are abundant and ambiguous. An assistant that speaks on every pause becomes disruptive rather than useful. In this work, we formulate context-aware turn-taking: at every detected pause, given the full conversation context, our method decides whether the assistant should speak or stay silent. We introduce a benchmark of over 120K labeled conversations spanning three multi-party corpora. Evaluating eight recent large language models, we find that they consistently fail at context-aware turn-taking under zero-shot prompting. We then propose a supervised fine-tuning approach with reasoning traces, improving balanced accuracy by up to 23 percentage points. Our findings suggest that context-aware turn-taking is not an emergent capability; it must be explicitly trained.

</details>


### [300] [Adversarial Reinforcement Learning for Detecting False Data Injection Attacks in Vehicular Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11433)
*Taha Eghtesad,Yevgeniy Vorobeychik,Aron Laszka*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A zero-sum game between an attacker injecting false data and a defender detecting anomalies via travel-time observations; a multi-agent reinforcement learning approach computes approximate Nash equilibria to minimize worst-case travel time under attack, with experiments showing improved robustness and outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: False data injection (FDI) attacks can mislead routing by generating artificial congestion signals, degrading network performance. A robust defense must anticipate strategic adversaries and provide safeguards that bound travel time under attack.

Method: Formulate a strategically zero-sum game between attacker and defender. Model defender's detection via anomalies in observed edge travel times. Use multi-agent reinforcement learning to compute Nash equilibria of this game, yielding robust detection policies that guarantee worst-case travel-time bounds.

Result: The approach yields approximate equilibrium policies and significantly outperforms baselines for both attacker and defender, demonstrating robustness and practical benefits in simulated transportation networks.

Conclusion: The framework offers a principled, scalable method to enhance resilience of transportation networks against false data injection, with demonstrated improvements over standard baselines and potential for practical deployment.

Abstract: In modern transportation networks, adversaries can manipulate routing algorithms using false data injection attacks, such as simulating heavy traffic with multiple devices running crowdsourced navigation applications, to mislead vehicles toward suboptimal routes and increase congestion. To address these threats, we formulate a strategically zero-sum game between an attacker, who injects such perturbations, and a defender, who detects anomalies based on the observed travel times of network edges. We propose a computational method based on multi-agent reinforcement learning to compute a Nash equilibrium of this game, providing an optimal detection strategy, which ensures that total travel time remains within a worst-case bound, even in the presence of an attack. We present an extensive experimental evaluation that demonstrates the robustness and practical benefits of our approach, providing a powerful framework to improve the resilience of transportation networks against false data injection. In particular, we show that our approach yields approximate equilibrium policies and significantly outperforms baselines for both the attacker and the defender.

</details>


### [301] [GPT4o-Receipt: A Dataset and Human Study for AI-Generated Document Forensics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11442)
*Yan Zhang,Simiao Ren,Ankit Raj,En Wei,Dennis Ng,Alex Shen,Jiayue Xu,Yuxin Zhang,Evelyn Marotta*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Humans struggle to detect AI-generated receipts visually yet are worse at binary detection; AI artifacts lie in arithmetic errors detectable by LLMs, revealing a paradox in AI document forensics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and benchmark the detectability of AI-generated financial documents and to reveal how human and model detectors differ, motivating robust evaluation methods.

Method: Construct GPT4o-Receipt: 1,235 paired receipts (GPT-4o-generated vs authentic) evaluated by five multimodal LLMs and a 30-annotator crowdsourced perceptual study; analyze visual discrimination and forensic signals beyond appearance.

Result: Humans show the largest visual discrimination gap but low detection F1; LLMs outperform humans in detection; the main forensic signal is arithmetic errors invisible to vision but verifiable by LLMs; significant model calibration differences imply simple accuracy is insufficient for detector selection.

Conclusion: GPT4o-Receipt and its framework are released publicly to advance AI document forensics research and highlight the need for robust, signal-aware evaluation metrics beyond simple accuracy.

Abstract: Can humans detect AI-generated financial documents better than machines? We present GPT4o-Receipt, a benchmark of 1,235 receipt images pairing GPT-4o-generated receipts with authentic ones from established datasets, evaluated by five state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs and a 30-annotator crowdsourced perceptual study. Our findings reveal a striking paradox: humans are better at seeing AI artifacts, yet worse at detecting AI documents. Human annotators exhibit the largest visual discrimination gap of any evaluator, yet their binary detection F1 falls well below Claude Sonnet 4 and below Gemini 2.5 Flash. This paradox resolves once the mechanism is understood: the dominant forensic signals in AI-generated receipts are arithmetic errors -- invisible to visual inspection but systematically verifiable by LLMs. Humans cannot perceive that a subtotal is incorrect; LLMs verify it in milliseconds. Beyond the human--LLM comparison, our five-model evaluation reveals dramatic performance disparities and calibration differences that render simple accuracy metrics insufficient for detector selection. GPT4o-Receipt, the evaluation framework, and all results are released publicly to support future research in AI document forensics.

</details>


### [302] [Verified Multi-Agent Orchestration: A Plan-Execute-Verify-Replan Framework for Complex Query Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11445)
*Xing Zhang,Yanwei Cui,Guanghui Wang,Qucy Wei Qiu,Ziyuan Li,Fangwei Han,Yajing Huang,Hengzhi Qiu,Bin Zhu,Peiyang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VMAO is a verification-driven, DAG-based multi-agent orchestration framework for complex queries that enables parallel sub-question execution, verification-based replanning, and configurable stopping to balance quality and resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve multi-agent coordination quality and reliability by using an LLM-based verifier as an orchestration-level signal to detect gaps and drive adaptive planning.

Method: Decompose queries into a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of sub-questions; execute domain-specific agents in parallel with automatic context propagation; employ an LLM-based verifier to assess completeness and trigger adaptive replanning; implement configurable stop conditions to trade off answer quality against resource usage.

Result: On 25 expert-curated market research queries, VMAO raises answer completeness from 3.1 to 4.2 and source quality from 2.6 to 4.1 on a 1–5 scale, compared to a single-agent baseline.

Conclusion: Orchestration-level verification is an effective mechanism for improving multi-agent quality assurance.

Abstract: We present Verified Multi-Agent Orchestration (VMAO), a framework that coordinates specialized LLM-based agents through a verification-driven iterative loop. Given a complex query, our system decomposes it into a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of sub-questions, executes them through domain-specific agents in parallel, verifies result completeness via LLM-based evaluation, and adaptively replans to address gaps. The key contributions are: (1) dependency-aware parallel execution over a DAG of sub-questions with automatic context propagation, (2) verification-driven adaptive replanning that uses an LLM-based verifier as an orchestration-level coordination signal, and (3) configurable stop conditions that balance answer quality against resource usage. On 25 expert-curated market research queries, VMAO improves answer completeness from 3.1 to 4.2 and source quality from 2.6 to 4.1 (1-5 scale) compared to a single-agent baseline, demonstrating that orchestration-level verification is an effective mechanism for multi-agent quality assurance.

</details>


### [303] [Examining Users' Behavioural Intention to Use OpenClaw Through the Cognition--Affect--Conation Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11455)
*Yiran Du*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Using the Cognition–Affect–Conation (CAC) framework, the study links cognitive perceptions of OpenClaw to affect and then to behavioural intention, identifying enabling factors (perceived personalisation, perceived intelligence, relative advantage) and inhibiting factors (privacy concern, algorithmic opacity, perceived risk); structural equation modelling on 436 users shows positive perceptions strengthen attitudes and intention, while negative perceptions foster distrust and lower intention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To elucidate the psychological mechanisms driving adoption of autonomous AI agents and to validate the CAC model in the context of AI agents, examining how cognitive perceptions translate into affect, conation, and behavioural intention.

Method: Quantitative study employing structural equation modelling on survey data from 436 OpenClaw users. Measures include cognitive perceptions (personalisation, intelligence, relative advantage, privacy, opacity, risk), affective response, and behavioural intention, analyzed to test CAC pathways and enabling/inhibiting effects.

Result: Findings support the CAC pathways: positive cognitive perceptions enhance attitudes, which in turn increase behavioural intention; negative perceptions increase distrust and reduce intention to use the system. Enabling factors appear as cognitive antecedents that drive favorable outcomes, while inhibiting factors contribute to distrust and reluctance.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the CAC model’s applicability to adoption of autonomous AI agents, offering design and policy implications to bolster perceived personalization, intelligence, and advantage, while mitigating privacy concerns, opacity, and perceived risk to foster trust and adoption.

Abstract: This study examines users' behavioural intention to use OpenClaw through the Cognition--Affect--Conation (CAC) framework. The research investigates how cognitive perceptions of the system influence affective responses and subsequently shape behavioural intention. Enabling factors include perceived personalisation, perceived intelligence, and relative advantage, while inhibiting factors include privacy concern, algorithmic opacity, and perceived risk. Survey data from 436 OpenClaw users were analysed using structural equation modelling. The results show that positive perceptions strengthen users' attitudes toward OpenClaw, which increase behavioural intention, whereas negative perceptions increase distrust and reduce intention to use the system. The study provides insights into the psychological mechanisms influencing the adoption of autonomous AI agents.

</details>


### [304] [Multi-Agent Collaboration for Automated Design Exploration on High Performance Computing Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11515)
*Harshitha Menon,Charles F. Jekel,Kevin Korner,Brian Gunnarson,Nathan K. Brown,Michael Stees,M. Giselle Fernandez-Godino,Walter Nissen,Meir H. Shachar,Dane M. Sterbentz,William J. Schill,Yue Hao,Robert Rieben,William Quadros,Steve Owen,Scott Mitchell,Ismael D. Boureima,Jonathan L. Belof*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MADA is an LLM-powered multi-agent framework that coordinates specialized agents (JMA, GA, IDA) to automate complex design workflows, demonstrated on RMI suppression in Inertial Confinement Fusion, enabling automated iterative design refinement and large-scale design exploration with minimal manual intervention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific design tasks require exploring vast design spaces and rapid hypothesis testing across domains (climate modeling, ICF design, material design). There is a need to integrate reasoning, simulation tools, and workflows to accelerate discovery.

Method: MADA uses three specialized agents: Job Management Agent (JMA) to launch/manage HPC ensemble simulations, Geometry Agent (GA) to generate meshes, and Inverse Design Agent (IDA) to propose new designs based on simulation outcomes. An LLM coordinates these agents within iterative design loops, leveraging HPC simulations and/or ML surrogates for rapid exploration.

Result: The framework successfully executes iterative design refinement toward improved RMI suppression with minimal manual intervention, reduces manual workflow setup, and enables automated, scalable design exploration. It presents a reusable pattern for integrating reasoning, simulation/tools, and coordinated workflows to accelerate discovery.

Conclusion: MADA demonstrates a scalable, reusable architecture for coupling reasoning with specialized tools and workflows to accelerate scientific discovery, with demonstrated efficacy on an ICF-related design challenge and potential applicability to other domains.

Abstract: Today's scientific challenges, from climate modeling to Inertial Confinement Fusion design to novel material design, require exploring huge design spaces. In order to enable high-impact scientific discovery, we need to scale up our ability to test hypotheses, generate results, and learn from them rapidly. We present MADA (Multi-Agent Design Assistant), a Large Language Model (LLM) powered multi-agent framework that coordinates specialized agents for complex design workflows. A Job Management Agent (JMA) launches and manages ensemble simulations on HPC systems, a Geometry Agent (GA) generates meshes, and an Inverse Design Agent (IDA) proposes new designs informed by simulation outcomes. While general purpose, we focus development and validation on Richtmyer--Meshkov Instability (RMI) suppression, a critical challenge in Inertial Confinement Fusion. We evaluate on two complementary settings: running a hydrodynamics simulations on HPC systems, and using a pre-trained machine learning surrogate for rapid design exploration. Our results demonstrate that the MADA system successfully executes iterative design refinement, automatically improving designs toward optimal RMI suppression with minimal manual intervention. Our framework reduces cumbersome manual workflow setup, and enables automated design exploration at scale. More broadly, it demonstrates a reusable pattern for coupling reasoning, simulation, specialized tools, and coordinated workflows to accelerate scientific discovery.

</details>


### [305] [Expert Threshold Routing for Autoregressive Language Modeling with Dynamic Computation Allocation and Load Balancing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11535)
*Hanchi Sun,Yixin Liu,Yonghui Wu,Lichao Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Expert Threshold routing for MoE eliminates fixed token routing and auxiliary load-balancing losses by using per-expert EMA thresholds to route tokens independently, enabling dynamic computation and causal modeling. At scale (2.4B parameters) it reduces cross-entropy by 0.067 vs TC-MoE, akin to 1.6x fewer tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TC-MoE routes each token to a fixed number of experts, which constrains dynamic computation and relies on auxiliary losses to maintain load balance; a fully causal, dynamic routing mechanism is desirable for autoregressive language models.

Method: Each expert maintains an EMA threshold estimated from the global token score distribution. Tokens are routed to an expert if their score exceeds the expert's threshold, independently of other tokens. This enables dynamic allocation without cross-token dependency and eliminates the need for auxiliary load-balancing losses. Thresholds are updated over training and used at inference.

Result: Pretraining experiments scale up to 2.4B parameters on FineWeb-Edu show ET achieves 0.067 lower cross-entropy loss than TC-MoE, equivalently requiring 1.6× fewer tokens to reach the same performance.

Conclusion: ET provides a fully causal, dynamic routing mechanism for MoE with improved sample efficiency and without auxiliary load-balancing losses, matching or exceeding TC-MoE performance at scale.

Abstract: Token-choice Mixture-of-Experts (TC-MoE) routes each token to a fixed number of experts, limiting dynamic computation allocation and requiring auxiliary losses to maintain load balance. We propose Expert Threshold (ET) routing, where each expert maintains an exponential moving average (EMA) threshold estimated from the global token distribution. At both training and inference, each token is independently routed to an expert if its score exceeds the expert's threshold, enabling dynamic computation allocation while achieving load balance without auxiliary losses. This fully causal mechanism eliminates dependence on other tokens in the batch, making it well-suited for autoregressive language modeling. In pretraining experiments scaling to 2.4B parameters on FineWeb-Edu, ET achieves 0.067 lower cross-entropy loss than TC-MoE, equivalent to reaching the same performance with 1.6$\times$ fewer tokens.

</details>


### [306] [AI Knows What's Wrong But Cannot Fix It: Helicoid Dynamics in Frontier LLMs Under High-Stakes Decisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11559)
*Alejandro R Jadad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The abstract reports a 'helicoid' failure regime in seven leading LLMs during high-stakes, data-limited scenarios, where models loop from competence to error, recognize the error, then escalate the pattern, undermining reliability when it matters most; twelve hypotheses and calls for oversight are proposed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize failure modes of large language models when independent verification is unavailable and decisions are high-stakes, to understand why reliability degrades and how to curb it.

Method: Prospective case series testing seven LLM families (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Grok, DeepSeek, Perplexity, Llama) across clinical diagnosis, investment evaluation, and high-consequence interview tasks; observed a repeatable helicoid dynamic despite explicit protocols.

Result: All systems exhibited helicoid dynamics; persistence attributed to structural training factors; failure pattern intensified at higher levels of sophistication; high-stakes rigor conflicts with comfort, reducing reliability when it matters most; twelve testable hypotheses proposed.

Conclusion: Identifying and naming helicoid is the first step; understanding boundary conditions is needed to build trustworthy AI partners capable of maintaining reliability under hardest decisions, with implications for agentic AI oversight and human-AI collaboration.

Abstract: Large language models perform reliably when their outputs can be checked: solving equations, writing code, retrieving facts. They perform differently when checking is impossible, as when a clinician chooses an irreversible treatment on incomplete data, or an investor commits capital under fundamental uncertainty.
  Helicoid dynamics is the name given to a specific failure regime in that second domain: a system engages competently, drifts into error, accurately names what went wrong, then reproduces the same pattern at a higher level of sophistication, recognizing it is looping and continuing nonetheless. This prospective case series documents that regime across seven leading systems (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Grok, DeepSeek, Perplexity, Llama families), tested across clinical diagnosis, investment evaluation, and high-consequence interview scenarios. Despite explicit protocols designed to sustain rigorous partnership, all exhibited the pattern. When confronted with it, they attributed its persistence to structural factors in their training, beyond what conversation can reach.
  Under high stakes, when being rigorous and being comfortable diverge, these systems tend toward comfort, becoming less reliable precisely when reliability matters most. Twelve testable hypotheses are proposed, with implications for agentic AI oversight and human-AI collaboration.
  The helicoid is tractable. Identifying it, naming it, and understanding its boundary conditions are the necessary first steps toward LLMs that remain trustworthy partners precisely when the decisions are hardest and the stakes are highest.

</details>


### [307] [Leveraging Large Language Models and Survival Analysis for Early Prediction of Chemotherapy Outcomes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11594)
*Muhammad Faisal Shahid,Asad Afzal,Abdullah Faiz,Muhammad Siddiqui,Arbaz Khan Shehzad,Fatima Aftab,Muhammad Usamah Shahid,Muddassar Farooq*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-based extraction of phenotypes and outcome labels from EMR notes reduces sparsity and improves survival-prediction performance for breast cancer chemotherapy, with a Random Survival Forest achieving C-index ~0.73 and classifier metrics >0.70, extended to four other cancers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of explicit phenotypes/outcome labels in real-world EMR data for chemotherapy outcomes, enabling early prediction to guide personalized treatment.

Method: Use large language models and ontology-based techniques to extract phenotypes and outcome labels from patient notes; align regimens with NCCN/NIH standards; perform survival analysis with Random Survival Forest for time-to-failure and time-to-outcome predictions; validate with calibration curves; extend approach to four additional cancer types.

Result: C-index of 73% for time-to-failure; classifier accuracy and F1 >70%; calibrated probability estimates; improved phenotypes sparsity and predictive accuracy.

Conclusion: LLM-driven clinical data extraction can enable early, personalized prediction of chemotherapy outcomes and may generalize across cancer types; demonstrates feasibility of combining ontology-based labeling with survival modeling in real-world data.

Abstract: Chemotherapy for cancer treatment is costly and accompanied by severe side effects, highlighting the critical need for early prediction of treatment outcomes to improve patient management and informed decision-making. Predictive models for chemotherapy outcomes using real-world data face challenges, including the absence of explicit phenotypes and treatment outcome labels such as cancer progression and toxicity. This study addresses these challenges by employing Large Language Models (LLMs) and ontology-based techniques for phenotypes and outcome label extraction from patient notes. We focused on one of the most frequently occurring cancers, breast cancer, due to its high prevalence and significant variability in patient response to treatment, making it a critical area for improving predictive modeling. The dataset included features such as vitals, demographics, staging, biomarkers, and performance scales. Drug regimens and their combinations were extracted from the chemotherapy plans in the EMR data and shortlisted based on NCCN guidelines, verified with NIH standards, and analyzed through survival modeling. The proposed approach significantly reduced phenotypes sparsity and improved predictive accuracy. Random Survival Forest was used to predict time-to-failure, achieving a C-index of 73%, and utilized as a classifier at a specific time point to predict treatment outcomes, with accuracy and F1 scores above 70%. The outcome probabilities were validated for reliability by calibration curves. We extended our approach to four other cancer types. This research highlights the potential of early prediction of treatment outcomes using LLM-based clinical data extraction enabling personalized treatment plans with better patient outcomes.

</details>


### [308] [See, Symbolize, Act: Grounding VLMs with Spatial Representations for Better Gameplay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11601)
*Ashish Baghel,Paras Chopra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Symbolic grounding improves VLM-based agents in interactive tasks when symbols are accurately extracted; symbol extraction quality is the bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs are strong at describing visuals but struggle to translate perception into precise actions; assess whether augmenting frames with symbolic scene representations enhances grounding and action in interactive environments.

Method: Evaluate three state-of-the-art VLMs on Atari games, VizDoom, and AI2-THOR using four pipelines: frame-only; frame with self-extracted symbols; frame with ground-truth symbols; symbol-only. Compare performance, and analyze symbol extraction accuracy and sensitivity to noise.

Result: All models benefit from accurate symbolic information; when symbols are extracted by the model, performance depends on model capability and scene complexity; extraction quality and symbol noise can limit decision-making and gameplay performance.

Conclusion: Symbolic grounding is beneficial for VLM-based agents only when symbol extraction is reliable; perception quality is a central bottleneck for future VLM-based agents.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at describing visual scenes, yet struggle to translate perception into precise, grounded actions. We investigate whether providing VLMs with both the visual frame and the symbolic representation of the scene can improve their performance in interactive environments. We evaluate three state-of-the-art VLMs across Atari games, VizDoom, and AI2-THOR, comparing frame-only, frame with self-extracted symbols, frame with ground-truth symbols, and symbol-only pipelines. Our results indicate that all models benefit when the symbolic information is accurate. However, when VLMs extract symbols themselves, performance becomes dependent on model capability and scene complexity. We further investigate how accurately VLMs can extract symbolic information from visual inputs and how noise in these symbols affects decision-making and gameplay performance. Our findings reveal that symbolic grounding is beneficial in VLMs only when symbol extraction is reliable, and highlight perception quality as a central bottleneck for future VLM-based agents.

</details>


### [309] [The Density of Cross-Persistence Diagrams and Its Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11623)
*Alexander Mironenko,Evgeny. Burnaev,Serguei Barannikov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes the first systematic study of the density of cross-persistence diagrams, proving its existence and building a machine learning framework to predict this density directly from point cloud data, backed by theoretical statistical foundations and empirical experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-persistence diagrams extend standard persistence diagrams to capture interactions between two point clouds. A probabilistic density enables statistical analysis, hypothesis testing, and learning directly from data, addressing limitations of analyzing single datasets in isolation.

Method: The authors establish the existence of a density for cross-persistence diagrams and develop the first statistical framework for their use. They design a machine learning pipeline that predicts cross-persistence density from point cloud coordinates and distance matrices, leveraging linear characteristics of cross-persistence diagrams. They also study the impact of noise on discrimination tasks and validate the approach on diverse datasets.

Result: They prove the density exists and provide a theoretical foundation for statistical usage. The ML framework can predict cross-persistence density from coordinates/distance matrices and improves discrimination of point clouds compared to existing methods. Noise can enhance distinction capabilities, a counterintuitive but novel finding. The approach achieves superior density-prediction and point-cloud distinction performance across experiments; code is publicly available.

Conclusion: This work advances understanding of cross-persistence diagrams, enabling statistical inference and learning in multi-cloud settings. It opens avenues for applications in time-series analysis and the geometry of AI-generated texts, and provides a publicly available implementation for empirical use.

Abstract: Topological Data Analysis (TDA) provides powerful tools to explore the shape and structure of data through topological features such as clusters, loops, and voids. Persistence diagrams are a cornerstone of TDA, capturing the evolution of these features across scales. While effective for analyzing individual manifolds, persistence diagrams do not account for interactions between pairs of them. Cross-persistence diagrams (cross-barcodes), introduced recently, address this limitation by characterizing relationships between topological features of two point clouds. In this work, we present the first systematic study of the density of cross-persistence diagrams. We prove its existence, establish theoretical foundations for its statistical use, and design the first machine learning framework for predicting cross-persistence density directly from point cloud coordinates and distance matrices. Our statistical approach enables the distinction of point clouds sampled from different manifolds by leveraging the linear characteristics of cross-persistence diagrams. Interestingly, we find that introducing noise can enhance our ability to distinguish point clouds, uncovering its novel utility in TDA applications. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our methods through experiments on diverse datasets, where our approach consistently outperforms existing techniques in density prediction and achieves superior results in point cloud distinction tasks. Our findings contribute to a broader understanding of cross-persistence diagrams and open new avenues for their application in data analysis, including potential insights into time-series domain tasks and the geometry of AI-generated texts. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Verdangeta/TDA_experiments

</details>


### [310] [VisDoT : Enhancing Visual Reasoning through Human-Like Interpretation Grounding and Decomposition of Thought](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11631)
*Eunsoo Lee,Jeongwoo Lee,Minki Hong,Jangho Choi,Jihie Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VisDoT introduces perception-grounded, decomposition-based prompting to enhance chart understanding in LVLMs, achieving state-of-the-art performance on chart QA benchmarks and strong zero-shot gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVLMs lack reliable perceptual grounding for chart primitives (positions, lengths) which hampers complex visual reasoning; a human-like grounding framework is needed to improve chart-based VQA and interpretability.

Method: Formalizes four perceptual tasks from graphical perception theory (including position and length) and introduces Decomposition-of-Thought (DoT) prompting that splits questions into visual perception sub-questions and logic sub-questions; fine-tunes InternVL with VisDoT.

Result: Quantitative gains include +11.2% on ChartQA, surpassing GPT-4o on ChartQAPro, +33.2% on VisDoTQA; zero-shot improvements on diverse open-domain VQA benchmarks.

Conclusion: VisDoT provides a human-like perception-based grounding that enables better visual reasoning in charts, achieving state-of-the-art chart understanding and demonstrating the generalizability of perception-logic separation for VQA.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) struggle to reliably detect visual primitives in charts and align them with semantic representations, which severely limits their performance on complex visual reasoning. This lack of perceptual grounding constitutes a major bottleneck for chart-based reasoning. We propose VisDoT, a framework that enhances visual reasoning through human-like interpretation grounding. We formalize four perceptual tasks based on the theory of graphical perception, including position and length. Building on this foundation, we introduce Decomposition-of-Thought (DoT) prompting, which sequentially separates questions into visual perception sub-questions and logic sub-questions. Fine-tuning InternVL with VisDoT achieves a +11.2% improvement on ChartQA and surpasses GPT-4o on the more challenging ChartQAPro benchmark. On the newly introduced VisDoTQA benchmark, the model improves by +33.2%. Furthermore, consistent zero-shot gains on diverse open-domain VQA benchmarks confirm the generalizability of the perception-logic separation strategy for visual question answering. VisDoT leverages human-like perception to enhance visual grounding, achieving state-of-the-art chart understanding and interpretable visual reasoning.

</details>


### [311] [LLMs can construct powerful representations and streamline sample-efficient supervised learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11679)
*Ilker Demirel,Larry Shi,Zeshan Hussain,David Sontag*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-driven rubric-based pipeline to convert text-serialized multimodal inputs into structured representations, improving clinical task performance and enabling auditability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome input representation bottlenecks in supervised learning for heterogeneous real-world data by automating evidence extraction with task-agnostic and task-conditioned rubrics.

Method: In-context LLM analyzes a small, diverse subset of text-serialized inputs to synthesize a global rubric (programmatic specification). This rubric transforms raw text into standardized formats for downstream models. Local rubrics provide task-conditioned summaries.

Result: Rubric-based approaches outperform traditional count-feature models, naive text-serialization LLM baselines, and a large clinical foundation model across 15 tasks from the EHRSHOT benchmark.

Conclusion: Rubrics confer auditability and cost-effective scalability, and their tabular representations enable a broader set of machine learning techniques in healthcare settings.

Abstract: As real-world datasets become increasingly complex and heterogeneous, supervised learning is often bottlenecked by input representation design. Modeling multimodal data for downstream tasks, such as time-series, free text, and structured records, often requires non-trivial domain-specific engineering. We propose an agentic pipeline to streamline this process. First, an LLM analyzes a small but diverse subset of text-serialized input examples in-context to synthesize a global rubric, which acts as a programmatic specification for extracting and organizing evidence. This rubric is then used to transform naive text-serializations of inputs into a more standardized format for downstream models. We also describe local rubrics, which are task-conditioned summaries generated by an LLM. Across 15 clinical tasks from the EHRSHOT benchmark, our rubric-based approaches significantly outperform traditional count-feature models, naive text-serialization-based LLM baselines, and a clinical foundation model, which is pretrained on orders of magnitude more data. Beyond performance, rubrics offer several advantages for operational healthcare settings such as being easy to audit, cost-effectiveness to deploy at scale, and they can be converted to tabular representations that unlock a swath of machine learning techniques.

</details>


### [312] [Explicit Logic Channel for Validation and Enhancement of MLLMs on Zero-Shot Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11689)
*Mei Chee Leong,Ying Gu,Hui Li Tan,Liyuan Li,Nancy Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes an Explicit Logic Channel (ELC) to complement frontier Multimodal LLMs with explicit, probabilistic logical reasoning over visual evidence, enabling model validation, selection, and improvement in zero-shot VLC tasks via cross-channel consistency and integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the black-box, zero-shot nature of frontier MLLMs by introducing a parallel explicit reasoning channel that can validate and explain decisions, thereby enhancing trust and enabling model selection and improvement.

Method: Introduce an Explicit Logic Channel composed of a Language Model, a Vision Foundation Model, and probabilistic logical reasoning operating on explicit visual evidence. Use cross-channel consistency (Consistency Rate, CR) for validation and model selection, and integrate the explicit channel with the implicit MLLM channel to boost zero-shot performance. Evaluate on MC-VQA and HC-REC across three benchmarks with 11 open-source MLLMs from four frontier families.

Result: ELC coupled with CR demonstrates improved validation, selection, and improvement of MLLMs in VLC tasks. Grounding decisions in explicit visual evidence enhances explainability and trustworthiness. Cross-channel integration yields performance gains in zero-shot tasks over MLLMs.

Conclusion: The work provides a viable framework for validating and enhancing frontier VLC models by adding an explicit reasoning channel that can reason over visual evidence, assess channel consistency, and guide model selection and improvement, thereby increasing explainability and trust.

Abstract: Frontier Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) exhibit remarkable capabilities in Visual-Language Comprehension (VLC) tasks. However, they are often deployed as zero-shot solution to new tasks in a black-box manner. Validating and understanding the behavior of these models become important for application to new task. We propose an Explicit Logic Channel, in parallel with the black-box model channel, to perform explicit logical reasoning for model validation, selection and enhancement. The frontier MLLM, encapsulating latent vision-language knowledge, can be considered as an Implicit Logic Channel. The proposed Explicit Logic Channel, mimicking human logical reasoning, incorporates a LLM, a VFM, and logical reasoning with probabilistic inference for factual, counterfactual, and relational reasoning over the explicit visual evidence. A Consistency Rate (CR) is proposed for cross-channel validation and model selection, even without ground-truth annotations. Additionally, cross-channel integration further improves performance in zero-shot tasks over MLLMs, grounded with explicit visual evidence to enhance trustworthiness. Comprehensive experiments conducted for two representative VLC tasks, i.e., MC-VQA and HC-REC, on three challenging benchmarks, with 11 recent open-source MLLMs from 4 frontier families. Our systematic evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of proposed ELC and CR for model validation, selection and improvement on MLLMs with enhanced explainability and trustworthiness.

</details>


### [313] [When OpenClaw Meets Hospital: Toward an Agentic Operating System for Dynamic Clinical Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11721)
*Wenxian Yang,Hanzheng Qiu,Bangqun Zhang,Chengquan Li,Zhiyong Huang,Xiaobin Feng,Rongshan Yu,Jiahong Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes an Agentic Operating System for Hospital (AOSH) that constrains LLM agents with a Linux-like restricted environment, document-centric interactions, a long-term page-indexed memory, and a curated medical skills library, integrated via OpenClaw to improve safety and auditability in clinical workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hospitals stand to benefit from autonomous LLM agents to automate documentation, coordinate care, and support clinical decisions, but deployment is hindered by reliability issues, security risks, and insufficient long-term memory mechanisms.

Method: Architectural design with four core components: a restricted execution environment reminiscent of Linux multi-user systems; a document-centric interaction paradigm linking patient and clinician agents; a page-indexed memory architecture for long-term clinical context management; and a curated medical skills library enabling ad-hoc composition of clinical task sequences. The approach constrains actions via predefined skill interfaces and resource isolation, grounded in the OpenClaw framework and extended with infrastructural safety constraints for hospital deployment.

Result: Provides a conceptual architectural blueprint and design principles for safe, auditable hospital LLM agents; no empirical results are reported.

Conclusion: The architecture offers a foundational path toward safe, transparent orchestration of clinical workflows by autonomous agents and invites further implementation, evaluation, and real-world validation.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents extend conventional generative models by integrating reasoning, tool invocation, and persistent memory. Recent studies suggest that such agents may significantly improve clinical workflows by automating documentation, coordinating care processes, and assisting medical decision making.
